etymology: Old French abaissier, from Latin ad- ('to') + bassus ('low')
exampleSentence: The cruel manager frequently tried to abase his employees during staff meetings.

memoryHook.text: Being brought to your base (low point) can be humiliating.

quizSentence: The captured knight refused to abase himself before the conquering king.
alternateExampleSentence: She refused to abase herself by begging the corrupt judge for mercy.

etymology: Old French abatre, from Latin ad- + battuere ('to beat down')
exampleSentence: We must wait for the heavy storm to abate before driving home.

memoryHook.text: If you don't use bait you reduce your odds in fishing.

quizSentence: We waited indoors for the howling winds to finally abate.
alternateExampleSentence: The doctor prescribed medication to help abate the severe pain in her knee.

etymology: Latin abdicare, from ab- ('away') + dicare ('to proclaim')
exampleSentence: The failing health of the king forced him to abdicate the royal throne.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "dic-" (speak) + "-ate" (verb) = to formally speak away your power.

quizSentence: Rather than face a violent coup, the aging monarch chose to abdicate.
alternateExampleSentence: The disgraced monarch chose to abdicate rather than face a violent revolution.

etymology: Latin aberratio, from aberrare ('to wander away'), from ab- + errare ('to stray')
exampleSentence: The sudden snowstorm in July was an extreme weather aberration for the region.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "err" (wander) = wandering away from what is normal.

quizSentence: Finding a poisonous snake in a suburban garage is a terrifying aberration.
alternateExampleSentence: His violent outburst was a complete aberration from his normally calm personality.

etymology: Old French abeter, from a- + beter ('to bait'), of Germanic origin
exampleSentence: He was arrested because he chose to abet his brother in the robbery.

memoryHook.text: A bet placed on someone encourages them to succeed.

quizSentence: She was charged with conspiracy for attempting to abet a known fugitive.
alternateExampleSentence: The corrupt official plotted to abet the smugglers in crossing the border.

etymology: Latin abhorrere, from ab- ('away from') + horrere ('to shudder')
exampleSentence: Most peaceful citizens abhor the senseless violence of the ongoing war.

memoryHook.text: A horror - you hate it and shrink away from it.

quizSentence: Pacifists inherently abhor any form of physical violence.
alternateExampleSentence: The senator abhors any policy that sacrifices environmental protections for short-term profit.

etymology: Old English abidan, from a- (intensive prefix) + bidan ('to wait, remain')

senses.exampleSentence: Though he did not agree with the decision, Chuck decided to abide by it.
Despite the beating they've taken from the weather throughout the millennia, the mountains abide.

memoryHook.text: You bide your time, enduring or putting up with the situation.

exampleSentence: Every student must abide by the strict rules outlined in the university handbook.
quizSentence: Everyone in the building must abide by the new fire regulations.
alternateExampleSentence: The honorable knight swore to abide by the strict royal code.

etymology: Latin abjectus, past participle of abicere ('to throw away'), from ab- + jacere ('to throw')
exampleSentence: The refugees lived in abject poverty after fleeing their war-torn home country.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "ject" (throw) = thrown away into a wretched state.

quizSentence: The abandoned puppy was found shivering in abject misery.
alternateExampleSentence: His failed business venture left him in a state of abject financial ruin.

etymology: Latin abjurare, from ab- ('away') + jurare ('to swear')
exampleSentence: The captured spy was forced to abjure his previous loyalty to the government.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "jur" (swear) = to swear away or legally renounce.

quizSentence: To gain citizenship, you must abjure allegiance to your former nation.
alternateExampleSentence: She decided to abjure her wealthy lifestyle and join a remote religious commune.

etymology: Latin abnegatio, from abnegare ('to refuse'), from ab- + negare ('to deny')
exampleSentence: The monk's life of abnegation required him to surrender all his worldly possessions.

memoryHook.text: "ab-" (away) + "neg" (deny) = the act of denying yourself something.

quizSentence: His strict diet required a complete abnegation of sugar and dairy.
alternateExampleSentence: Her complete abnegation of personal desires helped her achieve total spiritual enlightenment.

etymology: Old French abregier, from Latin abbreviare ('to shorten'), from ad- + brevis ('short')

senses.exampleSentence: The publisher thought the dictionary was too long and abridged it.
Even the abridged Moby-Dick remains longer than most novels on the shelf.

memoryHook.text: A bridge connects two points, making the journey shorter.

exampleSentence: The eager editor must abridge the lengthy manuscript to meet publication deadlines.
quizSentence: The publisher decided to abridge the lengthy novel for younger readers.
alternateExampleSentence: The professor asked the student to abridge her lengthy speech for the conference.

etymology: Latin abrogare, from ab- + rogare ('to propose a law, ask')
exampleSentence: The angry workers voted to abrogate the unfair contract forced upon them earlier.

memoryHook.text: A broken gate means the security rules have been abolished or repealed.

quizSentence: The new administration sought to abrogate the controversial trade agreement.
alternateExampleSentence: A president cannot simply abrogate the fundamental rights guaranteed by the national constitution.

etymology: Latin abscondere, from abs- ('away') + condere ('to hide, store')
exampleSentence: The clever thief managed to abscond with the stolen diamonds during the blackout.

memoryHook.text: Abscond with the scones - running away secretly with the stolen goods.

quizSentence: The dishonest treasurer planned to abscond with the charity's funds.
alternateExampleSentence: He attempted to abscond to a foreign country before the police could arrive.

etymology: Latin absolutio, from absolvere ('to set free'), from ab- + solvere ('to loosen')
exampleSentence: The criminal sought absolution from the priest before his scheduled execution date.

memoryHook.text: The absolute solution to guilt is being forgiven completely.

quizSentence: He knelt in the confessional seeking absolution for his recent sins.
alternateExampleSentence: The formal pardon provided the disgraced politician with legal absolution for his crimes.

etymology: Latin abstinere, from abs- ('from') + tenere ('to hold')
exampleSentence: The doctor warned the patient to abstain from eating sugary foods before surgery.

memoryHook.text: You abstain from wine so it doesn't stain your shirt.

quizSentence: Three jurors chose to abstain rather than vote on the contested evidence.
alternateExampleSentence: Three committee members decided to abstain from voting on the highly controversial measure.

etymology: Latin abstrusus, past participle of abstrudere ('to conceal'), from abs- + trudere ('to push')
exampleSentence: The professor's abstruse lecture on quantum mechanics confused nearly everyone in the classroom.

memoryHook.text: Sounds like obstructs - the complex meaning obstructs your understanding.

quizSentence: The physicist's lecture on quantum mechanics was far too abstruse for beginners.
alternateExampleSentence: The philosopher wrote an abstruse book that only a few scholars could comprehend.

etymology: Latin accedere, from ad- ('to') + cedere ('to go, yield')
exampleSentence: The tired parents finally decided to accede to their screaming child's unreasonable demands.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "cede" (yield) = to yield toward an agreement.

quizSentence: Management finally decided to accede to the union's demands for better pay.
alternateExampleSentence: The government will never accede to the violent threats of a terrorist organization.

etymology: Medieval Latin accentuare, from Latin accentus ('accent'), from ad- + cantus ('song')
exampleSentence: The tailor altered the dress to perfectly accentuate her tall and slender figure.
quizSentence: She wore a silver belt to accentuate her waist.
alternateExampleSentence: The harsh lighting served only to accentuate the deep wrinkles on his face.

etymology: Latin accessibilis, from accedere ('to approach'), from ad- + cedere ('to go')
exampleSentence: The new subway station makes the downtown museum easily accessible to suburban commuters.
quizSentence: The new ramp makes the library accessible to wheelchair users.
alternateExampleSentence: The author rewrote the complex manual to make it more accessible to beginners.

etymology: Latin acclamare, from ad- + clamare ('to shout')
exampleSentence: The brilliant independent film received widespread acclaim at the international cinema festival.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "claim" (shout) = shouting praise toward someone.

quizSentence: The young author received widespread acclaim for her debut novel.
alternateExampleSentence: The young artist achieved incredible global acclaim after her first major gallery exhibition.

etymology: French accolade, from accoler ('to embrace'), from Latin ad- + collum ('neck')
exampleSentence: Winning the prestigious literary prize was the highest accolade of her writing career.

memoryHook.text: From Latin "ad" (to) + "collum" (neck). Originally a ceremonial embrace around the neck when conferring knighthood.

quizSentence: Winning the Nobel Prize is the highest accolade a scientist can achieve.
alternateExampleSentence: The brave firefighter received a special accolade for saving three children from danger.

etymology: Latin accommodare, from ad- + commodus ('fitting'), from com- + modus ('measure')
exampleSentence: Finding an accommodating landlord made moving to the busy city much less stressful.
quizSentence: The accommodating faculty adviser rescheduled the meeting around the student's exam conflicts.
alternateExampleSentence: The teacher was very accommodating to students who needed extra time for testing.

etymology: Old French acorder, from Latin accordare, from ad- + cor ('heart')
exampleSentence: The two rival nations signed a historic peace accord to end the war.

memoryHook.text: "ac-" (toward) + "cord" (heart) = hearts moving toward agreement.

quizSentence: The warring nations finally signed a historic peace accord.
alternateExampleSentence: The committee finally reached a mutual accord regarding the new corporate budget proposal.

etymology: French accoster, from Italian accostare, from Latin ad- + costa ('rib, side')
exampleSentence: The angry protesters tried to accost the mayor as he left the building.

memoryHook.text: From Latin "ad costam" (to the rib/side). To aggressively come right up to someone's side and confront them.

quizSentence: The investigative journalist was accosted by the subject's security team outside the building.
alternateExampleSentence: A beggar attempted to accost the wealthy tourists walking through the public square.

etymology: Latin accretio, from accrescere ('to grow'), from ad- + crescere ('to grow')
exampleSentence: Over many centuries, the slow accretion of coral created a sprawling barrier reef.

memoryHook.text: A creation built up gradually layer by layer.

quizSentence: The island was formed by the slow accretion of coral sand.
alternateExampleSentence: The steady accretion of wealth allowed him to retire comfortably at age fifty.

etymology: Latin acerbus ('bitter, harsh'), related to acer ('sharp')
exampleSentence: The harsh critic wrote an acerbic review of the terribly directed action movie.

memoryHook.text: An acerbic comment is acidic and bites.

quizSentence: His acerbic sense of humor often offended sensitive listeners.
alternateExampleSentence: Her acerbic sense of humor often offended people who did not know her.

etymology: Latin acquiescere, from ad- + quiescere ('to be quiet'), from quies ('quiet')
exampleSentence: The exhausted parents had to acquiesce to their toddler's demand for more toys.

memoryHook.text: Acquire quiet - you stay quiet and just agree to it.

quizSentence: The reluctant board ultimately acquiesced to the shareholders' demand for a full audit.
alternateExampleSentence: He decided to simply acquiesce to the unfair ruling rather than fight it.

etymology: Latin acrimonia, from acer ('sharp, bitter') + -monia (noun suffix)
exampleSentence: The bitter divorce was marked by intense acrimony between the two former spouses.

memoryHook.text: A crime on me - leaves you with bitterness and ill will.

quizSentence: The bitter divorce was filled with endless acrimony and shouting.
alternateExampleSentence: A deep political acrimony divided the citizens of the small town for decades.

etymology: Latin acumen ('sharpness'), from acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')
exampleSentence: Her sharp business acumen allowed her to quickly turn the failing company around.

memoryHook.text: IQ men - men with high IQs have great mental sharpness.

quizSentence: Her sharp business acumen helped turn the failing startup around.
alternateExampleSentence: Her sharp financial acumen helped the struggling startup secure crucial venture capital funding.

etymology: Latin acutus, past participle of acuere ('to sharpen'), from acus ('needle')

senses.exampleSentence: Arnold could not walk because the pain in his foot was so acute.
Because she was so acute, Libby instantly figured out how the magician pulled off his trick.

memoryHook.text: A cute angle is sharp and precise.

exampleSentence: The patient arrived at the emergency room suffering from an acute asthma attack.
quizSentence: The patient complained of an acute pain in his lower back.
alternateExampleSentence: The small island nation is facing an acute shortage of clean drinking water.

etymology: Latin adamans, from Greek adamas ('unconquerable'), from a- ('not') + daman ('to tame')
exampleSentence: The manager was absolutely adamant that the project must be finished by tomorrow.

memoryHook.text: From Greek adamas, meaning "uncuttable" (the same root as diamond). Unyielding and unbreakable.

quizSentence: She remained adamant that she was telling the truth.
alternateExampleSentence: The stubborn senator remained adamant in his refusal to sign the controversial treaty.

etymology: Latin adeptus, past participle of adipisci ('to attain'), from ad- + apisci ('to reach')
exampleSentence: The veteran mechanic is adept at repairing classic cars from the 1960s.

memoryHook.text: A depth of knowledge makes you highly skilled.

quizSentence: The ninja was highly adept at moving silently through the shadows.
alternateExampleSentence: She is an adept negotiator who always secures the best deals for clients.

etymology: Latin adhaerere, from ad- + haerere ('to stick')

senses.exampleSentence: We adhered the poster to the wall with tape.
He adhered to the dictates of his religion without question.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "haerere" (stick) = to stick to.

exampleSentence: The construction workers must strictly adhere to all safety regulations on the site.
quizSentence: Glue is required to make the wooden panels adhere properly.
alternateExampleSentence: You must use heavy glue so the tiles will adhere to the wall.

etymology: Old French admonester, from Latin admonere ('to advise'), from ad- + monere ('to warn')
exampleSentence: The strict teacher had to admonish the students for cheating on the exam.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "monere" (warn) = to give a warning to.

quizSentence: The teacher had to admonish the students for cheating on the test.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict principal had to admonish the students for disrupting the school assembly.

etymology: Latin adornare, from ad- + ornare ('to furnish, decorate')
exampleSentence: They used hundreds of colorful lights to beautifully adorn the large holiday tree.
quizSentence: Intricate carvings adorn the cathedral's stone facade, each depicting a different biblical scene.
alternateExampleSentence: Brightly colored ribbons adorn the town square during the annual spring harvest festival.

etymology: French adroit, from à droit ('according to right'), from Latin directus ('straight')
exampleSentence: The politician gave an adroit response that cleverly dodged the reporter's difficult question.

memoryHook.text: From French à droit meaning "to the right." The right hand was historically considered the skilled and dexterous one.

quizSentence: An adroit mechanic fixed the engine problem in under ten minutes.
alternateExampleSentence: The adroit mechanic managed to fix the broken engine using only basic tools.

etymology: Latin adulatio, from adulari ('to fawn on'), possibly from cauda ('tail') — as a dog wagging its tail
exampleSentence: The famous pop star quickly grew tired of the constant adulation from fans.

memoryHook.text: Adult infatuation - excessive flattery and worship.

quizSentence: The pop star basked in the adulation of her screaming fans.
alternateExampleSentence: The heroic firefighter humbly rejected the public adulation he received after the rescue.

etymology: Latin adumbrare ('to sketch'), from ad- + umbra ('shadow')
exampleSentence: The mysterious prologue serves to beautifully adumbrate the tragic ending of the novel.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (toward) + "umbr" (shadow) = to cast a shadow over, or outline vaguely.

quizSentence: The CEO could only adumbrate the company's vague future plans.
alternateExampleSentence: The CEO used the quick meeting to vaguely adumbrate the upcoming corporate merger.

etymology: Latin adversus ('turned against'), from advertere, from ad- + vertere ('to turn')
exampleSentence: The experimental medication caused several severely adverse side effects in the test patients.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (against) + "vers" (turn) = turned against you; hostile.

quizSentence: The marathon was canceled due to adverse weather conditions.
alternateExampleSentence: The hikers canceled their dangerous mountain trip due to adverse weather conditions.

etymology: Latin advocare ('to call to one's aid'), from ad- + vocare ('to call')

senses.definition: To argue in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: Community leaders advocate for stricter safety standards near the highway.

senses.definition: A person who argues in favor of something
senses.exampleSentence: She became a fierce advocate for expanding access to mental health care.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (toward) + "voc" (voice) = to add your voice toward a cause.

exampleSentence: The passionate lawyer continues to loudly advocate for strict criminal justice reform laws.
quizSentence: She works as an advocate for children with learning disabilities.
alternateExampleSentence: Environmentalists advocate for the immediate reduction of toxic carbon emissions in the city.

definition: Existing or happening in the air
etymology: Latin aerius, from Greek aerios, from aer ('air')
exampleSentence: The sweeping aerial photograph revealed the hidden layout of the ancient Mayan ruins.
quizSentence: The drone captured breathtaking aerial footage of the coastline.
alternateExampleSentence: The skilled photographer used a drone to capture stunning aerial views of the forest.

etymology: Greek aisthetikos ('of sense perception'), from aisthanesthai ('to perceive')
exampleSentence: The minimalist aesthetic of the new smartphone heavily appealed to young tech enthusiasts.
quizSentence: The gallery's spare aesthetic emphasized natural light and uninterrupted sight lines throughout.
alternateExampleSentence: The modern furniture was chosen purely for its visual aesthetic rather than comfort.

etymology: Latin affabilis, from affari ('to speak to'), from ad- + fari ('to speak')
exampleSentence: The affable new neighbor immediately invited everyone on the block to a barbecue.

memoryHook.text: A fable - the grandpa telling a fable is friendly and easy to talk to.

quizSentence: The affable dean made a point of greeting students by name in the hallways.
alternateExampleSentence: The affable host greeted every arriving guest with a warm and genuine smile.

etymology: Latin affinitas ('relationship by marriage'), from affinis ('bordering on'), from ad- + finis ('border')
exampleSentence: The young child demonstrated a natural affinity for playing the classical piano early.

memoryHook.text: Infinity - an infinite, natural connection or liking for something.

quizSentence: Cats have a natural affinity for sleeping in small cardboard boxes.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt a strong affinity for the stray dog that wandered into her yard.

definition: Having a great deal of wealth
etymology: Latin affluens, from affluere ('to flow toward'), from ad- + fluere ('to flow')
exampleSentence: The affluent neighborhood featured sprawling estates, manicured lawns, and private tennis courts.

memoryHook.text: "af-" (toward) + "flu" (flow) = wealth flowing toward you.

quizSentence: The affluent neighborhood is known for its sprawling mansions.
alternateExampleSentence: Only the most affluent families could afford to send their children to the academy.

etymology: Old French afronter ('to slap in the face'), from Latin ad- + frons ('forehead, face')
exampleSentence: The sudden budget cuts were perceived as a direct affront to public education.

memoryHook.text: In front - an insult right in front of your face.

quizSentence: He took the joke as a personal affront to his dignity.
alternateExampleSentence: The blatant disregard for safety rules was a serious affront to the managers.

etymology: French agrandir, from a- + grandir ('to enlarge'), from Latin grandire, from grandis ('great')
exampleSentence: The vain emperor sought to aggrandize his legacy by building monumental marble statues.

memoryHook.text: To make something grander.

quizSentence: The dictator built towering statues to aggrandize his own legacy.
alternateExampleSentence: The ambitious manager tried to aggrandize her own role within the struggling company.

etymology: Latin aggregatus, from aggregare ('to add to a flock'), from ad- + grex ('flock')

senses.exampleSentence: The three branches form an aggregate more powerful than each part alone.
The dictator tried to aggregate as many people into his army as he possibly could.

memoryHook.text: "ag-" (toward) + "greg" (flock) = to bring the flock together into a whole.

exampleSentence: The university released an aggregate score based on the entire semester of testing.
quizSentence: The final score was an aggregate of all three judges' ratings.
alternateExampleSentence: The popular website serves to aggregate daily news from hundreds of different sources.

etymology: Old French agrever ('to make heavier'), from Latin aggravare, from ad- + gravis ('heavy')
exampleSentence: The aggrieved workers demanded fair compensation after sudden layoffs devastated their local community.

memoryHook.text: You feel aggrieved when someone causes you grief.

quizSentence: The aggrieved customers demanded a full refund for the broken items.
alternateExampleSentence: The aggrieved customer angrily demanded a full refund for the broken television set.

etymology: Latin agilis, from agere ('to do, drive')
exampleSentence: The agile gymnast executed a flawless backflip during the Olympic finals.
quizSentence: The agile gymnast executed a flawless backflip off the beam.
alternateExampleSentence: The agile software team quickly adapted their code to meet the new requirements.

etymology: Greek agnostos ('unknown'), from a- ('not') + gnostos ('known'), coined by T.H. Huxley in 1869
exampleSentence: He claimed to be agnostic regarding the existence of any higher spiritual power.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "gnos" (knowledge) = claiming to be without knowledge (usually of God).

quizSentence: The philosopher remained agnostic on the question of consciousness, finding both positions inadequate.
alternateExampleSentence: The new software is platform agnostic and works perfectly on any operating system.

etymology: Latin agricultura, from ager ('field') + cultura ('cultivation'), from colere ('to cultivate')
exampleSentence: The local economy relies heavily on traditional agriculture and seasonal crop yields.
quizSentence: Early human civilization relied heavily on the invention of agriculture.
alternateExampleSentence: The development of modern agriculture allowed early nomadic tribes to build permanent settlements.

etymology: Latin alacritas, from alacer ('brisk, lively')
exampleSentence: The eager intern accepted the difficult project with surprising speed and alacrity.

memoryHook.text: Sounds like electricity - moving with electric speed and readiness.

quizSentence: She accepted the exciting job offer with impressive alacrity.
alternateExampleSentence: She responded to the urgent corporate email with the alacrity of a true professional.

etymology: Latin alias ('otherwise, at another time'), from alius ('other')
exampleSentence: The undercover detective used a clever alias to infiltrate the dangerous criminal gang.
quizSentence: The spy traveled under an alias to avoid government detection.
alternateExampleSentence: The famous author published her first three mystery novels under a secret alias.

etymology: Old English alecgan, from a- (intensive) + lecgan ('to lay')
exampleSentence: The pilot spoke over the intercom to allay the fears of the nervous passengers.

memoryHook.text: To lay a baby down soothes and calms it.

quizSentence: A warm glass of milk helped allay the child's bedtime fears.
alternateExampleSentence: A quick phone call helped to allay her concerns about the upcoming test.

etymology: Old French alegier, from Latin allegare ('to send on a mission'), from ad- + legare ('to depute')
exampleSentence: The prosecutors allege that the corrupt mayor accepted bribes from the construction company.
quizSentence: The prosecutors allege that he was at the crime scene.
alternateExampleSentence: The prosecuting attorneys allege that the defendant deliberately embezzled millions from the charity.

etymology: Latin alleviare, from ad- + levis ('light'), meaning to lighten a burden
exampleSentence: The physical therapist recommended daily stretching to alleviate the chronic pain in his back.

memoryHook.text: "ad-" (to) + "lev" (light) = to make something lighter or less severe.

quizSentence: Taking ibuprofen can help alleviate the swelling in your ankle.
alternateExampleSentence: The government rushed to deliver emergency food supplies to alleviate the severe famine.

etymology: Medieval Latin allocare, from Latin ad- + locare ('to place'), from locus ('place')
exampleSentence: The city council must allocate more funds to repair the crumbling public schools.
quizSentence: The foundation voted to allocate a third of its annual budget to arts education.
alternateExampleSentence: You should carefully allocate your limited study time before the crucial final exam.

etymology: Latin alludere, from ad- ('to') + ludere ('to play')
exampleSentence: The clever politician managed to softly allude to the recent scandal without naming it.

memoryHook.text: "Allude" sounds like "elude" — you elude naming something by alluding to it.

quizSentence: Rather than quoting the poem directly, the critic chose to allude to its central metaphor.
alternateExampleSentence: During his speech, he made sure to allude to his extensive military experience.

etymology: Dutch te loef ('to windward'), nautical term meaning to keep a distance; loof relates to 'luff'
exampleSentence: The famous actor remained entirely aloof from his fans during the movie premiere.

memoryHook.text: A roof - sitting alone on a roof, emotionally detached from everyone below.

quizSentence: The famous actor seemed aloof and ignored his fans at the premiere.
alternateExampleSentence: The aloof celebrity rarely granted interviews or interacted with her adoring fans.

etymology: Latin altercatio, from altercari ('to dispute'), from alter ('other')
exampleSentence: The noisy altercation in the parking lot quickly caught the attention of the police.

memoryHook.text: When things alter (change) for the worse, a noisy argument starts.

quizSentence: A minor disagreement escalated into a physical altercation at the bar.
alternateExampleSentence: He was suspended from school after starting a violent altercation in the cafeteria.

etymology: Medieval Latin amalgamare, from amalgama ('alloy of mercury'), possibly from Greek malagma ('softening')
exampleSentence: The two struggling tech companies decided to amalgamate into a single powerful corporation.

memoryHook.text: A mall - a mall brings many different stores together into one place.

quizSentence: The two tech startups decided to amalgamate into one large corporation.
alternateExampleSentence: The chef tried to amalgamate three different cultural cuisines into one cohesive menu.

etymology: Latin ambiguus, from ambigere ('to wander around'), from ambi- ('both ways') + agere ('to drive')
exampleSentence: The mysterious author left the novel with a frustratingly ambiguous and open ending.

memoryHook.text: "ambi-" (both) + "agere" (drive) = driven in two directions, unclear.

quizSentence: The detective was frustrated by the suspect's ambiguous answers.
alternateExampleSentence: The politician gave an intentionally ambiguous answer to avoid upsetting any registered voters.

etymology: Latin ambi- ('both') + valentia ('strength'), coined in early 20th-century psychology
exampleSentence: She felt entirely ambivalent about accepting the new job in a different city.

memoryHook.text: "ambi-" (both) + "val" (strength/feeling) = having strong feelings in both directions.

quizSentence: The senator remained ambivalent about the bill, citing unresolved concerns about its fiscal impact.
alternateExampleSentence: The electorate remained ambivalent about the charismatic candidate despite his sweeping campaign promises.

etymology: French améliorer, from Old French meilleur ('better'), from Latin meliorare, from melior ('better')
exampleSentence: The charity aims to ameliorate the harsh living conditions in the impoverished neighborhood.

memoryHook.text: A million rate - taking a bad rating and improving it to a million.

quizSentence: Volunteers are working to ameliorate the harsh conditions in the shelter.
alternateExampleSentence: A sincere apology did little to ameliorate the severe damage to their friendship.

etymology: Anglo-French amenable, from Old French amener ('to lead to'), from a- + mener ('to lead'), from Latin minare ('to drive')
exampleSentence: The exhausted manager was finally amenable to the idea of hiring an assistant.

memoryHook.text: Amen-able - someone who frequently says "amen" (yes) and is easily persuaded.

quizSentence: The department head proved amenable to the proposed schedule change after reviewing the data.
alternateExampleSentence: A skilled mediator must be amenable to adjusting strategy when negotiations stall unexpectedly.

etymology: Latin amenitas ('pleasantness'), from amoenus ('pleasant')
exampleSentence: The luxury hotel features every modern amenity you could possibly want for a vacation.
quizSentence: A heated swimming pool is a lovely amenity at this resort.
alternateExampleSentence: A heated indoor swimming pool is the best amenity offered by the fitness center.

etymology: Old French amiable, from Latin amicabilis ('friendly'), from amicus ('friend')
exampleSentence: His amiable personality made him an ideal candidate for resolving difficult workplace disputes.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love/friend) = friendly and pleasant (used for people).

quizSentence: The amiable diplomat eased tensions simply by listening before presenting any formal position.
alternateExampleSentence: An amiable disposition helps new employees build trust with colleagues during onboarding.

etymology: Late Latin amicabilis, from Latin amicus ('friend'), from amare ('to love')
exampleSentence: The two former business partners managed to reach a surprisingly amicable legal settlement.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love/friend) = friendly and peaceful (used for agreements/situations).

quizSentence: Despite the breakup, they managed to reach an amicable agreement.
alternateExampleSentence: Despite the difficult circumstances, their divorce was resolved in a highly amicable manner.

etymology: Latin amorosus, from amor ('love'), from amare ('to love')
exampleSentence: An amorous couple shared a quiet, candlelit dinner in the elegant Parisian restaurant.

memoryHook.text: "am-" (love) + "-ous" (full of) = full of romantic love.

quizSentence: He wrote an amorous poem to his girlfriend for Valentine's Day.
alternateExampleSentence: The famous poet wrote a deeply amorous letter to his distant secret lover.

etymology: Greek amorphos, from a- ('without') + morphe ('form, shape')
exampleSentence: The ghost appeared as a terrifying, amorphous blob of white light in the hallway.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "morph" (shape) = without definite shape.

quizSentence: The blob in the sci-fi movie was an amorphous mass of slime.
alternateExampleSentence: The shadowy, amorphous creature slithered silently through the dark corridors of the dungeon.

etymology: Greek anachronismos, from ana- ('back') + chronos ('time')
exampleSentence: A rotary phone on the executive's desk felt anachronistic in the digital office.

memoryHook.text: "ana-" (backward/against) + "chron" (time) = placed in the wrong time period.

quizSentence: Using a typewriter in a modern office seems wonderfully anachronistic.
alternateExampleSentence: The director included an intentionally anachronistic pop song in the historical drama film.

etymology: Greek analgesia ('absence of pain'), from an- ('without') + algos ('pain')
exampleSentence: The doctor prescribed a powerful analgesic to help manage the severe postoperative pain.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "alg" (pain) = medicine that takes away pain.

quizSentence: Aspirin is a common analgesic used to treat minor headaches.
alternateExampleSentence: She applied a soothing analgesic cream to quickly heal the painful muscle cramp.

etymology: Greek analogos ('proportionate'), from ana- ('according to') + logos ('ratio, proportion')
exampleSentence: The brain of a computer is analogous to the human central nervous system.

alternateExampleSentence: His sudden political rise is analogous to the success of his legendary predecessor.

etymology: Greek anarchos ('without a ruler'), from an- ('without') + archos ('ruler')
exampleSentence: The radical anarchist plotted to overthrow the government by destroying critical city infrastructure.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "arch" (ruler) = one who believes in no government.

quizSentence: The radical anarchist protested against all forms of government control.
alternateExampleSentence: An angry anarchist threw a heavy brick through the window of the global bank.

etymology: Greek anathema ('thing devoted to evil'), from anatithenai ('to set up'), from ana- + tithenai ('to place')
exampleSentence: The concept of absolute censorship is total anathema to any working investigative journalist.

memoryHook.text: A nasty asthma - something you vehemently despise and avoid.

quizSentence: Eating meat is anathema to a strict vegan.
alternateExampleSentence: Eating meat is complete anathema to someone who strictly follows a vegetarian diet.

etymology: Greek anekdotos ('unpublished'), from an- ('not') + ekdotos ('published'), from ekdidonai ('to publish')
exampleSentence: The professor started his lecture with a humorous anecdote about his early college days.
quizSentence: The professor opened the lecture with a pointed anecdote about wartime medical triage.
alternateExampleSentence: She shared a brief, touching anecdote to illustrate the value of hard work.

etymology: Old French angoisse, from Latin angustia ('tightness, distress'), from angustus ('narrow')
exampleSentence: Years of chronic pain caused the weary patient agonizing mental and physical anguish.
quizSentence: She cried out in anguish when she heard the tragic news.
alternateExampleSentence: The injured athlete cried out in physical anguish when he twisted his knee.

etymology: Latin animatus, past participle of animare ('to give life to'), from anima ('breath, soul')
exampleSentence: The two friends engaged in a highly animated debate over the controversial movie ending.

memoryHook.text: "anima" (soul/life) = full of life.

quizSentence: The kids became animated when discussing their favorite video game.
alternateExampleSentence: The spirited debate grew increasingly animated as both candidates passionately defended their policies.

etymology: Latin annexare, from ad- + nectere ('to bind')

senses.exampleSentence: After defeating them in battle, the Russians annexed Poland.

senses.definition: A room attached to a larger room or space
senses.exampleSentence: He studied in a quiet annex attached to the main reading room.

exampleSentence: The aggressive empire launched a brutal military campaign to illegally annex the neighboring territory.
quizSentence: The city plans to annex the neighboring farmland for a new subdivision.
alternateExampleSentence: The aggressive empire launched a brutal military campaign to annex the neighboring territory.

etymology: Old French annuller, from Latin annullare, from ad- + nullus ('none')
exampleSentence: The frustrated couple hired a lawyer to quickly annul their hasty weekend marriage.

memoryHook.text: A null - making a contract legally null and void.

quizSentence: The couple quickly hired a lawyer to annul their drunken marriage.
alternateExampleSentence: A high court judge possesses the ultimate authority to legally annul the unfair contract.

etymology: Greek anomalia ('unevenness'), from anomalos ('uneven'), from an- ('not') + homalos ('even')
exampleSentence: The sudden spike in temperature was a complete weather anomaly for the winter season.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (not) + "homos" (same) = not the same as the normal rule.

quizSentence: A sudden spike in the heart rate monitor was a concerning anomaly.
alternateExampleSentence: Finding water in that barren desert region is a highly unusual geological anomaly.

etymology: Greek anonymos, from an- ('without') + onyma ('name')
exampleSentence: An anonymous donor generously provided the struggling orphanage with sufficient funds for winter.

memoryHook.text: "an-" (without) + "onym" (name) = without a name.

quizSentence: The whistleblower submitted the leaked documents through an anonymous source.
alternateExampleSentence: The controversial author published the fiery political manifesto under an anonymous pen name.

etymology: Greek antagonisma, from antagonizesthai ('to contend against'), from anti- + agonizesthai ('to contend')
exampleSentence: There was a bitter, historical antagonism between the two rival families in the village.
quizSentence: There is a long history of antagonism between the rival soccer teams.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager's constant micro-management bred severe antagonism among the frustrated junior office staff.

etymology: Latin antecedere ('to go before'), from ante- ('before') + cedere ('to go')
exampleSentence: The invention of the telegraph was a direct antecedent to the modern telephone system.

memoryHook.text: "ante-" (before) + "ced" (go) = the thing that goes before.

quizSentence: The telegraph is a historical antecedent to the modern telephone.
alternateExampleSentence: You must identify the grammatical antecedent of the pronoun in this complex sentence.

etymology: Latin ante ('before') + diluvium ('flood'), referring to the Biblical flood — before Noah's flood
exampleSentence: The aging professor desperately clung to antediluvian theories that modern science had debunked.

memoryHook.text: "ante-" (before) + "diluvium" (flood) = literally "before the biblical flood," meaning ancient.

quizSentence: His antediluvian views on gender roles belong in the nineteenth century.
alternateExampleSentence: His antediluvian views on modern technology frustrated his younger, more innovative colleagues.

etymology: Greek anthologia ('flower gathering'), from anthos ('flower') + legein ('to gather')
exampleSentence: The thick literary anthology featured acclaimed short stories from diverse twentieth-century global authors.

memoryHook.text: Greek anthos (flower) + logia (collection): a bouquet metaphor for chosen writings.

quizSentence: The professor assigned an anthology of classic British poetry.
alternateExampleSentence: Her brilliant short story was officially selected for inclusion in the prestigious yearly anthology.

etymology: Greek antipatheia, from anti- ('against') + pathos ('feeling')
exampleSentence: The striking workers felt a deep antipathy toward the greedy corporate board of directors.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (against) + "path" (feeling) = a strong feeling against someone.

quizSentence: Cats and dogs are often portrayed as having a natural antipathy.
alternateExampleSentence: A deep-seated antipathy existed between the two rival families for over three generations.

etymology: Latin antiquatus, past participle of antiquare ('to make old'), from antiquus ('ancient')
exampleSentence: The office still uses highly antiquated software that crashes multiple times a day.
quizSentence: The hospital's antiquated record system forced nurses to spend hours on manual documentation.
alternateExampleSentence: The failing factory relied on antiquated machinery that constantly broke down during production.

etymology: Greek anti- ('against') + septikos ('putrefying'), from sepein ('to make rotten')
exampleSentence: The prudent surgeon rigorously scrubbed her hands with a potent antiseptic before operating.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (against) + "septic" (infection) = against infection.

quizSentence: Wipe the cut with an antiseptic pad to prevent dangerous infections.
alternateExampleSentence: The hospital hallway smelled strongly of bleach and chemical antiseptic during the late shift.

etymology: Greek antithesis ('opposition'), from anti- ('against') + tithenai ('to place')
exampleSentence: The cruel dictator was the exact antithesis of the benevolent monarch he replaced.

memoryHook.text: "anti-" (opposite) + "thesis" (idea) = the exact opposite.

quizSentence: Slavery is the exact antithesis of freedom and human rights.
alternateExampleSentence: The brutal dictator is the antithesis of a modern, democratic political leader.

etymology: Latin anxietas, from anxius ('troubled'), from angere ('to choke, distress')
exampleSentence: He felt overwhelming anxiety before delivering the keynote speech to the large audience.
quizSentence: Persistent anxiety before public performances led the musician to seek professional counseling.
alternateExampleSentence: She practiced deep breathing techniques to successfully manage her severe social anxiety in crowds.

etymology: Greek apathes ('without feeling'), from a- ('without') + pathos ('feeling')
exampleSentence: The apathetic voters chose to stay home instead of participating in the election.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (without) + "path" (feeling) = without emotion or interest.

quizSentence: The apathetic voters didn't bother showing up for the local election.
alternateExampleSentence: The apathetic voters mostly ignored the crucial election despite widespread civic engagement campaigns.

etymology: Greek apokryphos ('hidden away'), from apo- ('away') + kryptein ('to hide')
exampleSentence: The popular legend of the hidden pirate treasure proved to be largely apocryphal.

memoryHook.text: A pocket full of lies - a story of doubtful authenticity.

quizSentence: The legend of the ghostly hitchhiker is likely an apocryphal tale.
alternateExampleSentence: Most of the amazing tales about the legendary king are entirely apocryphal in nature.

etymology: Old French apallir ('to grow pale'), from a- + pallir ('to be pale'), from Latin pallere
exampleSentence: The human rights inspectors discovered appalling living conditions inside the neglected prison facility.
quizSentence: The inspector shut down the restaurant due to appalling health violations.
alternateExampleSentence: The humanitarian inspectors discovered appalling living conditions within the overcrowded and neglected prison.

etymology: Old French apaisier, from a- + pais ('peace'), from Latin pax
exampleSentence: The weak manager tried to appease the angry customer by offering free merchandise.

memoryHook.text: To bring someone to a state of peace.

quizSentence: Offering a symbolic concession failed to appease protesters who demanded structural reform.
alternateExampleSentence: The government tried to appease the striking workers by offering a minor wage increase.

etymology: Old French aprisier, from a- + prisier ('to prize'), from Latin pretiare ('to value')
exampleSentence: The bank hired a licensed expert to officially appraise the value of the property.

memoryHook.text: You offer praise when you determine something has high value.

quizSentence: An expert was called in to appraise the rare diamond ring.
alternateExampleSentence: The meticulous jeweler must carefully appraise the diamond necklace before making an offer.

etymology: Latin apprehendere ('to seize'), from ad- + prehendere ('to grasp')

senses.exampleSentence: The criminal was apprehended at the scene.
The student has trouble apprehending concepts in math and science.

memoryHook.text: Up your hand - what cops say when they arrest you.

exampleSentence: The diligent detectives managed to apprehend the elusive thief before he fled the country.
quizSentence: Police managed to apprehend the bank robber within twenty minutes.
alternateExampleSentence: It took months for law enforcement to finally apprehend the cunning cyber criminal.

etymology: Latin approbatio, from approbare ('to approve'), from ad- + probare ('to prove, test')
exampleSentence: The brilliant scientist received universal approbation from her peers after publishing the breakthrough study.

etymology: Latin appropriare ('to make one's own'), from ad- + proprius ('one's own')
exampleSentence: The city council voted to appropriate emergency funds to repair the severely damaged bridge.

memoryHook.text: A property - to take something for your own use.

quizSentence: The state decided to appropriate the land for a new highway.
alternateExampleSentence: The corrupt mayor tried to appropriate public funds for his lavish private vacation.

etymology: Latin arabilis, from arare ('to plow')
exampleSentence: The farmer purchased the large plot of arable land to expand his crops.

memoryHook.text: "ar-" (plow) + "-able" = plowable and farmable.

quizSentence: The fertile valley provides plenty of arable land for growing crops.
alternateExampleSentence: Decades of severe drought slowly turned the once arable fields into barren desert.

etymology: Latin arbiter ('witness, judge'), possibly from ar- ('to') + baetere ('to go')
exampleSentence: The supreme court acts as the final arbiter in complex constitutional legal disputes.

memoryHook.text: An arbiter will bite down on the arguments and make the final decision.

etymology: Latin arbitrarius, from arbiter ('judge') — decisions made at a judge's discretion, not by rule
exampleSentence: The tyrannical manager enforced arbitrary rules that frustrated his dedicated and hardworking employees.

etymology: Latin arbitratio, from arbitrari ('to give judgment'), from arbiter ('judge')
exampleSentence: The union agreed to enter binding arbitration to resolve the bitter labor dispute.
quizSentence: The contract dispute was settled quickly through binding arbitration.
alternateExampleSentence: The bitter contract dispute was finally settled through binding arbitration rather than litigation.

etymology: Latin arboreus, from arbor ('tree')
exampleSentence: The rare species of monkey spends its entire life in an arboreal environment.

memoryHook.text: From Latin arbor (tree). Relating to trees.

quizSentence: Monkeys are arboreal creatures that spend their lives in the canopy.
alternateExampleSentence: The dense jungle canopy was teeming with a wide variety of arboreal creatures.

etymology: Latin arcanus ('secret, hidden'), from arca ('chest, box')
exampleSentence: The ancient manuscript contained arcane knowledge understood by only three living scholars.

memoryHook.text: Ark cane - Indiana Jones needed a cane to find the lost Ark because its location was highly mysterious and known only to a few.

quizSentence: The wizard studied an arcane tome filled with forgotten spells.
alternateExampleSentence: He spent years studying the arcane rituals of the forgotten religious cult.

etymology: Greek archaikos ('old-fashioned'), from archaios ('ancient'), from arche ('beginning')
exampleSentence: She struggled to read the archaic script inscribed on the crumbling stone monument.

etymology: Greek archetupos ('original pattern'), from arche ('beginning') + tupos ('type, model')

etymology: Latin ardor ('heat, flame'), from ardere ('to burn')

etymology: Latin aridus, from arere ('to be dry')

etymology: Latin arrogare ('to claim for oneself'), from ad- + rogare ('to ask')
exampleSentence: The ambitious general attempted to violently arrogate total power over the fragile government.

etymology: French artisan, from Italian artigiano, from Latin artitus ('skilled in the arts'), from ars ('art')
exampleSentence: The local bakery produces incredible artisan bread using a traditional sourdough starter.

etymology: Old French acertainer, from a- + certain ('certain'), from Latin certus ('sure')
exampleSentence: The detective carefully examined the crime scene to ascertain the exact time of death.

memoryHook.text: As certain - to make yourself absolutely certain of the facts.

etymology: Greek asketes ('one who exercises'), from askein ('to exercise, train')
exampleSentence: The silent monk lived an ascetic lifestyle in the isolated mountain temple.

alternateExampleSentence: The devout monk lived a sparse, ascetic lifestyle devoid of any material luxuries.

etymology: Latin ascribere, from ad- + scribere ('to write') — to write something to someone's account

etymology: Latin aspersio ('a sprinkling'), from aspergere ('to sprinkle'), from ad- + spargere — as in sprinkling someone with mud
exampleSentence: The bitter rival tried to cast a damaging aspersion on her flawless professional record.

etymology: Latin aspirare ('to breathe upon, aim at'), from ad- + spirare ('to breathe')
exampleSentence: The dedicated student continues to strongly aspire to a career in medical research.

etymology: Old French assaillir, from Latin assalire, from ad- + salire ('to leap')
exampleSentence: The angry critics used the media to loudly assail the controversial political candidate.

etymology: Latin asserere, from ad- ('to') + serere ('to join')
exampleSentence: The brilliant lawyer continued to loudly assert the innocence of his client.

memoryHook.text: "Assert" = to join your voice to a claim and stand by it.

etymology: Old French assesser, from Latin assidere ('to sit beside'), from ad- + sedere ('to sit') — as a judge sitting beside
exampleSentence: The insurance adjuster arrived quickly to properly assess the severe damage to the house.

alternateExampleSentence: The strict teacher will carefully assess your complete understanding of the mathematical formula.

etymology: Latin assiduus ('constant, persistent'), from assidere ('to sit beside'), from ad- + sedere ('to sit')
exampleSentence: The assiduous student spent three hours every single night studying advanced calculus.

etymology: Old French assouagier, from Latin ad- + suavis ('sweet, agreeable')
exampleSentence: The quick apology did absolutely nothing to assuage the anger of the customer.

alternateExampleSentence: He took a powerful painkiller to finally assuage the terrible ache in his shoulder.

etymology: Latin astutus, from astus ('craft, cunning')
exampleSentence: The astute detective noticed a tiny inconsistency in the suspect's carefully rehearsed alibi.

memoryHook.text: A student - a good student is sharp and clever.

quizSentence: An astute investor knows exactly when to sell a falling stock.
alternateExampleSentence: Her astute observations helped the committee quickly solve the complex budget crisis.

etymology: Latin asylum, from Greek asylon ('place of refuge'), from a- ('without') + syle ('right of seizure')

senses.definition: A place of refuge, protection, a sanctuary
senses.exampleSentence: For Thoreau, the forest served as an asylum from the pressures of urban life.

senses.definition: An institution in which the insane are kept
senses.exampleSentence: Once diagnosed by a certified psychiatrist, the man was put in an asylum.

exampleSentence: The political refugee desperately sought immediate asylum in a neighboring democratic country.
quizSentence: The political dissident was granted asylum in a neighboring democratic country.
alternateExampleSentence: The abandoned asylum on the hill was heavily rumored to be entirely haunted.

etymology: Middle English at on ('in agreement'), from at + one — literally to become 'at one' with someone you wronged
exampleSentence: He worked tirelessly at the local charity to properly atone for his past mistakes.

memoryHook.text: At one - making up for your sins so you are back "at one" with God.

quizSentence: He tried to atone for his mistakes by doing extensive charity work.
alternateExampleSentence: The criminal hoped to fully atone for his terrible actions through extensive community service.

etymology: Greek atrophia ('lack of nourishment'), from a- ('without') + trophe ('nourishment')
exampleSentence: A prolonged lack of intellectual stimulation can cause a sharp mind to atrophy.

memoryHook.text: A trophy - if you just stare at a trophy and stop training, your muscles will waste away.

quizSentence: Without regular exercise, your muscles will slowly begin to atrophy.
alternateExampleSentence: The prolonged economic recession caused the local manufacturing industry to dangerously atrophy.

etymology: Old French ataindre, from Latin attingere ('to touch'), from ad- + tangere ('to touch')
exampleSentence: The dedicated runner trained for months to successfully attain a qualifying marathon time.
quizSentence: Consistent daily practice is the most reliable path to attain professional fluency.
alternateExampleSentence: The dedicated athlete trained relentlessly to attain an elusive Olympic gold medal.

etymology: Latin attestari, from ad- ('to') + testari ('to bear witness')
exampleSentence: Multiple reliable witnesses can firmly attest to the suspect's exact location that evening.

memoryHook.text: "At-" + "test" = to be at the test as a witness.

quizSentence: Multiple independent records attest that the treaty was signed in April.
alternateExampleSentence: The ancient ruins attest to the remarkable engineering skills of that forgotten civilization.

etymology: Latin attribuere ('to assign'), from ad- + tribuere ('to grant, give')

senses.exampleSentence: He attributes all of his success to his mother's undying encouragement.
Among the beetle's most peculiar attributes is its thorny protruding eyes.

exampleSentence: The arrogant manager tried to attribute the team's shared success to his leadership.
quizSentence: She attributes her musical success to her supportive parents.
alternateExampleSentence: Many historians attribute the sudden collapse of the empire to rampant political corruption.

etymology: Greek a- ('not') + typos ('type, model'), via Latin typicalis
exampleSentence: The sudden heavy snowfall is a highly atypical weather event for this tropical region.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (not) + "typical" = not typical.

quizSentence: An atypical warmth in January prompted meteorologists to revisit their long-range forecasts.
alternateExampleSentence: The severe summer blizzard was an atypical weather event for the coastal region.

etymology: Latin audax ('bold'), from audere ('to dare')
exampleSentence: The audacious thieves managed to steal a priceless painting from the crowded museum.

memoryHook.text: All day shush - if you shush the teacher all day, you are boldly disrespectful.

quizSentence: The audacious thief stole the painting in broad daylight.
alternateExampleSentence: Her audacious plan to launch a rocket built from recycled materials surprised experts.

etymology: Latin audibilis, from audire ('to hear')
exampleSentence: The sharp crack of the falling tree was clearly audible across the entire valley.
quizSentence: The dog whistle produces a sound that is not audible to humans.
alternateExampleSentence: A sharp, audible crack echoed through the silent forest as the branch snapped.

etymology: Latin augmentare, from augere ('to increase') — root of words like August and auction
exampleSentence: The young teacher took a weekend tutoring job to augment her meager salary.

memoryHook.text: Argument - you augment (add to) your essay to win the argument.

quizSentence: He took a weekend job to augment his meager salary.
alternateExampleSentence: The general ordered fresh troops to quickly augment the heavily weakened defensive line.

etymology: Latin auspicium ('omen from bird observation'), from avis ('bird') + specere ('to look') — Romans read omens from birds
exampleSentence: The sudden surge in early sales marked an auspicious beginning for the new business.

memoryHook.text: Awesome space - entering an awesome space is a sign of future success.

quizSentence: Finding a four-leaf clover is considered an auspicious start to the day.
alternateExampleSentence: Finding a four-leaf clover is considered an auspicious sign by many superstitious folks.

etymology: Latin austerus, from Greek austeros ('harsh, severe'), from auein ('to dry')
exampleSentence: The austere monastery lacked modern comforts, focusing strictly on quiet spiritual reflection.

memoryHook.text: Out steer - steering out of the fun lane into a strict, severe, undecorated lifestyle.

quizSentence: The prison cell was stark and austere, containing only a metal cot.
alternateExampleSentence: His austere facial expression intimidated the young recruits during their first training session.

etymology: Latin avaritia, from avarus ('greedy'), from avere ('to crave')
exampleSentence: Motivated by pure avarice, the corrupt executive embezzled millions from his own company.

memoryHook.text: Have a rice - the greedy rich man wants to have every single grain of rice for himself.

quizSentence: Corporate avarice often leads to environmental destruction for profit.
alternateExampleSentence: Driven purely by immense avarice, the king taxed the poorest citizens of the realm.

etymology: Old French avengier, from a- + vengier, from Latin vindicare ('to claim, avenge')
exampleSentence: The prince swore to avenge the wrong done to his royal family.
quizSentence: The hero swore to avenge the unjust defeat of his family.
alternateExampleSentence: The powerful superhero fought to fiercely avenge the terrible destruction of his home city.

etymology: Latin aversio, from avertere ('to turn away'), from ab- + vertere ('to turn')
exampleSentence: Her strong aversion to public speaking made presentations a source of severe stress.

memoryHook.text: "a-" (away) + "vers" (turn) = turning away in disgust.

quizSentence: A strong aversion to confrontation made the manager reluctant to address the conflict directly.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict vegan maintained a deep moral aversion to consuming any animal products.

etymology: Old English balca ('ridge, beam'); figuratively a beam or obstacle that stops progress
exampleSentence: The stubborn horse decided to balk at the steep jump during the equestrian competition.

memoryHook.text: A dog barks when it balks (refuses to move forward).

quizSentence: The horse decided to balk at the jump and threw its rider.
alternateExampleSentence: Consumers often balk at paying premium prices for generic and unbranded grocery items.

etymology: French banal ('compulsory, common'), from ban ('proclamation, obligatory service') — what all villagers shared was banal
exampleSentence: The film disappointed audiences with its banal plot and poorly written dialogue.

memoryHook.text: Ban all - we should ban all boring, unoriginal movies.

quizSentence: Critics dismissed the screenplay as banal, noting its reliance on predictable plot reversals.
alternateExampleSentence: He quickly grew tired of the banal conversations at the formal corporate dinner.

etymology: Old English bana ('killer, murderer, slayer'); evolved to mean that which causes ruin or misery
exampleSentence: The complex mathematical formula remained the bane of the struggling high school student.

memoryHook.text: Batman's enemy Bane is the cause of his great distress.

quizSentence: Inefficient bureaucratic procedures remain the bane of hospital administrators across the country.
alternateExampleSentence: The persistent and destructive beetles became the bane of the local wheat farmers.

etymology: Middle English baischen ('to be abashed'), from Old French esbahir, from es- + bahir ('to astonish'); -ful suffix added in 16th century
exampleSentence: The bashful child immediately hid entirely behind his mother when the strangers arrived.

memoryHook.text: If you are shy, you might nervously bash your toe into the ground.

quizSentence: The bashful toddler hid his face behind his mother's skirt.
alternateExampleSentence: He gave a slightly bashful smile when the teacher praised his brilliant science project.

etymology: Middle English, from be- + guile ('deception'), from Old Norse *vegla, related to Old English wil ('trick')
exampleSentence: The cunning salesman used smooth flattery to beguile customers into buying useless warranties.

memoryHook.text: "be-" (completely) + "guile" (trickery) = to charm someone in a deceptive way.

quizSentence: The con artist used his charm to beguile tourists out of their money.
alternateExampleSentence: The beautiful music was carefully designed to entirely beguile the wealthy audience members.

etymology: Hebrew behemoth — a massive creature described in the Book of Job, possibly a hippopotamus or mythical beast
exampleSentence: The corporate behemoth dominated the retail industry by acquiring all small competitors.
quizSentence: The new corporate headquarters is a towering glass behemoth.
alternateExampleSentence: Navigating the icy mountain roads in that mechanical behemoth proved extremely difficult.

etymology: Old English belēogan, from be- + lēogan ('to lie')
exampleSentence: His calm and collected demeanor served to belie the panic he truly felt.

memoryHook.text: "Belie" hides the truth — it sounds like "lie" inside the word.

quizSentence: The company's polished report belied serious losses in its overseas division.
alternateExampleSentence: The cheerful music playing overhead belied the tense atmosphere inside the waiting room.

etymology: Latin benevolens, from bene ('well') + volens ('wishing'), present participle of velle ('to wish')
exampleSentence: The benevolent monarch distributed food and warm clothing to the impoverished peasants.

memoryHook.text: "bene-" (good) + "vol" (wish) = wishing good upon others.

quizSentence: A benevolent donor secretly paid for the sick child's surgery.
alternateExampleSentence: The benevolent teacher spent three extra hours tutoring the struggling student after school.

etymology: Latin benignus ('kind, generous'), from bene ('well') + gignere ('to beget')
exampleSentence: Despite his frightening appearance, the giant dog actually possessed a gentle, benign temperament.

memoryHook.text: "bene-" (good) + "gen" (born) = gentle and harmless.

quizSentence: The doctor smiled and announced that the tumor was benign.
alternateExampleSentence: The old golden retriever has a benign personality and loves every single stranger.

etymology: Old English becwethan, from be- + cwethan ('to say, declare') — formally declaring what one leaves behind
exampleSentence: The wealthy patriarch planned to bequeath his sprawling estate to his eldest daughter.

memoryHook.text: Beneath - leaving your possessions to the generation beneath you.

quizSentence: The author chose to bequeath her manuscript collection to the university's rare books library.
alternateExampleSentence: Many artists hope to bequeath a lasting cultural legacy through their creative works.

etymology: Middle English, from be- (intensive) + rate ('to scold'), of uncertain origin
exampleSentence: The aggressive coach would frequently berate the young players for making minor strategic mistakes.

memoryHook.text: To give someone a B rate (a bad grade) and scold them angrily for it.

quizSentence: The angry coach began to berate the players for their sloppy defense.
alternateExampleSentence: The angry coach began to loudly berate his players after their embarrassing defeat.

etymology: Past participle of bereave, from Old English bereafian ('to deprive'), from be- + reafian ('to rob')
exampleSentence: The sudden tragedy left the grieving family feeling bereft of hope and joy.

memoryHook.text: He left - feeling utterly deprived and sad because he left.

quizSentence: The grieving widow felt bereft of comfort after the funeral.
alternateExampleSentence: When her beloved dog passed away, she felt entirely bereft of any remaining joy.

etymology: Middle English bisechen, from be- + sechen ('to seek') — to urgently seek something from someone
exampleSentence: The desperate prisoners knelt to beseech the merciful king for a royal pardon.

memoryHook.text: Bee speech - begging the bees not to sting you.

quizSentence: The stranded climbers beseeched the rescue team to prioritize the most severely injured first.
alternateExampleSentence: The captured prisoners began to loudly beseech the cruel king for mercy.

etymology: Old French biais ('slope, slant'), probably from Provençal; originally a bowling term for a ball rolling crooked
exampleSentence: The judge must recuse himself due to a severe bias against the defendant.
quizSentence: The referee was accused of having a clear bias toward the home team.
alternateExampleSentence: The scientific study was heavily compromised by the blatant bias of the researchers.

etymology: 17th century; possibly from balk or from a card game term meaning to thwart an opponent
exampleSentence: The clever scammer managed to entirely bilk the elderly woman out of her savings.

memoryHook.text: Milk - the scammer will milk you for all your money.

quizSentence: The scammer managed to bilk elderly residents out of their savings.
alternateExampleSentence: The sophisticated con artist managed to bilk elderly investors out of their life savings.

etymology: Old French blandiss-, from Latin blandiri ('to flatter, coax'), from blandus ('mild, flattering')
exampleSentence: The eager assistant tried to blandish his demanding boss with constant, insincere compliments.

memoryHook.text: Bland dish - flattering the chef by saying the bland dish is actually delicious.

quizSentence: He tried to blandish the bouncer into letting them skip the line.
alternateExampleSentence: She tried to carefully blandish the angry manager into approving her vacation request.

etymology: Old French blemiss-, from blemir ('to make pale, injure'), of Germanic origin
exampleSentence: A single terrible mistake remained the only blemish on his perfect professional record.
quizSentence: A single scratch was the only blemish on the classic car's paint.
alternateExampleSentence: The antique dealer noted a tiny blemish on the surface of the rare painting.

etymology: 17th century, of uncertain origin; possibly related to Old English blæce ('skin disease')

senses.exampleSentence: The potato blight destroyed the harvest and bankrupted many families.
His bad morale is a blight upon this entire operation.

memoryHook.text: B-light - blocking out the light causes the crops to die.

exampleSentence: The sudden economic blight caused numerous local businesses to close their doors forever.
quizSentence: Urban blight has left this once-thriving neighborhood in ruins.
alternateExampleSentence: The abandoned factory was a terrible urban blight on the otherwise beautiful historic neighborhood.

etymology: Middle English boistuous ('crude, rough'), possibly from Old French boisteus ('lame, clumsy')
exampleSentence: The boisterous crowd cheered wildly when the home team scored the winning goal.

memoryHook.text: Boys terrorize - loud, energetic boys terrorizing the playground.

quizSentence: The boisterous crowd cheered wildly when the band took the stage.
alternateExampleSentence: A group of boisterous children ran laughing through the quiet public library.

etymology: Old English bolster ('long pillow'), from a word meaning 'to support'
exampleSentence: The general requested additional troops to quickly bolster the severely weakened defensive line.

memoryHook.text: A bolster pillow supports your head — to bolster an argument supports it.

quizSentence: The pilot study bolstered confidence in the larger experiment's design.
alternateExampleSentence: The attorney introduced surprising new evidence to bolster her client's shaky defense.

etymology: From bombast ('cotton padding'), from French bombace, from Medieval Latin bombax ('cotton') — speech that is stuffed with padding
exampleSentence: The politician's bombastic speech lacked real substance despite his dramatic hand gestures.

memoryHook.text: Bomb - inflating your speech with explosive words that lack actual substance.

quizSentence: His bombastic speech lacked any real substance or practical solutions.
alternateExampleSentence: Her bombastic attitude alienated the entire team during the crucial software development meeting.

etymology: Old Norse bon ('prayer, request'); evolved to mean something granted as a favor
exampleSentence: The sudden heavy rain was an incredible boon to the desperate, drought-stricken farmers.

memoryHook.text: Boom! - an explosive stroke of good luck or benefit.

quizSentence: The sudden rainstorm was a huge boon to the struggling farmers.
alternateExampleSentence: The sudden discovery of rare minerals was a significant boon to the local economy.

etymology: French bourgeois, from Old French burgeis ('town-dweller'), from bourg ('town'), from Latin burgus ('fort')
exampleSentence: The rebellious artist mocked the bourgeois obsession with manicured lawns and luxury cars.
quizSentence: He rejected his family's bourgeois lifestyle to live as a starving artist.
alternateExampleSentence: His bourgeois lifestyle centered around acquiring expensive clothes and dining at exclusive restaurants.

etymology: Old English braesen ('of brass'), from bras ('brass') — brass was associated with hardness and boldness
exampleSentence: The utterly brazen thief stole the expensive car directly in front of the police.

memoryHook.text: Brass - acting as bold and hard as brass without any shame.

quizSentence: The brazen student cheated while sitting right in front of the teacher.
alternateExampleSentence: The brazen thieves stole the diamond necklace in broad daylight without wearing masks.

etymology: French brusque, from Italian brusco ('sour, rough'), possibly from Medieval Latin bruscus ('butcher's broom')
exampleSentence: The busy manager gave a brusque reply to the junior employee's simple question.

memoryHook.text: Brush - brushing someone off with a quick, rude reply.

quizSentence: The surgeon's brusque manner alarmed patients who mistook efficiency for indifference.
alternateExampleSentence: The busy doctor gave a brusque reply when asked about the lengthy wait times.

etymology: Old French buffeter, from buffet ('a blow'), of imitative origin

senses.exampleSentence: The strong winds buffeted the ships, threatening to capsize them.

senses.definition: An arrangement of food set out on a table
senses.exampleSentence: Guests took food from the buffet and ate standing in the hall.

memoryHook.text: A buff guy hitting you repeatedly.

exampleSentence: The powerful coastal winds continued to heavily buffet the small wooden cabin all night.
quizSentence: Violent winds continued to buffet the small plane during the storm.
alternateExampleSentence: Economic hardships constantly buffet the struggling citizens of the isolated, impoverished island nation.

etymology: Old French burniss-, from burnir ('to polish'), variant of brunir, from brun ('brown, shining')
exampleSentence: The public relations team worked tirelessly to quickly burnish the disgraced politician's ruined image.

memoryHook.text: Burn - rubbing the metal so fast it burns and becomes highly polished.

quizSentence: Use this special polish to burnish the silver teapot until it shines.
alternateExampleSentence: The skilled silversmith used a soft cloth to carefully burnish the antique serving tray.

etymology: Old French bouterez, from bouter ('to push, thrust'), from Frankish *botan — the arch that pushes against a wall

senses.exampleSentence: The column buttresses the roof above the statue.
The buttress supports the roof above the statues.

memoryHook.text: Butt rest - a support structure that acts as a rest for the building.

exampleSentence: The defense attorney found a new witness to firmly buttress his client's shaky alibi.
quizSentence: The engineers built a steel wall to buttress the crumbling foundation.
alternateExampleSentence: The builders used heavy steel beams to securely buttress the collapsing ancient stone wall.

etymology: Greek kakophonia, from kakos ('bad') + phone ('sound, voice')
exampleSentence: A deafening cacophony of car horns erupted during the severe downtown traffic jam.

memoryHook.text: "kakos" (bad) + "phon" (sound) = a harsh, bad mixture of sounds.

quizSentence: A loud cacophony of car horns erupted during the traffic jam.
alternateExampleSentence: The amateur orchestra produced a painful cacophony instead of a harmonious musical performance.

etymology: Italian cadenza, from cadere ('to fall'), from Latin cadere — the falling of pitch at the end of a musical phrase
exampleSentence: The elite marathon runner maintained a steady cadence throughout the grueling 20k race.
quizSentence: The rowers synchronized their oars to the steady cadence of the drum.
alternateExampleSentence: The talented speaker delivered his emotional speech with a beautifully rhythmic and poetic cadence.

etymology: French cajoler ('to cajole, chatter like a jay'), from Old French gaioler ('to chatter like a jay in a cage')
exampleSentence: The teenager tried to cleverly cajole his strict parents into extending his evening curfew.

memoryHook.text: Cage hole - coaxing the scared puppy out of the cage hole with a treat.

quizSentence: She managed to cajole her reluctant friend into riding the rollercoaster.
alternateExampleSentence: She managed to quickly cajole the stubborn manager into granting her an extra vacation.

etymology: Latin calamitas ('damage, disaster'), possibly related to calamus ('stalk') — crop damage from storms
exampleSentence: Failing to secure the fragile cargo resulted in a costly maritime calamity.

memoryHook.text: Calm am I? T(ee) - No, I am not calm, it's a disaster!

quizSentence: The devastating earthquake was the worst calamity the nation had ever faced.
alternateExampleSentence: The sudden stock market crash proved to be a financial calamity for investors.

etymology: From caliber, from Italian calibro, from Arabic qalib ('shoemaker's last, mold'); to measure the bore of a gun
exampleSentence: The scientist must carefully calibrate the sensitive equipment before starting the highly complex experiment.
quizSentence: You must calibrate the scale before weighing the chemical samples.
alternateExampleSentence: The skilled technician must precisely calibrate the sensitive equipment before the scientific experiment.

etymology: Latin callosus ('hard-skinned'), from callum ('hardened skin') — emotionally thick-skinned
exampleSentence: The callous dictator ignored the desperate pleas of his starving and impoverished citizens.

memoryHook.text: Having physical calluses on your hands makes them hard; having a callous personality means your heart is hard and unfeeling.

quizSentence: The callous thief laughed at the crying child he had just robbed.
alternateExampleSentence: Stealing money from a charitable donation bin is a truly callous criminal act.

etymology: Latin calumnia ('false accusation, slander'), from calvi ('to trick, deceive')
exampleSentence: The vicious tabloid article was nothing but pure calumny designed to ruin her career.

memoryHook.text: Column - writing a gossip column full of slander to ruin someone's reputation.

quizSentence: The politician sued the newspaper for printing malicious calumny about him.
alternateExampleSentence: He sued the rival politician for intentionally spreading horrible calumny during the election campaign.

etymology: French camaraderie, from camarade ('comrade'), from Spanish camarada ('roommate'), from camara ('chamber'), from Latin camera
exampleSentence: The intense military training fostered a deep sense of camaraderie among the young recruits.
quizSentence: Military training builds a strong sense of camaraderie among the recruits.
alternateExampleSentence: A wonderful feeling of camaraderie quickly developed among the members of the championship team.

etymology: Latin candor ('whiteness, brilliance, frankness'), from candere ('to shine, be white')
exampleSentence: Candor in a performance review, however uncomfortable, gives employees the clarity to improve.

memoryHook.text: Can do - being completely open and honest about what you can do.

quizSentence: The whistleblower's candor, though politically costly, earned broad respect from the oversight committee.
alternateExampleSentence: His strict boss demanded total candor regarding the reasons for the delayed shipment.

etymology: Scottish/Northern English, from can ('to know'), from Old English cunnan ('to know, be able')
exampleSentence: The canny investor bought cheap real estate just before the major highway was built.

memoryHook.text: Can he? - Yes, he can, because he's shrewd and clever.

quizSentence: A canny shopper always looks for the best discount codes before buying.
alternateExampleSentence: She made a highly canny decision to sell her startup before the market crashed.

definition: To solicit votes, opinions, or support; to survey systematically
etymology: From canvas (sailcloth); early pollsters and campaigners recorded responses on canvas sheets
exampleSentence: The senator's team canvassed suburban districts for weeks ahead of the primary election.

senses.definition: To seek opinions or support systematically
senses.exampleSentence: Volunteers canvassed the precinct door to door before the municipal election.

memoryHook.text: Canvass with two s's — campaigners going door to door with clipboards, not blank artist cloth.

quizSentence: Campaign volunteers canvassed the neighborhood to register voters before the municipal election.
alternateExampleSentence: Investigative reporters canvassed expert opinion before publishing the controversial report.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin capax, capac- 'capable' + -ious.
exampleSentence: The capacious auditorium comfortably seated over three thousand eager students for the graduation ceremony.

memoryHook.text: Capacity - having a massive capacity; very roomy.

quizSentence: Her capacious handbag held everything from an umbrella to a laptop.
