alternateExampleSentence: She purchased a capacious leather tote bag to carry her heavy academic textbooks.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from medieval Latin capitulat- 'drawn up under headings', from capitulum 'little head'.
exampleSentence: Refusing to capitulate during the negotiations, the union leaders demanded better working conditions.

memoryHook.text: "caput" (head) = to give up your head to the enemy, to surrender.

quizSentence: The surrounded army had no choice but to capitulate to the enemy.
alternateExampleSentence: The stubborn union refused to capitulate to the greedy demands of the corporate executives.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French capricieux, from Italian capriccioso.
exampleSentence: The capricious weather in the mountains can shift from sunny to snowing within minutes.

memoryHook.text: Capri Sun - a toddler's mood is capricious; one second they are happy, the next they are crying because they dropped their Capri Sun.

quizSentence: The capricious weather shifted from sunny to hailing in minutes.
alternateExampleSentence: His capricious mood swings made it difficult for his friends to plan social outings.

etymology: Early 16th century: from late Latin captivat- 'taken captive'.
exampleSentence: The brilliant magician managed to entirely captivate the audience with his stunning visual illusions.
quizSentence: The magician's incredible illusions managed to captivate the entire audience.
alternateExampleSentence: The beautiful classical symphony will captivate anyone who appreciates deeply emotional orchestral music.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from German gar aus 'right out' (used of emptying a bumper).
exampleSentence: The victorious soldiers decided to loudly carouse in the local tavern until the morning.

memoryHook.text: Car house - driving your car into the house because you were partying and drinking noisily.

quizSentence: The sailors hit the local taverns to carouse until dawn.
alternateExampleSentence: The college students would frequently carouse in the streets after passing their final exams.

etymology: Middle English: from Old Norse karpa 'brag', later meaning 'find fault'.
exampleSentence: The unhappy customer continued to bitterly carp about the minor delay in his food.

memoryHook.text: A carp fish just swims around complaining and nitpicking.

quizSentence: A leader who constantly carps about minor setbacks damages team morale without solving problems.
alternateExampleSentence: The cynical movie critic tends to carp about minor historical inaccuracies in fictional films.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French catalogue, from late Latin catalogus.

senses.definition: To list, enter into a list
senses.exampleSentence: The judge cataloged the victim’s injuries before calculating how much money he would award.
We received a catalog from J. Crew that displayed all of their new items.

exampleSentence: The librarian meticulously worked to catalog the thousands of newly donated historical manuscripts.
quizSentence: The librarian worked tirelessly to catalog the new book donation.
alternateExampleSentence: The museum published a comprehensive catalog detailing its impressive collection of ancient pottery.

etymology: Mid 19th century: back-formation from catalysis.
exampleSentence: The shocking documentary helped catalyze a nationwide movement for vital environmental reform.
quizSentence: The protests helped catalyze a major shift in public policy.
alternateExampleSentence: Adding a specific chemical enzyme will efficiently catalyze the sluggish biological reaction.

etymology: Mid 18th century (originally US): perhaps from Algonquian.
exampleSentence: The political caucus met privately to select a new candidate for the upcoming election.

memoryHook.text: Cactus - politicians gathering around a spiky cactus to hold a closed political meeting.

quizSentence: Party leaders held a private caucus to select their presidential nominee.
alternateExampleSentence: The senators formed a special caucus to deeply address the growing national environmental crisis.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin causticus, from Greek kaustikos.
exampleSentence: The careless worker sustained a severe burn from the spilled caustic cleaning chemicals.

memoryHook.text: Cause sick - drinking a harsh chemical will cause you to be sick.

quizSentence: Bleach is a caustic chemical that requires careful handling.
alternateExampleSentence: Her caustic wit often alienated peers who misunderstood her harsh and biting sarcasm.

etymology: Late 18th century (originally US): perhaps an alteration of curvet.
exampleSentence: The joyous puppies began to cavort happily in the muddy puddles after the rainstorm.

memoryHook.text: Cave fort - jumping and dancing around excitedly in your childhood cave fort.

quizSentence: The joyful puppies began to cavort happily in the fresh snow.
alternateExampleSentence: Excited festival attendees cavort wildly to the energetic music late into the summer night.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin censura 'judgment, assessment'.

senses.exampleSentence: The frustrated teenager could not put up with anymore of her critical mother’s censure.
The principal censured the head of the English Department for forcing students to learn esoteric vocabulary.

memoryHook.text: Censor - the government officially criticizes and censors the journalist.

exampleSentence: The ethics committee voted to formally censure the corrupt politician for his illegal actions.
quizSentence: The ethics committee issued a formal censure against the corrupt senator.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager faced severe corporate censure after entirely ignoring the critical safety guidelines.

etymology: Early 19th century: from Latin cerebrum 'brain' + -al.
exampleSentence: The cerebral movie required the audience to pay close attention to understand it.

memoryHook.text: "cerebrum" (brain) + "-al" = relating to the brain/intellect.

quizSentence: The cerebral indie film explored deep philosophical questions about existence.
alternateExampleSentence: The cerebral film challenged audiences with complex philosophical themes and ambiguous character motivations.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin, from Greek khaos 'vast chasm, void'.
exampleSentence: Absolute chaos erupted in the crowded stadium when the sudden fire alarm unexpectedly sounded.
quizSentence: The sudden fire alarm plunged the crowded theater into total chaos.
alternateExampleSentence: Sudden power outages plunged the busy metropolitan train station into unpredictable chaos.

etymology: Middle English: from obsolete chastie (from Old French chastier) + -ise.
exampleSentence: Parents should gently chastise their children for lying rather than imposing draconian punishments.

memoryHook.text: Chase ties - the boss will chase you down and scold you for not wearing a tie.

quizSentence: The editor publicly chastised the reporter for publishing unverified claims in the breaking story.
alternateExampleSentence: The frustrated manager decided to publicly chastise the lazy employee for missing the deadline.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French cheriss-, lengthened stem of cherir.
exampleSentence: The elderly veteran will always cherish the faded medals from his military service.
quizSentence: The archivist cherished every letter in the collection, handling each document with deliberate care.
alternateExampleSentence: Athletes who cherish the process, not just outcomes, build more enduring careers.

etymology: Old English cīdan, of unknown origin.
exampleSentence: The loving mother had to gently chide her son for leaving his toys outside.

memoryHook.text: Child - how you gently scold a child for making a mistake.

quizSentence: She gently chided her husband for forgetting to buy milk.
alternateExampleSentence: The stern librarian had to gently chide the giggling students for making noise.

etymology: Late 18th century: from Greek khoreia 'dancing in unison' + -graphy.
exampleSentence: The ballet critic praised the innovative choreography featured in the modern dance performance.
quizSentence: The complex choreography required the dancers to practice for months.
alternateExampleSentence: Mastering the intricate sword choreography took the dedicated actors several months of practice.

etymology: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French cronicle, from late Latin chronica.

senses.exampleSentence: The library featured the newly updated chronicle of World War II.
Albert’s diary chronicled the day-to-day growth of his obsession with Cynthia.

exampleSentence: The detailed journal serves to chronicle the daily hardships of early colonial settlers.
quizSentence: This thick volume is a detailed chronicle of the Roman Empire.
alternateExampleSentence: The journalist decided to accurately chronicle the difficult journey of the political refugees.

etymology: Late 16th century: from modern Latin chronologicus + -al.
exampleSentence: The museum arranged the dinosaur fossils in strict chronological order for educational clarity.

memoryHook.text: "chron" (time) + "log" (order) = arranged in order of time.

quizSentence: Please arrange these historical documents in strict chronological order.
alternateExampleSentence: You must present the complex events of the story in a strict chronological sequence.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin circuitosus, from Latin circuitus.
exampleSentence: The lost hikers took a frustratingly circuitous route back to the mountain base.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "ire" (go) = going in a circular, indirect route.

quizSentence: The cab driver took a circuitous route to drive up the meter.
alternateExampleSentence: His circuitous explanation failed to answer the journalist's direct question about the scandal.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumlocutio(n-), from circum 'around' + locutio.
exampleSentence: The deceptive salesman used confusing circumlocution to hide the actual price of the vehicle.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "loc" (speak) = talking around the subject to avoid the point.

quizSentence: His annoying circumlocution made it impossible to get a straight answer.
alternateExampleSentence: The evasive politician used endless circumlocution to avoid answering the debate moderator directly.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumscribere, from circum 'around' + scribere 'write'.
exampleSentence: The strict prison rules heavily circumscribed the daily activities of the  dangerous inmates.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "scribere" (write/draw) = drawing a line around something to restrict it.

quizSentence: The guard dogs were kept within a carefully circumscribed area of the yard.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict warden circumscribed the daily activities allowed within the maximum security prison.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin circumspectus, from circumspicere 'look around'.
exampleSentence: The wise general was circumspect before committing troops to the dangerous battle.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "spect" (look) = looking around carefully before acting; cautious.

quizSentence: A circumspect investor always reads the fine print before signing a contract.
alternateExampleSentence: She remained circumspect about her future career plans during the public interview.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin circumvent- 'baffled, enclosed', from circumvenire.
exampleSentence: The clever hacker found a brilliant way to circumvent the new corporate firewall.

memoryHook.text: "circum" (around) + "vent" (go) = to find a way around an obstacle.

quizSentence: Hackers found a clever way to circumvent the company's new firewall.
alternateExampleSentence: Clever hackers found a novel way to circumvent the corporate firewall security protocols.

etymology: Late 17th century: from French, from clair 'clear' + voyant 'seeing'.
exampleSentence: The psychic claimed to possess clairvoyant abilities to predict highly specific future events.

memoryHook.text: "clair" (clear) + "voyant" (seeing) = having clear vision of the future.

quizSentence: The fortune teller claimed to be clairvoyant and predicted a dark future.
alternateExampleSentence: The desperate widow visited a reputed clairvoyant to communicate with her deceased husband.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French clamour, from Latin clamor.

senses.exampleSentence: Each morning the birds outside my window make such a clamor that they wake me up.
Fans clamored for an encore, though the singer had already left the stage.

memoryHook.text: Claim more - the angry mob is loudly shouting to claim more rights.

exampleSentence: A sudden clamor arose from the angry protesters gathered outside the courthouse steps.
quizSentence: A loud clamor arose from the angry protesters outside the courthouse.
alternateExampleSentence: The hungry animals began to loudly clamor for their food early this morning.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from French clandestin or Latin clandestinus.
exampleSentence: The secret agents held a clandestine meeting inside the abandoned industrial warehouse tonight.

memoryHook.text: Clan destiny - the secret clan held a hidden meeting to discuss their destiny.

quizSentence: The spies held a clandestine meeting in the back of a dark pub.
alternateExampleSentence: The secret agents held a clandestine meeting in a dark underground parking garage.

etymology: Old English clēofan 'split'.

senses.exampleSentence: Following the scandalous disgrace of their leader, the entire political party cleaved into warring factions.
After resolving their marital problems, Junior and Rosa cleaved to one another all the more tightly.

memoryHook.text: Meat cleaver - a heavy knife used to split things apart.

exampleSentence: The skilled butcher used a heavy axe to cleave the frozen meat apart.
quizSentence: The butcher used a heavy meat axe to cleave the bone in half.
alternateExampleSentence: The dedicated marathoner must entirely cleave to his strict weekly training schedule.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin clementia, from clemens, clement- 'merciful'.
exampleSentence: The corrupt governor refused to grant any clemency to the convicted political prisoners.

memoryHook.text: Lemon tea - giving the sore-throated prisoner a warm lemon tea as an act of mercy.

quizSentence: The condemned man begged the governor for clemency at the last minute.
alternateExampleSentence: The defense lawyer begged the stern judge for clemency during the trial.

etymology: Late Middle English: from obsolete cloy 'to clog'.
exampleSentence: The novel's sentimental ending struck critics as cloying rather than genuinely moving.

memoryHook.text: Clawing - romantic words that are so overly sweet you want to claw your ears off.

quizSentence: The romantic comedy was ruined by a cloying and overly sweet ending.
alternateExampleSentence: The cloying sweetness of the cheap perfume gave the elegant dinner guests headaches.

definition: To thicken into a solid mass
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coagulat- 'curdled', from the verb coagulare.
exampleSentence: The heavy cream will slowly begin to coagulate if left in the warm sun.

memoryHook.text: Colgate - toothpaste thickens and solidifies if left out.

quizSentence: Exposure to the air caused the spilled latex paint to rapidly coagulate.
alternateExampleSentence: The patient lacked the necessary platelets to properly coagulate the wound.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin coalescere, from co- 'together' + alescere 'grow up'.
exampleSentence: The scattered political factions finally began to coalesce into a single unified party.

memoryHook.text: Fuse into one: co- (together) + alescere (to grow)-parts grow together into a single mass.

quizSentence: The different neighborhood watch groups decided to coalesce into one organization.
alternateExampleSentence: Her brilliant, disparate ideas quickly started to coalesce into a brilliant business plan.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin coercere 'restrain'.
exampleSentence: The corrupt detective tried to coerce the frightened witness into signing a false statement.

memoryHook.text: Force - using force or threats to get someone to do something.

quizSentence: You cannot coerce a witness into providing a false testimony.
alternateExampleSentence: No amount of extreme peer pressure could coerce him into breaking the law.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin cogent- 'compelling', from the verb cogere.
exampleSentence: The defense attorney presented a cogent argument that easily swayed the skeptical jury.

memoryHook.text: Co-agent - your FBI co-agent presents a clear, logical, and convincing argument.

quizSentence: The attorney presented a very cogent argument that swayed the jury.
alternateExampleSentence: She offered a cogent analysis of the economic factors driving the sudden inflation.

etymology: Early 19th century: from obsolete cognizance 'knowledge' + -ant.
exampleSentence: The hiking group remained fully cognizant of the approaching dangerous severe weather system.

memoryHook.text: "cogn" (know) = having knowledge or awareness.

quizSentence: Any effective policymaker must be cognizant of how regulations affect vulnerable populations.
alternateExampleSentence: A successful manager must remain cognizant of shifting employee morale during stressful projects.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin cohaerent- 'sticking together'.
exampleSentence: The exhausted student struggled to formulate a single coherent sentence after the exam.

memoryHook.text: "co-" (together) + "haerere" (stick) = sticking together logically.

quizSentence: The exhausted speaker was unable to form a coherent sentence.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant professor delivered a remarkably coherent lecture on advanced quantum physics theories.

etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin collateralis, from col- 'together with' + lateralis.

senses.exampleSentence: Divorcing cost him dearly; losing her income was a harsh collateral effect.
Jacob left his watch as collateral for the $500 loan.

exampleSentence: The bank required the business owner to use his house as loan collateral.
quizSentence: She put up her car as collateral to secure the bank loan.
alternateExampleSentence: The desperate businessman offered his luxury yacht as collateral for the bank loan.

etymology: Mid 18th century: from Latin colloquium 'conversation' + -al.
exampleSentence: The author used colloquial language to make the dialogue feel authentic and realistic.

memoryHook.text: "col-" (together) + "loqu" (speak) = how people speak together in everyday conversation.

quizSentence: The essay was marked down for using too much informal colloquial slang.
alternateExampleSentence: You should strictly avoid using colloquial phrases in a formal academic research paper.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin collusio(n-), from the verb colludere.
exampleSentence: The executives were arrested for their illegal collusion to fix global market prices.

memoryHook.text: "col-" (together) + "ludere" (play/deceive) = playing together in a secret, deceitful plot.

quizSentence: Three major airlines were investigated for illegal price-fixing collusion.
alternateExampleSentence: Federal investigators suspected widespread collusion between the rival telecom companies to fix prices.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin, from Greek kolossos.
exampleSentence: The ancient bronze colossus guarded the harbor entrance until an earthquake destroyed it.
quizSentence: The ancient Colossus of Rhodes was a bronze statue honoring the sun god.
alternateExampleSentence: The new stadium stands as a modern colossus of incredible concrete and steel.

etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin combustio(n-), from comburere 'burn up'.
exampleSentence: The mechanical engineer closely monitored the internal combustion of the experimental car engine.
quizSentence: The internal combustion engine revolutionized the transportation industry.
alternateExampleSentence: The volatile chemicals caused a sudden, violent combustion inside the small laboratory.

etymology: Middle English: from Latin commendatio(n-), from the verb commendare.
exampleSentence: The brave firefighter received a formal commendation for saving three trapped apartment residents.

memoryHook.text: Recommend a nation - giving official praise.

quizSentence: The brave police dog received a special commendation for finding the suspect.
alternateExampleSentence: The principal gave a special commendation to the students who organized the charity.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from late Latin commensuratus, from com- 'together' + mensuratus.
exampleSentence: Her starting salary will be entirely commensurate with her extensive professional coding experience.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "mensur" (measure) = corresponding in size or degree.

quizSentence: Your starting salary will be commensurate with your level of experience.
alternateExampleSentence: Your starting salary will be commensurate with your previous experience and educational background.

etymology: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin commodiosus, from Latin commodus 'convenient'.
exampleSentence: The wealthy family purchased a highly commodious apartment in the center of town.

memoryHook.text: Commode (toilet) - a massive, spacious bathroom.

quizSentence: The commodious living room had plenty of space for a large sectional couch.
alternateExampleSentence: The wealthy family purchased a commodious estate with plenty of room for entertaining.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin compellere, from com- 'together' + pellere 'drive'.
exampleSentence: The documentary provided truly compelling evidence regarding the severe impact of climate change.
quizSentence: The documentary offered a compelling look at the harsh realities of climate change.
alternateExampleSentence: He made a compelling argument for investing in the new tech startup.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin compensat- 'weighed against', from the verb compensare.
exampleSentence: The struggling airline promised to financially compensate the passengers for the delayed flight.
quizSentence: The airline agreed to compensate the passengers for the delayed flight.
alternateExampleSentence: The airline must legally compensate passengers for the sudden cancellation of their flight.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin complacentia, from Latin complacere 'to please'.
exampleSentence: The defending champions lost the crucial match due to their uncharacteristic arrogant complacency.

memoryHook.text: Come play, senor - relaxing and playing instead of staying alert to danger.

quizSentence: Early success bred complacency, leaving the team unprepared for the competitor's rapid advances.
alternateExampleSentence: Corporate complacency often allows innovative new startups to capture significant market share quickly.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin complementum, from complere 'fill up'.
exampleSentence: The crisp white wine served to complement the spicy vegetarian pasta dish.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "plere" (fill) = to fill up together, completing the whole.

quizSentence: That dark green tie perfectly complements the color of your eyes.
alternateExampleSentence: Her exceptional data analysis skills complement his brilliant, creative marketing strategy ideas.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from comply + -ant.
exampleSentence: The compliant child quietly finished his homework without offering any resistance or complaints.
quizSentence: The restaurant kitchen is fully compliant with all state health codes.
alternateExampleSentence: Restaurants must remain strictly compliant with local health codes to avoid being closed.

etymology: Mid 20th century: back-formation from complicity.
exampleSentence: The silent bystanders were deemed complicit in the bullying for failing to intervene.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (together) + "plic" (fold) = folded together into an illegal act.

quizSentence: By hiding the stolen money, she became complicit in the bank robbery.
alternateExampleSentence: Several bank tellers were secretly complicit in the carefully orchestrated vault robbery scheme.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from French compliment, from Italian complimento.
exampleSentence: The talented chef received a glowing compliment from the notoriously harsh food critic.

memoryHook.text: Latin complementum (what completes); courtesy praise - remember: I like compliments.

quizSentence: It is always nice to receive a genuine compliment on your hard work.
alternateExampleSentence: It is considered polite to formally compliment the host after a lovely dinner.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compoune, from Latin componere 'put together'.

senses.exampleSentence: Smoke and panic compounded the difficulty of reaching the fire escape.
Her attraction was a compound of curiosity, desire, and quiet admiration.

senses.definition: A walled area containing a group of buildings
senses.exampleSentence: When fighting broke out, families retreated to the walled family compound.

exampleSentence: Repeated shipping delays will compound the financial losses for the struggling retailer.
quizSentence: Adding salt to the wound will only compound his intense pain.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant scientist discovered a new chemical compound that effectively destroys the virus.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French compréhensif, -ive, or late Latin comprehensivus.
exampleSentence: The committee released a comprehensive report regarding the failing city transit infrastructure.
quizSentence: The doctor requested a comprehensive blood test to check everything.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant application provides a totally comprehensive study guide for the difficult exam.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French compresser or Latin compressare, frequentative of comprimere.
exampleSentence: You must compress the digital video file before sending it through regular email.
quizSentence: You can use software to compress the video file so it sends faster.
alternateExampleSentence: He applied a freezing compress to quickly reduce the painful swelling on his knee.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French componction, from ecclesiastical Latin compunctio(n-).
exampleSentence: The ruthless thief showed no compunction when stealing the expensive diamond ring.

memoryHook.text: "com-" (intensive) + "punct" (prick) = a sharp prick of guilt.

quizSentence: He felt no compunction about stealing candy from a small child.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt severe compunction after accidentally revealing her best friend's highly guarded secret.

etymology: Late 15th century: from French concéder or Latin concedere, from con- 'completely' + cedere 'yield'.
exampleSentence: The stubborn politician refused to concede the election despite trailing by thousands of votes.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (completely) + "cedere" (yield) = to completely yield the argument.

quizSentence: The losing candidate called her rival to concede the tight election.
alternateExampleSentence: He had to concede that his original design for the database was entirely flawed.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin conciliatorius, from conciliare 'combine, gain'.
exampleSentence: The company president issued a conciliatory statement to appease the striking union.

memoryHook.text: Council - bringing the arguing sides to the council to make peace.

quizSentence: He sent a bouquet of flowers as a conciliatory gesture after their fight.
alternateExampleSentence: The apologetic manager offered a conciliatory discount to the frustrated restaurant patrons tonight.

etymology: Late 16th century: from French concis or Latin concisus, past participle of concidere 'cut up, cut down'.
exampleSentence: The brilliant executive demanded a concise summary of the lengthy financial report immediately.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (completely) + "caedere" (cut) = cut down to just the essential words.

quizSentence: Please keep your presentation concise and skip the unnecessary details.
alternateExampleSentence: Her clear and concise instructions helped the novice team finish the complicated project.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin concoct- 'cooked together, digested', from concoquere.
exampleSentence: The clever chef managed to concoct a brilliant meal from the leftover ingredients.

memoryHook.text: Cocktail - mixing ingredients together to invent a new cocktail.

quizSentence: The chef managed to concoct a brilliant sauce out of leftover ingredients.
alternateExampleSentence: The desperate teenager tried to concoct a believable excuse for breaking curfew again.

etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin concomitant- 'accompanying', from concomitari.
exampleSentence: The concomitant risks of the radical surgery were carefully explained to the patient.

memoryHook.text: Comet tent - if a comet hits your tent, disaster is a naturally accompanying result.

quizSentence: Loss of memory is a natural concomitant of advanced old age.
alternateExampleSentence: The rapid economic growth brought a concomitant increase in urban traffic and pollution.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French concorde, from Latin concordia, from concors 'of one mind'.
exampleSentence: The two rival nations signed a historic concord to officially end the war.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "cord" (heart) = hearts together in harmony.

quizSentence: The two neighboring towns existed in a state of peaceful concord for decades.
alternateExampleSentence: The historic treaty brought decades of peaceful concord between the two warring nations.

etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin condolere 'suffer together' + -ence.
exampleSentence: The governor sent a formal letter of condolence to the grieving local family.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "dolere" (grieve) = to grieve with someone.

quizSentence: The ambassador expressed condolence on behalf of the nation following the devastating earthquake.
alternateExampleSentence: He offered his deepest condolence after hearing about the tragic loss of life.

etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin condonare 'refrain from exacting'.
exampleSentence: The university explicitly stated that it does not condone any form of cheating.

memoryHook.text: Con done - the convict is done with his sentence, so you overlook his past offense.

quizSentence: The school principal refuses to condone bullying of any kind.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict university refuses to condone cheating on final exams under any circumstances.

etymology: Latin conducere, from con- ('together') + ducere ('to lead')
exampleSentence: A cluttered, noisy workspace is rarely conducive to sustained analytical thinking.

memoryHook.text: "Con-" (together) + "duc-" (lead) = conditions that lead you toward a result.

quizSentence: The mentor argued that smaller seminar sizes are conducive to deeper discussion.
alternateExampleSentence: Adequate sleep and proper nutrition are conducive to maintaining a healthy immune system.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French, from medieval Latin conductus 'pipe, channel'.
exampleSentence: The underground metal conduit protected the sensitive fiber optic cables from water damage.

memoryHook.text: Can do it - the pipe is the channel that can do the job of moving the water.

quizSentence: A large copper pipe acts as the main conduit for the building's water supply.
alternateExampleSentence: The ambassador acted as a crucial conduit between the two deeply hostile nations.

etymology: Middle English: from Latin confectio(n-), from conficere 'put together'.
exampleSentence: The decadent chocolate confection was decorated with delicate edible gold leaf and raspberries.
quizSentence: The bakery displayed a beautiful chocolate confection in the front window.
alternateExampleSentence: The delicate sugar confection was the centerpiece of the beautiful dinner party.

etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of confident (as a noun), probably to represent the pronunciation of French confidente.
exampleSentence: The lonely king trusted only his loyal advisor as a true, reliable confidant.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "fid" (trust) = the person you completely trust with your secrets.

quizSentence: A therapist serves as a professional confidant, bound by strict obligations of confidentiality.
alternateExampleSentence: The powerful queen relied heavily on her trusted confidant for vital political advice.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin conflagratio(n-), from the verb conflagrare 'burn up'.
exampleSentence: The dry forest provided perfect fuel for the rapidly spreading and deadly conflagration.

memoryHook.text: Flag ration - a massive fire burned through the military base, so they had to ration their remaining flags.

quizSentence: The dropped cigarette started a conflagration that destroyed the forest.
alternateExampleSentence: Brave firefighters battled the raging conflagration that threatened to consume the historic library.

etymology: Late Middle English: from late Latin confluentia, from confluere 'flow together'.
exampleSentence: The thriving trading city was built at the confluence of three major rivers.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "flu" (flow) = rivers (or ideas) flowing together into one.

quizSentence: The city was founded right at the confluence of the Ohio and Mississippi rivers.
alternateExampleSentence: A rare confluence of artistic talent and financial backing produced the spectacular film.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from conform + -ist. Originally applied to someone conforming to the practices of the Church of England.
exampleSentence: The rigid corporate culture was highly conformist and aggressively discouraged any creative thinking.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "form" = taking the exact same form or shape as the rest of society.

quizSentence: The strict corporate culture rewarded conformist behavior over creative thinking.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict corporate culture demanded a conformist attitude that stifled individual creative expression.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French confondre, from Latin confundere 'pour together, mix up'.
exampleSentence: The complex mathematical puzzle continues to confound even the most brilliant university professors.

memoryHook.text: Dumbfounded - you are so thoroughly confused you are left dumbfounded.

quizSentence: The complicated math problem continued to confound the frustrated student.
alternateExampleSentence: The magician's stunning illusion managed to entirely confound the highly skeptical theater audience.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French congeler, from Latin congelare, from con- 'together' + gelare 'to freeze'.
exampleSentence: The heavy grease began to slowly congeal in the cold frying pan overnight.

memoryHook.text: Jell-O - it cools down and thickens into a solid mass, just like Jell-O.

quizSentence: The bacon grease began to congeal into a white paste in the pan.
alternateExampleSentence: His entirely abstract ideas finally started to congeal into a totally brilliant plan.

definition: Pleasant and suited to one's tastes
etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin con- 'together' + genialis 'festive'.
exampleSentence: The local coffee shop offered a congenial atmosphere for quiet studying.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "gen" (nature) = sharing the same nature; highly agreeable and friendly.

quizSentence: The congenial host made sure every single party guest had a drink.
alternateExampleSentence: The congenial host ensured every guest felt welcomed and at ease throughout the evening.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin congregatio(n-), from congregare 'collect into a flock'.
exampleSentence: The entire congregation gathered in the church for the Sunday morning service.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "greg" (flock) = a flock of people gathering together.

quizSentence: The pastor looked out over the large congregation gathered for Sunday mass.
alternateExampleSentence: A large congregation of migrating birds settled around the quiet lake.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Old French congruite or late Latin congruitas, from congruus 'agreeing'.
exampleSentence: There is a clear congruity between her stated values and her daily actions.

memoryHook.text: Geometry - just like congruent triangles, everything fits perfectly together in agreement.

quizSentence: There is a noticeable lack of congruity between his words and his actions.
alternateExampleSentence: The detective noticed a strange lack of congruity in the suspect's timeline.

etymology: Latin conjectura, from conjectus ('thrown together'), from conicere

senses.definition: An inference formed without complete evidence
senses.exampleSentence: The article treats the link between diet and mood as conjecture, not certainty.

senses.definition: To form an opinion on incomplete evidence
senses.exampleSentence: Researchers conjectured that the artifact predated the written records.

memoryHook.text: "Con-" (together) + "ject" (throw) = ideas thrown together into a guess.

quizSentence: What looks like proof may still be conjecture until it survives repeated testing.
exampleSentence: Without physical evidence, the theory remains nothing more than pure conjecture.
alternateExampleSentence: The reporter based his sensational article on unproven conjecture and wild rumors.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French conniver or Latin connivere 'shut the eyes (to)'.
exampleSentence: The corrupt executives managed to connive a scheme to steal pension funds.

memoryHook.text: Knives - secretly plotting behind someone's back with knives.

quizSentence: The greedy siblings began to connive a way to steal their father's inheritance.
alternateExampleSentence: The greedy heirs tried to connive against each other for the inheritance.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consecrat- 'dedicated, devoted as sacred', from the verb consecrare.
exampleSentence: The visiting bishop arrived to formally consecrate the newly built cathedral.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "sacr" (sacred) = to officially declare something holy.

quizSentence: The bishop arrived to officially consecrate the newly built church altar.
alternateExampleSentence: The historical society will consecrate the battlefield as a permanent memorial site.

etymology: Mid 19th century: from Latin, 'agreement', from consens- 'agreed', from the verb consentire.
exampleSentence: The divided committee finally reached a consensus on the new budget proposal.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "sens" (feel) = everyone in the room feeling the same way; a general agreement.

quizSentence: The jury finally reached a unanimous consensus after three days of deliberation.
alternateExampleSentence: It took hours of debate to build a general consensus among the voters.

etymology: Late Middle English: from French consigner or Latin consignare 'mark with a seal'.
exampleSentence: The author decided to consign his original manuscripts to the university library.

memoryHook.text: Sign - you sign the paperwork to formally hand the goods over to someone else's care.

quizSentence: The estate executor consigned the furniture to an auction house specializing in antique furnishings.
alternateExampleSentence: The cruel dictator sought to consign all his political enemies to prison.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consolatio(n-), from the verb consolari 'to comfort'.
exampleSentence: The silver medal was of little consolation to the heartbroken Olympic athlete.

memoryHook.text: Console - giving a sad gamer a new console to comfort them after a major loss.

quizSentence: The bronze medal was little consolation to the athlete who expected gold.
alternateExampleSentence: A heartfelt letter from a former mentor offered real consolation during the difficult transition.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin consonant- 'sounding together', from the verb consonare.
exampleSentence: The company's new policies are consonant with its public commitment to sustainability.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "son" (sound) = sounding together in perfect harmony.

quizSentence: His charitable actions are entirely consonant with his deep religious beliefs.
alternateExampleSentence: His actions were rarely consonant with the moral promises he often made.

definition: A voting member of a community
etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin constituent- 'establishing, appointing', from the verb constituere.
exampleSentence: The senator held a meeting to address the pressing concerns of his constituent base.
quizSentence: Caffeine is the primary active constituent found in a cup of coffee.
alternateExampleSentence: Oxygen is a primary constituent of the water molecule along with hydrogen.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French constraindre, from Latin constringere 'bind tightly together'.
exampleSentence: Strict copyright law constrained the documentary filmmaker's use of archival footage and music.
quizSentence: Budget limitations constrain the department's ability to recruit top candidates from competing institutions.
alternateExampleSentence: The narrow mountain road served to constrain the speed of the passing traffic.

definition: To interpret words a certain way
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin construere 'build up, assemble', from con- 'together' + struere 'build'.
exampleSentence: A neutral facial expression should never be construed as disagreement or disengagement.

memoryHook.text: Construct - you carefully construct the meaning of a complex sentence in your mind.

quizSentence: The attorney urged the jury not to construe the defendant's silence as guilt.
alternateExampleSentence: It was easy to construe the sudden departure as a sign of failure.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consummat- 'brought to completion', from the verb consummare.
exampleSentence: The veteran diplomat handled the tense negotiations with consummate skill and grace.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "summa" (highest) = brought to the absolute highest, most complete level of skill.

quizSentence: He played the violin with the consummate skill of a seasoned professional.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a consummate professional who demands absolute perfection from his team.

definition: The using up of a resource
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin consumptio(n-), from the verb consumere 'use up'.
exampleSentence: The runner increased his carbohydrate consumption in the days before the race.
quizSentence: The doctor warned him that excessive alcohol consumption would ruin his liver.
alternateExampleSentence: The government launched a campaign to reduce the overall consumption of electricity.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin contemporaneus (from con- 'together with' + tempus, tempor- 'time') + -ous.
exampleSentence: The two scientific discoveries were contemporaneous, occurring on the exact same day.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "tempor" (time) = two events happening at the exact same time.

quizSentence: The two distinct ancient civilizations were actually contemporaneous with each other.
alternateExampleSentence: Historians rely on contemporaneous accounts written by people who lived through the war.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French contentieux, from Latin contentiosus, from content- 'striven'.
exampleSentence: The property line dispute proved to be a contentious issue between the neighbors.

memoryHook.text: Contender - a boxer who loves to fight and argue.

quizSentence: Gun control remains a contentious issue in modern political debates.
alternateExampleSentence: The city council meeting erupted into a contentious debate over tax increases.

etymology: From Latin contextus ('woven together') + -ize
definition: To place in a descriptive setting
exampleSentence: To understand the novel, you must contextualize the author's writing within history.

memoryHook.text: Context + -ize = to put something inside its surrounding context.

quizSentence: Good historians contextualize events instead of judging them by modern standards alone.
alternateExampleSentence: The professor asked the students to contextualize the data within modern economics.

definition: To violate a rule or law
etymology: Mid 16th century: from late Latin contravenire, from Latin contra 'against' + venire 'come'.
exampleSentence: The factory's waste disposal methods flagrantly contravene the new environmental protection laws.

memoryHook.text: "contra-" (against) + "venire" (come) = to come against a direct rule or law.

quizSentence: By parking in front of the fire hydrant, you contravene city traffic laws.
alternateExampleSentence: You cannot contravene the terms of the signed contract without facing strict penalties.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French contrit, from Latin contritus, past participle of conterere 'grind down'.
exampleSentence: The student offered a contrite apology after breaking the expensive laboratory equipment.

memoryHook.text: From Latin contritus (ground to pieces). Your heart is crushed and ground to pieces by the guilt of what you've done.

quizSentence: The contrite child apologized sincerely for breaking the neighbor's window.
alternateExampleSentence: His contrite expression suggested that he genuinely regretted his harsh words yesterday.

etymology: Late 16th century: a word of unknown origin, originally an Oxford University slang term for a pedant.
exampleSentence: Figuring out how to monetize the app remains a significant financial conundrum.

memoryHook.text: Nun drum - a nun playing a heavy metal drum kit is a confusing, puzzling situation to figure out.

quizSentence: Figuring out how to pay rent without a job is a stressful conundrum.
alternateExampleSentence: The detective faced a conundrum when the evidence pointed to two different suspects.

definition: To come together for a meeting
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin convenire 'assemble, agree, fit', from con- 'together' + venire 'come'.
exampleSentence: The board of directors will convene next Tuesday to elect new corporate officers.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "venire" (come) = to officially come together for a meeting.

quizSentence: The board of directors will convene next Tuesday to discuss the merger.
alternateExampleSentence: The delegates agreed to convene in Geneva to discuss the global climate treaty.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin conventio(n-) 'meeting, covenant', from the verb convenire.

senses.exampleSentence: The hotel was full because of the cattleranchers’ convention.
The cattle-ranchers have a convention that you take off your boots before entering their houses.

exampleSentence: The software designer decided to break with UI convention and build something unique.
quizSentence: Shaking hands is a standard social convention when meeting someone new.
alternateExampleSentence: It is a standard convention to shake hands when greeting someone professionally.

etymology: From converse ('turned about') + -ly
exampleSentence: The medication cured his headache; conversely, it caused severe nausea all day.

memoryHook.text: Converse = turn around — conversely turns the argument to the other side.

quizSentence: The first trial showed gains in speed; conversely, accuracy declined sharply.
alternateExampleSentence: A strong economy helps politicians; conversely, a recession usually leads to their defeat.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin convivialis, from convivium 'a feast', from con- 'with' + vivere 'live'.
exampleSentence: The dinner party was a convivial affair filled with laughter and good food.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (with) + "viv" (life) = full of life, highly festive, and sociable.

quizSentence: The Irish pub has a warm, convivial atmosphere that draws huge crowds.
alternateExampleSentence: The local tavern was known for its convivial atmosphere and excellent live music.

etymology: Late 18th century: past participle of convolute, from Latin convolutus 'rolled together'.
exampleSentence: The legal document was so convoluted that even seasoned attorneys struggled reading it.

memoryHook.text: "con-" (together) + "volvere" (roll) = rolled and twisted together so many times it becomes impossibly complicated.

quizSentence: The movie's convoluted plot left the entire audience confused by the ending.
alternateExampleSentence: The plot of the thriller movie became hopelessly convoluted by the third act.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French copieux or Latin copiosus, from copia 'plenty'.
exampleSentence: The dedicated student took copious notes during the three-hour lecture on algorithms.

memoryHook.text: Copy us - the printer made so many copies you now have an abundant supply.

quizSentence: She took copious notes during the lecture to prepare for the final exam.
alternateExampleSentence: The heavy storm dumped copious amounts of rain, leading to severe local flooding.

etymology: Middle English: from medieval Latin cordialis, from Latin cor, cord- 'heart'.
exampleSentence: The rival athletes shared a surprisingly cordial conversation after the championship match.

memoryHook.text: "cord" (heart) = acting in a warm, polite, and heartfelt way.

quizSentence: The ex-spouses managed to maintain a polite and cordial relationship for their kids.
alternateExampleSentence: The hotel staff offered a cordial greeting to every guest who arrived today.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin corpulentia, from corpulentus 'fleshy', from corpus 'body'.
exampleSentence: The king's increasing corpulence was attributed to a rich diet and little exercise.

memoryHook.text: "corp" (body) = having a massive, excessively fat body.

quizSentence: His increasing corpulence made it difficult for him to walk up the stairs.
alternateExampleSentence: The veterinarian warned that the dog's corpulence would lead to severe joint issues.

etymology: Medieval Latin correlare, from Latin com- ('together') + relatus ('carried back')
exampleSentence: The researcher found that high stress levels strongly correlate with chronic sleep deprivation.

memoryHook.text: "Co-" (together) + "relate" = variables related together.

quizSentence: Analysts correlated rising temperatures with increased crop failures across the region.
alternateExampleSentence: The marketing data seemed to correlate user retention directly with the onboarding experience.

definition: To confirm or give support to
etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin corroborat- 'strengthened', from the verb corroborare.
exampleSentence: The security camera footage helped corroborate the eyewitness's account of the robbery.

memoryHook.text: "cor-" (together) + "robor" (strength) = to add strength to a witness's story by providing matching evidence.

quizSentence: Security camera footage helped to corroborate the witness's timeline of events.
alternateExampleSentence: Multiple scientific studies corroborate the theory that regular exercise improves mental health.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French corosif, -ive, from late Latin corrosivus, from Latin corrodere 'gnaw through'.
exampleSentence: The corrosive acid slowly ate through the thick metal piping in the laboratory.
quizSentence: Battery acid is highly corrosive and will burn right through your clothes.
alternateExampleSentence: The politician's corrosive rhetoric severely damaged the fragile unity of the community.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek kosmopolitēs (from kosmos 'world' + politēs 'citizen') + -an.
exampleSentence: Having lived in Tokyo and London, she possessed a truly cosmopolitan worldview.

memoryHook.text: "cosm" (world) + "polit" (citizen) = a citizen of the world; highly sophisticated and well-traveled.

quizSentence: New York is a truly cosmopolitan city featuring cultures from around the world.
alternateExampleSentence: The city's diverse culinary scene reflects its growing cosmopolitan population base.

definition: To act against to reduce force
etymology: Mid 17th century: from counter- 'against' + act.
exampleSentence: The doctor prescribed an antidote specifically designed to counteract the snakebite venom.
quizSentence: Drinking plenty of water can help counteract the dehydrating effects of coffee.
alternateExampleSentence: He drank black coffee to counteract the drowsiness caused by his allergy medication.

etymology: Late 18th century: from French, from medieval Latin colpus 'blow'.

senses.exampleSentence: Pulling off the merger on such short notice was a remarkable coup for the firm.

senses.definition: The overthrow of a government and assumption of authority
senses.exampleSentence: The sudden coup toppled the elected government within a single night.

memoryHook.text: Coop - the angry chickens violently overthrew the farmer to take control of the coop.

definition: A sudden, violent seizure of power
exampleSentence: Landing the sought-after executive was considered a major corporate coup for the startup.
quizSentence: The military generals staged a sudden coup and overthrew the democratic government.
alternateExampleSentence: The military staged a sudden coup, overthrowing the elected government in one night.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French, past participle of covrir 'to cover'.
exampleSentence: The commando brigade launched a covert operation deep inside dangerous hostile territory.

memoryHook.text: Cover it - keeping the military operation completely covered up and secret.

quizSentence: The CIA launched a covert operation to rescue the hostages at midnight.
alternateExampleSentence: The investigative journalist used a covert camera to expose the illegal factory conditions.

definition: To fiercely desire what others have
etymology: Middle English: from Old French cuveitier, based on Latin cupiditas 'cupidity'.
exampleSentence: It is human nature to covet the success of those who are wealthy.

memoryHook.text: Cover it - you want your neighbor's new car so badly you throw a tarp over it to steal it.

quizSentence: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's luxury sports car.
alternateExampleSentence: The collector heavily coveted the rare comic book displayed inside the museum.

definition: A tendency to be too trusting
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French credulite or Latin credulitas, from credulus 'believing'.
exampleSentence: The phishing scam was entirely reliant on the credulity of older internet users.

memoryHook.text: "cred" (believe) = an excessive readiness to believe things; extreme gullibility.

quizSentence: Scammers prey on the credulity of people who don't understand the internet.
alternateExampleSentence: His absurd explanation for being late stretched the teacher's credulity to its limit.

etymology: Late 18th century: Italian, present participle of crescere 'to grow', from Latin.
exampleSentence: The crowd's cheering reached a deafening crescendo as the team scored the goal.
quizSentence: The symphony built to a magnificent, deafening crescendo in the final movement.
alternateExampleSentence: The classical symphony built to a magnificent crescendo in its dramatic final movement.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Greek kritērion 'means of judging', from kritēs 'judge'.
exampleSentence: The judges established strict criteria for evaluating the contestants in the science fair.
quizSentence: You must meet all the physical criteria to be accepted into the military.
alternateExampleSentence: The software engineer ensured the code met all criteria for the store review.

definition: The highest or final climactic point
etymology: Early 17th century: from late Latin culminatio(n-), from the verb culminare 'to crown'.
exampleSentence: The successful launch was the culmination of months of intense coding and design.
quizSentence: The successful rocket launch was the culmination of ten years of hard work.
alternateExampleSentence: The championship trophy was the culmination of a grueling season for the team.

definition: Deserving heavy blame for a fault
etymology: Middle English: from Old French coupable, from Latin culpabilis, from culpa 'fault, blame'.
exampleSentence: The jury found the reckless driver culpable for the devastating traffic accident.

memoryHook.text: "culp" (blame) = fully deserving of the blame.

quizSentence: The investigation determined that the driver was culpable for the tragic accident.
alternateExampleSentence: The getaway driver was still legally culpable for the robbery of the bank.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin cultivat- 'prepared for crops', from the verb cultivare.
exampleSentence: He worked hard to cultivate a thriving garden of raised beds this spring.
quizSentence: The farmer worked hard to cultivate the rocky soil into a fertile garden.
alternateExampleSentence: The university attempts to cultivate a deep sense of intellectual curiosity among students.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin cumulatus 'heaped up' + -ive.
exampleSentence: The cumulative effect of skipping multiple training runs resulted in a terrible time.
quizSentence: The cumulative effect of smoking for forty years severely damaged his lungs.
alternateExampleSentence: The professor warned that the final exam would be cumulative covering the semester.

etymology: Middle English: perhaps from Old Norse kunnandi 'knowledge', from kunna 'know'.
exampleSentence: The cunning fox managed to bypass the traps set by the frustrated farmer.
quizSentence: The cunning fox sneaked right past the sleeping guard dogs into the henhouse.
alternateExampleSentence: The defense attorney used a cunning strategy to discredit the prosecution's star witness.

definition: Extreme greed for money or possessions
etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French cupidite or Latin cupiditas, from cupidus 'desirous', from cupere 'to desire'.
exampleSentence: The CEO's cupidity led him to embezzle millions from the company pension fund.

memoryHook.text: Named after Cupid, the Roman god of desire. While Cupid is associated with love, the word evolved to mean an extreme, greedy desire for…

quizSentence: The villain's blinding cupidity drove him to steal from his own family.
alternateExampleSentence: The novel serves as a cautionary tale about the destructive nature of cupidity.

etymology: Early 17th century: from French cursoire or late Latin cursorius 'of a runner', from Latin cursor 'runner'.
exampleSentence: He gave the dense legal document only a cursory glance before foolishly signing.

memoryHook.text: Cursor - moving your computer cursor quickly over the text without actually stopping to read it.

quizSentence: The busy editor only gave the manuscript a quick, cursory glance.
alternateExampleSentence: The security guard performed a cursory inspection of the vehicle before waving it through.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin curtus 'cut short, abridged'.
exampleSentence: The busy manager's curt email left the employee wondering if she was angry.

memoryHook.text: Cut - a spoken response so short and rude it feels like you've been cut off.

quizSentence: The director's curt response to the proposal discouraged further discussion at the meeting.
alternateExampleSentence: When asked about his controversial decision, the politician gave a remarkably curt reply.

definition: To reduce in extent or quantity
etymology: Late 15th century: from older curtal, from French courtault, from court 'short'.
exampleSentence: The school board voted to severely curtail the budget for extracurricular arts programs.

memoryHook.text: Cut tail - to cut the tail off a long meeting to shorten it.

quizSentence: Rising fuel costs forced the airline to curtail several domestic routes without prior notice.
alternateExampleSentence: A deteriorating forecast forced the expedition to curtail the offshore fieldwork by three days.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French danter, from Latin domare 'to tame'.
exampleSentence: The prospect of completing a lengthy reserve service requirement was undeniably daunting.

memoryHook.text: Haunting - a ghost haunting your house is an incredibly intimidating and scary problem to deal with.

quizSentence: Climbing Mount Everest is a daunting task even for experienced mountaineers.
alternateExampleSentence: Standing at the base of the mountain, the climbers faced a daunting ascent.

etymology: Middle English: from dear + -th. Original sense was 'shortness of food'.
exampleSentence: There is a severe dearth of affordable housing available in the downtown area.

memoryHook.text: Dead earth - a dead patch of earth has a severe scarcity and lack of crops.

quizSentence: There is a severe dearth of fresh produce at this tiny corner store.
alternateExampleSentence: The frustrated coach complained about the dearth of experienced players on the roster.

etymology: Early 19th century: from French débâcle, from débâcler 'unbar', from dé- 'un-' + bâcler 'to bar'.
exampleSentence: The poorly planned product launch quickly turned into a public relations debacle.

memoryHook.text: Da buckle - the buckle on the CEO's belt breaks during a keynote speech, causing a complete public disaster.

quizSentence: The badly planned music festival turned into a logistical debacle.
alternateExampleSentence: The military offensive was a debacle that resulted in zero strategic gain.

definition: To reduce the moral value of
etymology: Mid 16th century: from de- 'down' + base, probably on the pattern of abase.
exampleSentence: The corrupt king chose to debase the national currency by mixing in lead.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "base" = to lower the value, status, or quality of something.

quizSentence: Printing too much paper money will inevitably debase the national currency.
alternateExampleSentence: The bitter politician used his platform to debase the character of his opponent.

definition: To destroy the moral purity of
etymology: Late 16th century: from French débaucher 'turn away from one's duty'.
exampleSentence: The conquering army proceeded to debauch the peaceful village during their ruthless invasion.

memoryHook.text: Da botch - you completely botched your life by engaging in extreme, corrupt partying.

quizSentence: The lottery winner allowed endless parties to debauch his orderly life.
alternateExampleSentence: Greed and unlimited power will eventually debauch even the most noble political leader.

definition: To expose a myth as false
etymology: 1920s: from de- (expressing removal) + bunk.
exampleSentence: The scientists conducted a rigorous experiment to definitively debunk the popular internet myth.

memoryHook.text: De-bunk - kicking your brother out of the top bunk because his claim that he called dibs is entirely false.

quizSentence: The science show aims to debunk common urban legends using physics.
alternateExampleSentence: The documentary aims to debunk the conspiracy theories surrounding the historical event.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin decorus 'becoming' + -ous.
exampleSentence: The guests at the diplomatic dinner were expected to maintain decorous behavior always.

memoryHook.text: Decorate - having excellent manners, perfectly presented like a beautifully decorated room.

quizSentence: The students maintained a quiet, decorous demeanor during the solemn assembly.
alternateExampleSentence: Despite the emotional trial, the courtroom proceedings remained quiet, respectful, and decorous.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from de- 'down' + cry, on the pattern of French décrier.
exampleSentence: Human rights organizations routinely decry the use of solitary confinement in the prison.

memoryHook.text: Cry - crying out publicly against a policy you hate.

quizSentence: Activists gathered in the square to decry the harsh new immigration laws.
alternateExampleSentence: Human rights activists frequently decry the harsh conditions within the overcrowded detention centers.

definition: To spoil the surface or appearance
etymology: Middle English: from Old French desfacier, from des- (expressing removal) + face 'face'.
exampleSentence: The vandals used bright spray paint to deface the historic monument downtown.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down/remove) + "face" = to ruin the surface or outward appearance of something (like graffiti).

quizSentence: Vandals used red spray paint to deface the historic marble monument.
alternateExampleSentence: It is a serious federal crime to intentionally deface protected government property.

definition: Damaging the good reputation of someone
etymology: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin diffamatorius, from diffamare 'publish a bad report'.
exampleSentence: The celebrity sued the tabloid magazine for publishing defamatory rumors about her life.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (remove) + "fame" = words specifically designed to destroy someone's fame or reputation.

quizSentence: The actor sued the tabloid for publishing a defamatory article about his marriage.
alternateExampleSentence: The CEO issued a statement condemning the defamatory allegations made by the employee.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Old French differer 'differ, defer', from Latin differre.
exampleSentence: The university allowed the accepted student to defer her enrollment for one year.
quizSentence: Junior physicians are trained to defer to attending surgeons during high-stakes procedures.
alternateExampleSentence: The committee agreed to defer the vote until additional financial data could be reviewed.

etymology: Early 19th century: from deference + -ial.
exampleSentence: The young apprentice was deferential to the master craftsman's decades of experience.

memoryHook.text: Reference - treating someone with such deep respect that you always use them as a reference.

quizSentence: The young apprentice was deeply deferential to the master craftsman.
alternateExampleSentence: The hotel staff maintained a deferential attitude toward the visiting foreign dignitaries.

etymology: Middle English: alteration of defoul, from Old French defouler 'trample down', influenced by file 'to foul'.
exampleSentence: The invading army deliberately tried to defile the sacred temple with insulting graffiti.

memoryHook.text: File - spilling black coffee all over a clean file, completely corrupting and dirtying it.

quizSentence: Industrial runoff continues to defile the waters of the local river.
alternateExampleSentence: The invading army sought to defile the sacred temple of the local population.

etymology: Middle English: variant of daft, in the sense 'meek'.
exampleSentence: The baker used deft movements to braid the strands of the artisanal pastry.

memoryHook.text: Theft - a pickpocket must be incredibly quick and skillful to pull off a theft.

quizSentence: The deft surgeon successfully repaired the tiny blood vessel with absolute precision.
alternateExampleSentence: The politician's deft handling of the hostile interview impressed even his fiercest critics.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin defunctus 'dead', past participle of defungi 'carry out, finish'.
exampleSentence: The urban explorer photographed the rusted machinery inside the defunct automobile factory.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (off) + "funct" (perform) = no longer functioning; dead or extinct.

quizSentence: The explorers found a rusted train engine belonging to a defunct railway company.
alternateExampleSentence: The ancient laws governing the city's trade routes have been defunct for centuries.

definition: To entrust a task to another
etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin delegatus 'sent on a commission', from the verb delegare.
exampleSentence: The exhausted manager finally learned how to successfully delegate tasks to his employees.
quizSentence: A good manager knows how to delegate tedious tasks to her capable assistants.
alternateExampleSentence: The software architect decided to delegate the interface design to the junior developer.

etymology: Mid 17th century: via medieval Latin from Greek dēlētērios 'noxious', from dēlētēr 'destroyer'.
exampleSentence: The deleterious effects of smoking on cardiovascular health have been extensively documented.

memoryHook.text: Delete - a computer virus that is so harmful it deletes all your hard drive files.

quizSentence: Smoking has deleterious effects on your respiratory system over time.
alternateExampleSentence: The chemical runoff had a deleterious impact on the local river's fish population.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin deliberatus, past participle of deliberare 'consider carefully'.
exampleSentence: The jury took three days to deliberate the complex evidence presented in court.
quizSentence: The arsonist made a deliberate choice to set the abandoned warehouse on fire.
alternateExampleSentence: The arsonist's deliberate actions caused millions of dollars in structural damage tonight.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin delineat- 'outlined', from the verb delineare, from de- 'out, completely' + linea 'line'.
exampleSentence: The contract serves to clearly delineate the specific responsibilities of each business partner.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (out) + "line" = to trace the exact lines or outline of a plan in detail.

quizSentence: The treaty delineates the maritime boundary between the two nations' contested fishing zones.
alternateExampleSentence: The architect used a red marker to delineate the property boundaries on paper.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Greek dēmagōgos, from dēmos 'the people' + agōgos 'leading'.
exampleSentence: The populist demagogue manipulated the anger of the working class to secure power.

memoryHook.text: "dem" (people) + "agog" (leader) = a toxic political leader who manipulates the dark emotions of the people to gain power.

quizSentence: The populist demagogue manipulated the crowd's anger to win the election.
alternateExampleSentence: History shows that a charismatic demagogue can quickly dismantle a fragile democratic system.

etymology: Early 18th century: from Spanish demarcación, from demarcar 'mark the bounds of'.
exampleSentence: The flowing river serves as a natural line of demarcation between the provinces.

memoryHook.text: The mark - setting the physical mark on the ground to define a clear boundary.

quizSentence: The river serves as a natural line of demarcation between the two counties.
alternateExampleSentence: The CEO established a clear demarcation of duties between the marketing and sales.

etymology: Early 17th century: from de- 'completely' + mean (in the sense 'inferior').
exampleSentence: The abusive coach would frequently demean his young players in front of their families.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "mean" = to put someone down and intentionally make them feel inferior.

quizSentence: The arrogant boss loves to demean his employees in front of major clients.
alternateExampleSentence: She refused to demean herself by participating in the petty office gossip.

etymology: Late Middle English: perhaps from Old French demoure, past participle of demourer 'remain'.
exampleSentence: The demure young woman sat quietly in the corner of the crowded room.

memoryHook.text: Murmur - a shy, modest girl who only speaks in a quiet murmur.

quizSentence: The demure young woman sat quietly with her hands folded in her lap.
alternateExampleSentence: Despite her explosive talent on stage, the famous actress was demure in life.

etymology: Late 15th century: from Latin denigrat- 'blackened', from the verb denigrare, from de- 'completely' + niger 'black'.
exampleSentence: The harsh film critic used his column to denigrate the director's latest movie.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (completely) + "nigr" (black) = to heavily blacken someone's reputation with insults.

quizSentence: You shouldn't denigrate her artistic efforts just because you don't like abstract art.
alternateExampleSentence: It is unprofessional to unfairly denigrate your former employer during a job interview.

etymology: Middle English: from Old French denoncier, from Latin denuntiare 'give official information'.
exampleSentence: The international community was quick to denounce the country's unprovoked military aggression.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (down) + "nuntiare" (speak/announce) = to publicly announce your rejection of someone.

quizSentence: The president went on television to denounce the violent terrorist attacks.
alternateExampleSentence: The mayor held a press conference to publicly denounce the corrupt police chief.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from French déplorer or Italian deplorare, from Latin deplorare.
exampleSentence: Human rights organizations deplore the systematic use of detention without formal charges.
quizSentence: The ethics board deplored the researcher's decision to falsify data in the published study.
alternateExampleSentence: The teachers deplore the drastic funding cuts that will eliminate the music program.

etymology: Mid 17th century: alteration of obsolete pravity, from Latin pravitas, from pravus 'crooked, perverse'.
exampleSentence: The serial killer's crimes revealed a level of psychological depravity that shocked detectives.

memoryHook.text: Deprive - a villain completely deprived of any moral goodness.

quizSentence: The serial killer's diary revealed a shocking level of psychological depravity.
alternateExampleSentence: The ancient Roman emperor was infamous for his legendary cruelty and moral depravity.

etymology: Early 17th century: from Latin deprecat- 'prayed against', from the verb deprecari.
exampleSentence: The seasoned comedian would frequently use gentle humor to deprecate his own appearance.

memoryHook.text: Appreciate (the opposite) - to heavily express disapproval of something, the exact opposite of appreciating it.

quizSentence: He tends to self-deprecate whenever someone compliments his incredible cooking.
alternateExampleSentence: The software company announced it would deprecate the outdated version of the application.

etymology: Mid 17th century: from Latin derelictus 'forsaken', past participle of derelinquere.
exampleSentence: The city ordered the demolition of the derelict building because it was unsafe.

memoryHook.text: Dairy licked - the abandoned ice cream factory is so ruined that stray dogs have licked all the rusted dairy vats.

quizSentence: The city tore down the derelict apartment building because it was unsafe.
alternateExampleSentence: The security guard was fired for being derelict in his duties during the robbery.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deridere 'scoff at'.
exampleSentence: The arrogant seniors would often deride the freshmen for their lack of experience.

memoryHook.text: The ride - ruthlessly mocking the kid who is too scared to go on the roller coaster ride.

quizSentence: The bullies used to deride him for wearing thick glasses in middle school.
alternateExampleSentence: Critics were quick to deride the billionaire's absurd plan to colonize the moon.

definition: Imitative of the work of another
etymology: Late Middle English: from French dérivatif, -ive, from Latin derivare 'draw off'.
exampleSentence: The movie was panned by critics for being a derivative copy of older films.
quizSentence: The new fantasy novel feels derivative of The Lord of the Rings.
alternateExampleSentence: The artist's new collection was uninspired and derivative of his earlier, better work.

definition: To treat a sacred place disrespectfully
etymology: Late 17th century: from de- (expressing reversal) + consecrate.
exampleSentence: The vandals broke into the cemetery overnight to purposefully desecrate the historical gravesites.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (reverse) + "sacr" (sacred) = to deliberately ruin something holy.

quizSentence: Grave robbers broke into the tomb to desecrate the ancient pharaoh's sarcophagus.
alternateExampleSentence: The invading forces threatened to desecrate the holy relics held within the cathedral.

etymology: Late 16th century: from Latin desiccat- 'made thoroughly dry', from the verb desiccare.
exampleSentence: After months without rain, the harsh summer sun left the agricultural fields desiccated.

memoryHook.text: Desert - completely dried out and lifeless, exactly like a desert.

quizSentence: The desert sun left the abandoned fruit desiccated and hard as rock.
alternateExampleSentence: The explorer discovered the desiccated remains of a civilization buried beneath the sand.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin desolatus 'abandoned', from the verb desolare.
exampleSentence: The harsh winter winds swept across the barren, desolate landscape of the tundra.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (completely) + "sol" (alone) = completely left alone, empty, and barren.

quizSentence: The desolate landscape of the moon is devoid of life or color.
alternateExampleSentence: After the factory closed, the once thriving industrial town became a desolate wasteland.

etymology: Late 17th century: from Latin despondent- 'giving up', from the verb despondere.
exampleSentence: The athlete felt despondent after a severe knee injury ended his Olympic dreams.

memoryHook.text: Respond - someone so hopelessly depressed they won't even respond to your texts.

quizSentence: He became severely despondent after failing the bar exam for the third time.
alternateExampleSentence: She became despondent when her meticulously planned startup failed to secure venture funding.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from French despote, from Greek despotēs 'master, absolute ruler'.
exampleSentence: The ruthless despot ruled the small island nation with a brutal secret police.

memoryHook.text: Death pot - a cruel, absolute dictator who throws his enemies into a boiling death pot.

quizSentence: The cruel despot ruled his small country with an iron fist and secret police.
alternateExampleSentence: The citizens finally launched a revolution to overthrow the cruel despot in power.

etymology: Late Middle English: from Latin destitutus 'forsaken', past participle of destituere.
exampleSentence: The sudden stock market crash left wealthy investors penniless and destitute overnight.

memoryHook.text: Deserted - someone who has been entirely deserted by wealth, leaving them in extreme poverty.

quizSentence: The hurricane left thousands of families homeless and destitute.
alternateExampleSentence: The charity organization provides hot meals and warm clothing to the destitute population.

etymology: Mid 16th century: from Latin deterrere, from de- 'away from' + terrere 'frighten'.
exampleSentence: The presence of visible security cameras helped to deter shoplifters from targeting stores.
quizSentence: The heavy security presence is meant to deter shoplifters from stealing merchandise.
alternateExampleSentence: Harsh winter storms did not deter the brave explorers from reaching the summit.

definition: Using underhanded tactics to achieve goals
etymology: From Latin devius ‘off the road,’ hence roundabout or dishonest.
exampleSentence: The villain devised a devious plot to steal the crown jewels during dinner.

memoryHook.text: "de-" (away) + "via" (way) = wandering away from the straight, honest path to be sneaky.

quizSentence: The devious villain concocted a brilliant scheme to rob the heavily guarded bank.
alternateExampleSentence: The detective quickly saw through the suspect's devious attempts to establish an alibi.

etymology: Greek diaphanēs ‘showing through,’ from dia- + phainein ‘show.’
exampleSentence: The graceful dancer wore a beautiful, diaphanous silk gown that floated on stage.
