memoryHook.text: "dia-" (through) + "phan" (show) = fabric so light and sheer that light shows right through it.

quizSentence: The dancer wore a diaphanous silk skirt that floated beautifully as she spun.
alternateExampleSentence: The early morning mist created a diaphanous veil over the surface of the lake.

etymology: Greek didaktikos ‘skilled in teaching,’ from didáskein ‘teach.’

senses.exampleSentence: She wrote up a didactic document showing new employees how to handle the company’s customers.
His didactic lectures pressed one viewpoint and left little room for debate.

memoryHook.text: Dictator - a teacher who constantly lectures you on morals like a strict dictator.

definition: Intended to teach a moral lesson
exampleSentence: The children's television show is didactic, teaching basic math and reading skills daily.
quizSentence: The children's fable is heavily didactic, aiming to teach a moral lesson about greed.
alternateExampleSentence: The professor's didactic lecture style was informative but slightly dry for the students.

etymology: Latin diffidere ‘to mistrust,’ hence lacking self-confidence.
exampleSentence: The diffident student was too shy to raise her hand in the classroom.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "fid" (trust) = lacking trust in your own abilities; highly shy and unconfident.

quizSentence: The diffident student was too shy to raise her hand in the large lecture hall.
alternateExampleSentence: He offered a diffident apology for arriving late to the important board meeting.

etymology: Latin diffundere ‘pour out,’ from dis- + fundere ‘pour.’

senses.definition: To scatter, thin out, break up
senses.exampleSentence: He diffused the tension in the room by making a joke.
In her writings, she tried unsuccessfully to make others understand her diffuse thoughts.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "fundere" (pour) = poured apart, spreading out thinly over a wide area.

definition: To spread over a wide area
exampleSentence: The frosted glass was designed to soften and diffuse the harsh sunlight entering.
quizSentence: Use this glass lampshade to diffuse the harsh glare of the bare lightbulb.
alternateExampleSentence: The journalist wrote an article to diffuse the controversial information to the public.

etymology: Late Latin dilatorius ‘tending to delay,’ from differre ‘put off.’
exampleSentence: The contractor's dilatory work ethic caused the construction project to fall behind schedule.

memoryHook.text: Delay story - the lawyer told a long, pointless delay story just to waste time in court.

quizSentence: The contractor used dilatory tactics to delay finishing the bathroom remodel.
alternateExampleSentence: The lawyer used dilatory tactics to intentionally delay the start of the trial.

etymology: Latin diligere ‘to love, esteem,’ extended to careful effort.
exampleSentence: The diligent developer spent hours debugging the Swift code before the app launch.
quizSentence: A diligent researcher always double-checks her sources before publishing a paper.
alternateExampleSentence: Her diligent study habits easily earned her the highest grade in the class.

etymology: Old French diminuer, from Latin deminuere, from de- ('away') + minuere ('lessen')
exampleSentence: The heavy rain did nothing to diminish the enthusiasm of the concert crowd.

memoryHook.text: "Di-" (away) + "min" (small) = to make something smaller.

quizSentence: The new evidence diminished the credibility of the earlier conclusion.
alternateExampleSentence: The scandal will permanently diminish the senator's carefully cultivated reputation in the capital.

etymology: Latin deminuere ‘lessen,’ via diminutivus ‘small-sized.’
exampleSentence: Despite his diminutive stature, the fierce guard dog possessed a surprisingly loud bark.

memoryHook.text: "min" (small) = extremely or unusually small.

quizSentence: The diminutive Chihuahua bravely barked at the giant Great Dane.
alternateExampleSentence: The diminutive apartment in the city center was barely large enough for him.

etymology: Latin dirige ‘direct,’ opening word of a funeral chant.
exampleSentence: The lonely bagpiper played a mournful dirge as the fallen soldier was buried.

memoryHook.text: Dirt - the slow, mournful song played while throwing dirt on a coffin.

quizSentence: A lone bagpiper played a mournful dirge as the casket was lowered.
alternateExampleSentence: The slow, haunting cello solo sounded like a dirge written for lost love.

etymology: Latin dis- ‘apart’ + affectus ‘disposed,’ reversal of favorable feeling.
exampleSentence: The disaffected voters abandoned the incumbent political party during the recent midterm elections.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "affect" = no longer emotionally affected by or loyal to the leader.

quizSentence: Disaffected voters are unlikely to participate in the upcoming mayoral election.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager struggled to motivate the disaffected employees who felt undervalued by leadership.

definition: To deny any responsibility or support
etymology: Old French desavouer; Latin dis- + Medieval Latin avoware ‘declare.’
exampleSentence: The candidate was forced to disavow the controversial statements made by his manager.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "avow" (swear) = to swear you have absolutely no connection to something.

quizSentence: The politician rushed to disavow any connection to the bribery scandal.
alternateExampleSentence: The captured spy was told the government would disavow any knowledge of him.

etymology: Latin discernere ‘separate apart,’ hence distinguish.
exampleSentence: The expert could easily discern the subtle differences between the authentic painting and forgery.
quizSentence: It is hard to discern the subtle differences between these two shades of blue.
alternateExampleSentence: It was difficult to discern the true motive behind his generous charitable donation.

etymology: Latin disclaudere ‘unclose,’ from dis- + claudere ‘close.’
exampleSentence: The strict contract forbade the employees to disclose any unreleased product features.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (un) + "close" = to un-close the vault and reveal a secret.

quizSentence: The company is legally required to disclose all potential side effects of the drug.
alternateExampleSentence: The journalist refused to disclose the identity of the confidential government informant.

etymology: Middle French desconforter ‘rob of courage,’ confiture figuratively ‘comfort.’
exampleSentence: The prosecutor's aggressive line of questioning was designed to discomfit the witness.

memoryHook.text: Discomfort - to make someone feel deeply embarrassed and in a state of discomfort.

quizSentence: The reporter's aggressive personal questions managed to discomfit the evasive mayor.
alternateExampleSentence: The unexpected malfunction of his presentation slides served to discomfit the speaker.

etymology: Latin discordant-, stem of discordāns, from discordia ‘disagreement.’
exampleSentence: The beginner orchestra produced a discordant sound during their very first rehearsal.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "cord" (heart) = hearts apart, creating harsh, clashing sounds or opinions.

quizSentence: The beginner orchestra produced a horrible, discordant screeching noise.
alternateExampleSentence: The committee meeting was derailed by the discordant opinions of the board.

etymology: Latin discrepantia ‘discord,’ from discrepare ‘sound differently.’
exampleSentence: The auditor noticed a discrepancy between the reported profits and bank statements.
quizSentence: The auditor found a discrepancy between the bank statements and the ledger.
alternateExampleSentence: There was a confusing discrepancy in the suspect's timeline that worried detectives.

etymology: Latin discretio ‘separation, discernment,’ from discernere.
exampleSentence: The therapist assured the nervous patient that their conversations required discretion.
quizSentence: A therapist must maintain discretion regarding her patients' private lives.
alternateExampleSentence: The judge has the legal discretion to lower the harsh criminal sentence.

etymology: Latin discursus ‘running about,’ from dis- + currere ‘run.’
exampleSentence: The professor's discursive lecture wandered through six different topics without a point.

memoryHook.text: Cursive - a speech wandering off topic, looping all over the place just like cursive handwriting.

quizSentence: The professor's discursive lecture rambled through five different unrelated topics.
alternateExampleSentence: The author's discursive writing style made the dense historical novel difficult to read.

etymology: Latin disdēns ‘feeling aversion,’ from dis- + dignārī ‘deem worthy.’

senses.definition: To scorn, hold in low esteem
senses.exampleSentence: Older staff disdained the new hires, who were young and highly capable.
After learning of his immoral actions, Justine held Lawrence in disdain.

memoryHook.text: This stain - looking with absolute snobby disgust at "this stain" on your brand new shirt.

exampleSentence: The aristocratic lady looked at the poorly dressed peasant with disdain.
quizSentence: The wealthy aristocrat looked with disdain at the filthy beggar.
alternateExampleSentence: The veteran programmer viewed the sloppy, untested code with obvious professional disdain.

etymology: dis- + gruntled (obsolete ‘content’); playful formation modeled on earlier senses.
exampleSentence: The disgruntled customer loudly demanded to speak to the restaurant's general manager.

memoryHook.text: Grunt - an angry, dissatisfied worker walking away just grunting under his breath.

quizSentence: A group of disgruntled employees organized a strike to protest the pay cuts.
alternateExampleSentence: A disgruntled former employee is suspected of intentionally leaking the confidential data.

etymology: dis- + hearten; meaning loss of courage.
exampleSentence: The marathoner felt disheartened when a severe knee injury forced him to quit.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (remove) + "heart" = having your courage or spirit entirely taken away.

quizSentence: She felt disheartened after receiving yet another rejection letter from a publisher.
alternateExampleSentence: The team was disheartened by the devastating loss in the final championship game.

etymology: Old French desparagier ‘marry beneath rank,’ hence degrade.
exampleSentence: The bitter rival took every opportunity to disparage her colleague's hard work.

memoryHook.text: Spar - to verbally spar with someone just to belittle and insult their worth.

quizSentence: It is unprofessional to disparage your former employer during a job interview.
alternateExampleSentence: It is poor sportsmanship to disparage the opposing team after a victory.

etymology: Latin disparātus ‘separated,’ from dis- + parāre ‘prepare as distinct.’
exampleSentence: The urban planner merged disparate architectural styles into one cohesive city block.

memoryHook.text: Separate - two things that are so fundamentally different they must be kept totally separate.

quizSentence: The support group brought together people from disparate walks of life.
alternateExampleSentence: The tech team consisted of individuals from disparate professional backgrounds and cultures.

definition: To send off to a destination
etymology: Italian dispacciare or Spanish despachar ‘send off hastily.’
exampleSentence: The local police chief ordered the dispatch of three patrol cars tonight.
quizSentence: The general ordered the immediate dispatch of medical supplies to the front lines.
alternateExampleSentence: The experienced chef handled the busy dinner rush with dispatch and precision.

etymology: Latin dispellere ‘drive apart,’ from dis- + pellere ‘drive.’
exampleSentence: The CEO held a sudden meeting to dispel the rumors of impending layoffs.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "pel" (drive) = to drive away a rumor or bad feeling.

quizSentence: The CEO held a meeting to dispel the rumors about an upcoming mass layoff.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant sunlight breaking through the clouds helped to dispel the morning fog.

definition: To distribute over a wide area
etymology: Latin dispersus ‘scattered,’ past participle of dispergere.
exampleSentence: The riot police used loud sirens and tear gas to disperse the crowd.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "spars" (scatter) = to scatter the crowd in different directions.

quizSentence: Police used tear gas to quickly disperse the angry mob blocking the street.
alternateExampleSentence: The wind began to slowly disperse the thick black smoke from the chimney.

etymology: French désréputation; Latin repute replaced by disrepute pattern.
exampleSentence: The prestigious university fell into disrepute following a major admissions cheating scandal.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (not) + "repute" (reputation) = held in extremely low esteem by the public.

quizSentence: The once-famous restaurant fell into disrepute after several terrible health inspections.
alternateExampleSentence: The doctor's controversial treatments brought severe disrepute to his private medical practice.

etymology: Middle French dissembler ‘not resemble,’ mask true intentions.
exampleSentence: The corrupt politician used confusing rhetoric to dissemble his true policy motives.

memoryHook.text: Disassemble - to disassemble your true face and hide behind a fake, deceptive one.

quizSentence: The guilty suspect tried to dissemble his true motives with a fake smile.
alternateExampleSentence: She tried to dissemble her profound disappointment when she lost the promotion.

etymology: Latin dissemināre ‘scatter seed,’ from dis- + semen ‘seed.’
exampleSentence: The goal of the campaign is to disseminate accurate vaccine information everywhere.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "semin" (seed) = to scatter information widely, just like a farmer planting seeds.

quizSentence: The internet makes it easy to disseminate information across the globe.
alternateExampleSentence: The rebel group used underground printing presses to disseminate their revolutionary pamphlets.

etymology: Latin dissentīre ‘feel differently,’ from dis- + sentīre ‘feel.’

senses.exampleSentence: The principal argued that the child should repeat the fourth grade, but the unhappy parents dissented.
The last juror voiced dissent, unconvinced the defendant was guilty.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "sent" (feel) = to feel apart from the group; to officially disagree.

exampleSentence: The oppressive government violently crushed any public displays of political dissent.
quizSentence: Two justices wrote a strong opinion to express their dissent from the court's ruling.
alternateExampleSentence: Three brave judges wrote a formal dissent against the controversial court ruling.

etymology: Latin dissipāre ‘scatter,’ from dis- + supāre ‘to throw.’

senses.exampleSentence: The sun finally came out and dissipated the haze.
She dissipated her fortune on a series of bad investments.

memoryHook.text: Sip it - to sip your drink so slowly over hours that the ice entirely melts and dissipates.

exampleSentence: The intense anger she felt began to dissipate after a genuine apology.
quizSentence: The heavy morning fog began to dissipate as the hot sun finally rose.
alternateExampleSentence: The morning sun caused the heavy dew on the grass to dissipate.

etymology: Latin dissonantia ‘disagreement in sound,’ from dis- + sonāre ‘sound.’
exampleSentence: The cognitive dissonance between his stated morals and his actions was jarring.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "son" (sound) = musical notes that clash aggressively and don't belong together.

quizSentence: The sudden musical dissonance in the jazz piece startled the relaxed audience.
alternateExampleSentence: The musical composition relied heavily on acoustic dissonance to unsettle the audience.

etymology: Latin dissuādēre ‘advise against,’ from dis- + suādēre ‘urge.’
exampleSentence: The severe blizzard warnings were enough to dissuade the hikers from climbing.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (against) + "suadere" (persuade) = to heavily persuade someone not to do something.

quizSentence: Concerned advisers failed to dissuade the executive from pursuing the risky acquisition.
alternateExampleSentence: No logical reasoning could dissuade him from making the foolish financial investment.

definition: To swell due to internal pressure
etymology: Latin distendere ‘stretch apart,’ from dis- + tendere ‘stretch.’
exampleSentence: The severe malnutrition caused the young child's stomach to painfully distend.

memoryHook.text: "dis-" (apart) + "tend" (stretch) = to stretch outwards, usually used for medical swelling.

quizSentence: Severe malnutrition caused the starving child's belly to painfully distend.
alternateExampleSentence: The balloon began to dangerously distend as he forced more helium inside.

etymology: Middle English didderen ‘tremble’; modern sense of indecision.
exampleSentence: The committee continued to dither over minor details while the crisis worsened.

memoryHook.text: Dizzy - spinning around dizzy, completely paralyzed and unable to make a decision.

quizSentence: Stop trying to dither over the dinner menu and just pick a restaurant.
alternateExampleSentence: If you dither over which college to attend, you might miss the deadline.

etymology: Latin dīvīnus ‘of a god,’ from deus ‘god.’ Multiple senses converged in English.
exampleSentence: The ancient priests claimed they could divine the future by reading stars.

memoryHook.text: Vine - predicting the future by reading the tangled growth of a magic vine.

quizSentence: The ancient oracle claimed she could divine the future from the flight of birds.
alternateExampleSentence: The homemade gluten-free sourdough bread tasted absolutely divine fresh out of the oven.

etymology: Latin dīvīs-, past stem of dīvidere ‘divide.’
exampleSentence: The controversial tax policy proved to be a divisive issue among the voters.
quizSentence: The proposal to raise property taxes proved to be a divisive issue.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager's aggressive leadership style was divisive among the frustrated office staff.

etymology: Latin dīvulgāre ‘publish widely,’ from dis- + vulgus ‘common people.’
exampleSentence: The captured soldier refused to divulge the location of the hidden rebel base.

memoryHook.text: Bulge - the secret is bulging out of your pockets until you finally crack and reveal it.

quizSentence: The journalist refused to divulge the identity of his secret government source.
alternateExampleSentence: The doctor cannot legally divulge your private medical history to the public.

definition: Ready to accept control or instruction
etymology: Latin docilis ‘teachable,’ from docēre ‘teach.’
exampleSentence: The normally aggressive guard dog became docile when the professional trainer arrived.

memoryHook.text: "doc" (teach) = easily taught and completely submissive to handling.

quizSentence: The normally wild horse became docile after months of gentle training.
alternateExampleSentence: The exotic wild horse was far too spirited to ever become docile.

etymology: Greek dogmatikos ‘opinionated,’ from dogma ‘opinion, decree.’
exampleSentence: The cult leader's dogmatic teachings demanded absolute obedience from all his followers.

memoryHook.text: Dog - a dog guarding its bone is stubbornly attached to it, just like someone stubbornly attached to their unproven opinion.

quizSentence: The dogmatic leader refused to consider any opinions that contradicted his own.
alternateExampleSentence: The professor was so dogmatic about his theories that he ignored new evidence.

etymology: Latin dormīre ‘sleep,’ present participle dormiens.
exampleSentence: Bears spend the freezing winter months resting inside caves in a dormant state.

memoryHook.text: "dorm" (sleep) = currently sleeping or temporarily inactive (like a volcano).

quizSentence: The volcano lay dormant for three hundred years before suddenly erupting again.
alternateExampleSentence: The bear retreated to its dark cave to remain dormant throughout winter.

etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ influenced Gaelic and Middle English senses.
exampleSentence: The old headmaster possessed a dour expression that silenced the noisy classroom.

memoryHook.text: Sour - having a sour, gloomy, and extremely severe facial expression.

quizSentence: The dour librarian scowled at anyone who dared to whisper in the reading room.
alternateExampleSentence: The persistent, dour weather in London ruined the family's planned summer vacation.

etymology: Latin dubius ‘wavering,’ from duo ‘two’ minds.
exampleSentence: The detective was dubious of the suspect's convenient alibi today.

memoryHook.text: "dub" (doubt) = a claim filled with hesitation and serious doubt.

quizSentence: The journalist remained dubious of the official's explanation, noting several factual inconsistencies.
alternateExampleSentence: The company's dubious accounting practices caught the attention of federal financial investigators.

etymology: Latin duplicitās ‘doubleness,’ from duplex ‘twofold.’
exampleSentence: The spy's duplicity allowed him to work simultaneously for two opposing governments.

memoryHook.text: "du" (two) + "plic" (fold) = being two-faced or highly deceitful.

quizSentence: The spy's shocking duplicity was revealed when they found his hidden radio transmitter.
alternateExampleSentence: The angry voters were shocked by the blatant duplicity of the corrupt councilman.

etymology: Latin dūrus ‘hard’ + neuter abstract formation via Old French duress.
exampleSentence: The legal confession was thrown out because it was obtained under police duress.

memoryHook.text: Stress - being put under extreme physical stress or threats to force a confession.

quizSentence: He only signed the confession under extreme duress after hours of interrogation.
alternateExampleSentence: The bank teller handed over the cash entirely under the extreme duress of robbery.

etymology: Greek dunamikos ‘powerful,’ from dunamis ‘power.’
exampleSentence: The software engineer created a dynamic interface that adapted to different screens.
quizSentence: The dynamic speaker captivated the audience with her incredible energy and passion.
alternateExampleSentence: The two founders shared a dynamic relationship that propelled their tech startup.

etymology: Latin ēbullīre ‘bubble out,’ from e- + bullīre ‘boil.’
exampleSentence: The ebullient fans poured into the streets to celebrate their championship victory.

memoryHook.text: From Latin ebullire (to boil over). You are so enthusiastic and joyful that your energy literally boils over the pot.

quizSentence: The ebullient crowd cheered wildly as the home team won the championship game.
alternateExampleSentence: She was ebullient when she received the acceptance letter from her dream university.

definition: Deriving from a broad, diverse range
etymology: Greek eklektikos ‘selective,’ from eklegein ‘pick out.’
exampleSentence: The hipster coffee shop featured an eclectic mix of vintage and modern furniture.

memoryHook.text: Elect - electing to take the best individual pieces from a wide variety of different styles.

quizSentence: Her eclectic taste in music ranges from classical opera to heavy metal.
alternateExampleSentence: His eclectic taste in music ranged from heavy metal to underground jazz.

etymology: Greek ekstasis ‘standing outside oneself,’ intense displacement.
exampleSentence: The parents were ecstatic when their infant son finally slept through the night.
quizSentence: She was ecstatic when she found out she got into her dream college.
alternateExampleSentence: The young actress was ecstatic to land the lead role in the production.

etymology: Latin ēdictum ‘thing proclaimed,’ neuter past participle of ēdīcere.
exampleSentence: The monarch issued a royal edict banning all forms of public assembly.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "dict" (speak) = an official, undeniable order spoken out by a king or authority.

quizSentence: The monarch issued a harsh edict banning all public gatherings.
alternateExampleSentence: The principal's new edict prohibited the use of mobile phones on school campus.

etymology: French effacer ‘literally smooth face away,’ from Latin ex- + facies.
exampleSentence: The harsh desert winds will eventually efface the ancient carvings on the wall.

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "face" = to completely wipe out or erase the face of a monument.

quizSentence: Time and weather will eventually efface the carvings on the ancient stone monument.
alternateExampleSentence: He tried desperately to efface the terrible memories of the tragic car accident.

etymology: Latin effervēscere ‘begin to boil,’ from ex- + fervēre ‘boil.’
exampleSentence: The effervescent young host charmed every single guest at the large dinner party.

memoryHook.text: From Latin effervescere (to begin to boil). Used literally for fizzy drinks, and figuratively for bubbly, highly enthusiastic personalities.

quizSentence: Champagne is an effervescent beverage perfect for celebrating special occasions.
alternateExampleSentence: She poured the effervescent sparkling water over ice and added a fresh lemon.

etymology: Latin efficāx ‘effect-producing,’ from efficere ‘accomplish.’
exampleSentence: The new marketing strategy proved efficacious in driving premium subscription app conversions.

memoryHook.text: Effective - highly successful in producing the exact desired result.

quizSentence: The new vaccine proved to be highly efficacious in preventing the spread of the virus.
alternateExampleSentence: The experimental medical treatment was efficacious at halting the spread of the virus.

etymology: French effronterie ‘shamelessness,’ based on effronté ‘barefaced.’
exampleSentence: The arrogant intern had the effrontery to interrupt the CEO during the meeting.

memoryHook.text: Front - having the brazen, arrogant nerve to get right up in someone's "front" (face).

quizSentence: He had the unbelievable effrontery to ask for a raise after being caught sleeping.
alternateExampleSentence: The contractor's effrontery in demanding payment for work that failed inspection shocked the client.

etymology: Latin effulgēre ‘shine forth,’ from ex- + fulgēre ‘shine.’
exampleSentence: The bride looked stunning in her effulgent white dress as she walked down.

memoryHook.text: Full gem - shining radiantly and brightly like a flawless, full gem.

quizSentence: The effulgent morning sun peeked beautifully over the horizon, lighting up the valley.
alternateExampleSentence: The effulgent morning sun brilliantly illuminated the dew resting on the green meadow.

etymology: Latin ēgregius ‘standing out from the flock,’ from ex- + grex ‘herd.’
exampleSentence: The accountant made an egregious mathematical error that cost the corporation millions.

memoryHook.text: From Latin ex (out) + grege (flock). Standing out from the rest of the flock in a shockingly bad or horrific way.

quizSentence: Spelling the CEO's name wrong on the presentation was an egregious error.
alternateExampleSentence: The referee's egregious penalty call arguably cost the home team the championship.

etymology: Latin ēlabōrāre ‘work out,’ later senses of ornament and complexity.
exampleSentence: The elaborate security system featured retinal scanners, laser grids, and armed guards.
quizSentence: The thieves planned an elaborate heist involving lasers and grappling hooks.
alternateExampleSentence: The suspect concocted an elaborate, winding lie to explain his sudden disappearance.

etymology: Latin ēlātus ‘lifted up,’ past participle of efferre ‘carry out.’
exampleSentence: The software developer was elated when his new application hit the top charts.
quizSentence: The exhausted runner was elated to finally cross the marathon finish line.
alternateExampleSentence: The exhausted marathoner was elated to cross the finish line under his goal time.

etymology: Greek elegeia ‘mourning verse,’ often sung with flute accompaniment.
exampleSentence: The brilliant poet wrote a moving elegy to honor his late younger brother.

memoryHook.text: Allergy - writing a beautiful, sad poem for a friend who tragically died from a peanut allergy.

quizSentence: The poet recited a beautiful elegy at the funeral of his closest friend.
alternateExampleSentence: The somber cello performance served as a beautiful elegy for the disaster victims.

etymology: Latin ēlicere ‘draw out,’ from e- + lacere ‘entice.’
exampleSentence: The comedian's sharp jokes failed to elicit even a chuckle from the crowd.

memoryHook.text: Illegal - the detective trying to draw out a confession from someone who did something illegal.

quizSentence: The comedian's sharp jokes failed to elicit even a chuckle from the tough crowd.
alternateExampleSentence: The skilled detective used psychological tactics to elicit a full confession from him.

etymology: Latin ēloquēns ‘speaking out,’ from ēloquī ‘speak out.’
exampleSentence: The eloquent speaker moved the audience to tears with her powerful graduation speech.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "loqu" (speak) = speaking out in a beautifully fluent, persuasive manner.

quizSentence: The civil rights leader delivered an eloquent speech that moved everyone to tears.
alternateExampleSentence: Her eloquent and beautifully written essay earned her the top academic prize today.

etymology: Late Latin ēlūcidāre ‘make clear,’ from ē- + lūcidus ‘bright.’
exampleSentence: The professor used a simple analogy to elucidate the highly complex quantum theory.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "luc" (light) = to shine a spotlight on a complex topic to make it clear.

quizSentence: The professor used a simple diagram to elucidate the complex physics theorem.
alternateExampleSentence: The confusing instruction manual did nothing to elucidate the complex assembly process.

etymology: Latin ēlūdere ‘play away from,’ mock or slip past.
exampleSentence: The clever bank robber managed to elude the police for several long months.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "ludere" (play) = to play a clever trick to escape the guards.

quizSentence: The cunning fox managed to elude the hunting dogs by running through a stream.
alternateExampleSentence: The correct answer to the difficult math problem continues to elude the students.

etymology: Latin ēmaciāre ‘make lean,’ from ex- + maciēs ‘leanness.’
exampleSentence: The rescued stray dog was emaciated after surviving weeks without proper dog food.

memoryHook.text: Mac (Macaroni) - someone who is so starving and bone-thin they desperately need a giant bowl of Mac and Cheese.

quizSentence: The rescued dog was horribly emaciated after surviving on the streets for weeks.
alternateExampleSentence: The prisoners returned home severely emaciated from the terrible conditions of the camp.

etymology: Old French embellir ‘make beautiful,’ from bel ‘beautiful.’

senses.exampleSentence: My mom embellished the living room by adding lace curtains.
When Harry said he had done stuff on vacation, I asked him to embellish.

memoryHook.text: Bell - decorating a plain, boring Christmas tree by hanging shiny bells all over it.

exampleSentence: The fisherman would always embellish the actual size of the fish he caught.
quizSentence: The candidate embellished a minor internship into a senior leadership role on the résumé.
alternateExampleSentence: She decided to embellish her resume to hide her severe lack of experience.

etymology: Anglo-French embeseiller ‘destroy, melt,’ shifted to secret theft.
exampleSentence: The greedy accountant devised a scheme to embezzle thousands from the corporate pension.

memoryHook.text: Bedazzle - the corrupt accountant stole company funds just to bedazzle her own wardrobe.

quizSentence: The accountant used a complex scheme to embezzle millions from the corporate fund.
alternateExampleSentence: He was arrested after attempting to embezzle money from the local charity organization.

definition: To make corrections to a text
etymology: Latin ēmendāre ‘free from faults,’ from ex- + mendum ‘fault.’
exampleSentence: The editor asked the author to emend the confusing paragraphs in the manuscript.

memoryHook.text: Amend - to make precise corrections and improvements to a written text.

quizSentence: The editor had to carefully emend the sloppy manuscript before sending it to print.
alternateExampleSentence: You must quickly emend the severe grammatical errors before submitting your final essay.

etymology: Latin ēminēre ‘stand out,’ from ex- + mineō ‘project.’

senses.exampleSentence: The eminent scholar's lecture drew professors from across the campus.
There is an eminent stain on that shirt.

memoryHook.text: Eminem - a highly respected, famous, and prominent figure in their field.

definition: Famous and respected within a profession
exampleSentence: The eminent scientist won a prestigious award for her groundbreaking work in biology.
quizSentence: The university invited an eminent scientist to deliver the keynote graduation address.
alternateExampleSentence: An eminent historian will deliver a keynote speech at the university this evening.

definition: Having the quality of softening skin
etymology: Latin ēmollīre ‘soften,’ from ex- + mollis ‘soft.’
exampleSentence: The dermatologist prescribed a soothing emollient to treat the severe rash on her arm.

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "mollire" (soften) = a lotion designed to completely soften the skin.

quizSentence: Apply this thick emollient lotion to soothe your terribly dry, cracked winter skin.
alternateExampleSentence: She applied a rich emollient to protect her dry hands from the winter cold.

etymology: Back-formation from emotion on pattern of verbs like vote.
exampleSentence: The theater director urged the young actor to emote more during the tragic scene.
quizSentence: Stage actors must learn to emote grandly so the back row can feel their performance.
alternateExampleSentence: It is difficult for him to emote and share his true feelings with others.

definition: The ability to share another's feelings
etymology: Greek empatheia ‘passion,’ coined from em- + pathos ‘feeling.’
exampleSentence: The nurse showed great empathy toward the frightened child in the hospital room.

memoryHook.text: "em-" (in) + "path" (feeling) = physically feeling into someone else's emotions.

quizSentence: A good nurse shows deep empathy for patients dealing with chronic pain.
alternateExampleSentence: Reading classic literature helps to build deep empathy for people from diverse backgrounds.

etymology: Greek empeirikos ‘experienced,’ from empeiria ‘experience.’

senses.exampleSentence: The study rests on empirical data gathered from controlled field trials.

senses.definition: Capable of being proved or disproved by experiment
senses.exampleSentence: That cats hate water is empirical; I could test it on mine.

memoryHook.text: Empire - the Roman Empire was built on practical, observable facts and engineering, not just philosophical theories.

exampleSentence: The researcher gathered empirical data to definitively prove his controversial scientific theory.
quizSentence: The scientist demanded empirical evidence rather than relying on philosophical theories.
alternateExampleSentence: Without solid empirical evidence, your argument relies entirely on speculation and personal bias.

definition: To match or surpass a person
etymology: Latin aemulārī ‘strive to equal,’ from aemulus ‘rivaling.’
exampleSentence: The young athlete tried to emulate the perfect shooting form of her favorite player.

memoryHook.text: Emulator - running a video game emulator on your PC to perfectly imitate an old console.

quizSentence: Young basketball players constantly try to emulate Michael Jordan's iconic fadeaway jumper.
alternateExampleSentence: Many struggling startups attempt to emulate the successful business model of giant tech companies.

etymology: Old French enamourer ‘inspire with love,’ from en- + amour.
exampleSentence: The beautiful coastal town will quickly enamor any tourist who visits during the summer.

memoryHook.text: "en-" (in) + "amor" (love) = to be entirely filled with a feeling of love and fascination.

quizSentence: The charming coastal town managed to enamor the visiting tourists.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant musician managed to enamor the entire audience with her stunning piano performance.

definition: A repeated performance demanded by audience
etymology: French encore ‘still, again,’ shouted for another round.
exampleSentence: The cheering crowd loudly demanded an encore after the band finished their final song.
quizSentence: The cheering concert crowd demanded an encore after the band played their final song.
alternateExampleSentence: The exhausted opera singer returned to the stage to perform a brief vocal encore.

etymology: Old French encombrer ‘block up,’ from Medieval Latin incombrāre.
exampleSentence: The heavy backpack will certainly encumber the hikers during their long mountain ascent.

memoryHook.text: Cucumber - trying to run a marathon while carrying a 50-pound sack of cucumbers is a massive burden.

quizSentence: Do not encumber yourself with heavy luggage on our long walking tour.
alternateExampleSentence: Strict government regulations continue to encumber the rapid growth of the small local business.

etymology: Latin ēnervāre ‘cut sinews,’ metaphorically drain strength.
exampleSentence: The blazing summer heat will quickly enervate even the most experienced marathon runner today.

memoryHook.text: Energy out - a disease that completely drains the energy out of your body.

quizSentence: The blistering desert heat will quickly enervate even the most experienced hiker.
alternateExampleSentence: A severe bout of the flu can enervate a patient for several long weeks.

definition: To give the right to vote
etymology: Old French enfranchir ‘set free,’ from franc ‘free.’
exampleSentence: The new federal law seeks to enfranchise minority voters in rural agricultural communities.

memoryHook.text: Franchise - giving a citizen the keys to the "franchise," meaning granting them the legal right to vote.

quizSentence: The historical amendment was passed to enfranchise millions of women across the country.
alternateExampleSentence: The historic amendment was passed to finally enfranchise women across the entire nation.

definition: To cause or give rise to
etymology: Old French engendrer ‘beget,’ Latin ingenerāre ‘plant in.’
exampleSentence: The harsh new company policies will only engender deep resentment among the loyal staff.

memoryHook.text: "en-" (in) + "gen" (birth) = to give birth to a situation or a feeling of trust.

quizSentence: The strict new dress code will only engender deep resentment among the student body.
alternateExampleSentence: Honest communication helps to engender trust between business partners during difficult financial negotiations.

etymology: Greek ainissesthai ‘speak in riddles,’ related to ainigma ‘riddle.’
exampleSentence: The enigmatic stranger refused to share his real name or his mysterious background.

memoryHook.text: Enigma machine - highly mysterious and impossible to crack, just like the WWII code machine.

quizSentence: The enigmatic artist wore a mask and refused to ever speak to the press.
alternateExampleSentence: Scientists are still trying to understand the enigmatic behavior of the deep ocean creatures.

definition: The state of being actively opposed
etymology: Old French enemistié; Latin inimīcitia ‘unfriendliness.’
exampleSentence: There is a bitter, historical enmity between the two rival families in the village.

memoryHook.text: Enemy - the intense, deeply rooted hatred you feel toward a bitter enemy.

quizSentence: There is a bitter, centuries-old enmity between the two rival warring clans.
alternateExampleSentence: The bitter election campaign created deep enmity between the opposing political party members.

etymology: French ennui ‘annoyance, boredom,’ from Latin in odio ‘in hatred.’
exampleSentence: A deep sense of ennui settled over the teenagers during the rainy summer vacation.

memoryHook.text: Are we? - "Are we there yet?" asked the kid in the backseat, suffering from extreme, soul-crushing boredom.

quizSentence: The wealthy heir suffered from ennui, bored by his luxurious routine.
alternateExampleSentence: The wealthy heir suffered from terrible ennui because he never had to actually work.

definition: To involve as a necessary consequence
etymology: Anglo-Norman entailer ‘cut in,’ restricting inheritance; extended to logical consequence.
exampleSentence: The difficult software project will entail long hours of writing and debugging complex code.
quizSentence: Adopting a puppy will entail a serious commitment of your time and energy.
alternateExampleSentence: Please explain what duties this new management position will entail on a daily basis.

definition: To capture the fascinated attention of
exampleSentence: The brilliant magician managed to enthrall the audience with his stunning visual illusions.
etymology: Middle English *enthrallen*: *en-* ‘in’ + *thrall* ‘bondsman’; literally to hold in bondage, then to rivet attention.

memoryHook.text: Originally meant to put someone in "thrall" (slavery). Now it means to capture someone's attention so completely that they are a willing…

quizSentence: The acrobat's daring high-wire act managed to enthrall the entire circus audience.
alternateExampleSentence: The gripping mystery novel will enthrall readers from the first page to the last.

exampleSentence: The beauty of a blooming cherry blossom is beautiful but tragically ephemeral.
etymology: Greek *ephemeros* ‘lasting a day,’ from *epi-* ‘upon’ + *hemera* ‘day.’

memoryHook.text: From Greek ephemeros (lasting only one day). Originally used by biologists for short-lived insects like mayflies, now means anything…

quizSentence: The beauty of a blooming cherry blossom is sadly ephemeral, lasting only a few days.
alternateExampleSentence: The pop star enjoyed a brief and ephemeral period of global fame before fading.

definition: Relating to the writing of letters
exampleSentence: The classic novel is written in an epistolary format using letters between the characters.
etymology: Latin *epistolaris*, from Greek *epistolē* ‘letter, message.’

memoryHook.text: Pistol - writing a highly formal series of letters to threaten a duel with a pistol.

quizSentence: The classic novel Dracula is written in an epistolary format using diary entries and letters.
alternateExampleSentence: The historian discovered an epistolary exchange between the two famous European inventors.

definition: A perfect example of a type
exampleSentence: The luxury hotel is the epitome of modern elegance and superior customer service.
etymology: Greek *epitomē* ‘a cutting into’ (abridgment), hence a compact summary; extended to a perfect representative.

memoryHook.text: Epic tome - an epic, massive tome that serves as the perfect, flawless example of fantasy literature.

quizSentence: That luxurious penthouse suite is the epitome of high-end modern living.
alternateExampleSentence: Her flawless performance on the ice was the epitome of athletic grace and precision.

definition: Mental calmness in a difficult situation
exampleSentence: The seasoned pilot handled the sudden engine failure with surprising calm and equanimity.
etymology: Latin *aequanimitas*, from *aequus* ‘even’ + *animus* ‘mind.’

memoryHook.text: "equ" (equal) + "anim" (mind/spirit) = keeping an even, perfectly balanced mind under intense stress.

quizSentence: The pilot maintained complete equanimity even when the left engine caught on fire.
alternateExampleSentence: She faced the harsh criticism of her peers with remarkable dignity and equanimity.

definition: Open to more than one interpretation
exampleSentence: The politician gave an equivocal answer to avoid upsetting any registered voters today.
etymology: Late Latin *aequivocus*, from Latin *aequus* ‘equal’ + *vox* ‘voice’—two voices at once.

memoryHook.text: "equi" (equal) + "voc" (voice) = a politician speaking with two equal voices, making their stance completely ambiguous and unclear.

quizSentence: The politician gave an equivocal answer that dodged the reporter's question.
alternateExampleSentence: The scientific results remain equivocal and require much more testing to be proven.

exampleSentence: The erudite professor published three separate books regarding ancient Roman political history.
etymology: Latin *eruditus* ‘well instructed,’ from *e-* ‘out’ + *rudis* ‘raw, untrained.’

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "rud" (raw/rude) = trained completely out of a raw, ignorant state into profound scholarly knowledge.

quizSentence: The erudite professor has read almost every single book in the university library.
alternateExampleSentence: She is an erudite scholar who reads classic literature in five different languages.

exampleSentence: The strict monk decided to eschew all worldly possessions and live in isolated poverty.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *eschiver*, akin to German *scheuen* ‘to shun, dread.’

memoryHook.text: Ah - choo!-you sneeze, and I deliberately avoid you to escape the germs.

quizSentence: A strict vegetarian chooses to entirely eschew all meat and animal products.
alternateExampleSentence: Many healthy athletes eschew processed foods and focus on eating fresh organic vegetables.

exampleSentence: The philosopher wrote an esoteric book that only a few scholars could actually comprehend.
etymology: Greek *esōterikos* ‘inner,’ comparative of *esō* ‘within’ (opposed to exoteric).

memoryHook.text: Isolated - knowledge that is so highly isolated that only a tiny group of experts actually understand it.

quizSentence: The professor's esoteric research focuses on the mating habits of a rare Amazonian beetle.
alternateExampleSentence: The software developer used esoteric programming languages that baffled the junior engineering team.

exampleSentence: The political candidate continues to espouse progressive policies regarding renewable energy resources.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *espouser* ‘to marry,’ from Latin *sponsus* ‘betrothed’; figuratively to take up a cause.

memoryHook.text: Spouse - to marry yourself to a political idea or belief, defending it just like you would a spouse.

quizSentence: The progressive candidate continues to espouse the importance of universal healthcare.
alternateExampleSentence: She used her popular blog to espouse the clear benefits of a vegan lifestyle.

exampleSentence: The delicate dancer moved across the stage with a beautiful, ethereal grace.
etymology: Latin *aetherius*, from Greek *aithēr* ‘upper bright air.’

memoryHook.text: "ether" (upper air) = light, airy, and belonging entirely to the heavens rather than earth.

quizSentence: The glowing aurora borealis creates an ethereal, ghostly light in the northern sky.
alternateExampleSentence: The morning mist gave the ancient forest a haunting and ethereal appearance.

exampleSentence: The linguistics professor specializes in the fascinating etymology of ancient Germanic languages.
etymology: Greek *etumologia*, from *etumon* ‘true sense’ + *logos* ‘account’—the study of a word’s true origin.
quizSentence: Studying the etymology of English words reveals fascinating connections to ancient Latin.
alternateExampleSentence: Understanding the etymology of a difficult word can help you remember its specific definition.

exampleSentence: The exhausted marathoner felt euphoric as he finally crossed the grueling race finish line.
etymology: Greek *euphoria* ‘bearing well,’ from *eu-* ‘well’ + *pherein* ‘to bear.’

memoryHook.text: "eu-" (good) + "pherein" (bear) = bearing a feeling of intense, overwhelmingly good happiness.

quizSentence: The winning team felt euphoric as they lifted the championship trophy.
alternateExampleSentence: The fans were euphoric when their underdog team won the national soccer championship.

definition: Quickly fading from sight or memory
exampleSentence: The evanescent rainbow disappeared from the sky just minutes after the summer storm ended.
etymology: Latin *evanescere* ‘to vanish,’ from *e-* ‘out’ + *vanus* ‘empty.’

memoryHook.text: "e-" (out) + "vanescere" (vanish) = fading rapidly out of sight; vanishing like vapor.

quizSentence: The evanescent rainbow disappeared from the sky just minutes after the storm ended.
alternateExampleSentence: Childhood is an evanescent period of innocence that fades far too quickly.

exampleSentence: His nervous facial expressions began to evince a deep sense of internal guilt.
etymology: Latin *evincere* ‘to conquer completely,’ then ‘to prove’; from *e-* ‘out’ + *vincere* ‘conquer.’

memoryHook.text: Evidence - to clearly show evidence or proof of a hidden feeling.

quizSentence: His constantly shaking hands evince a severe level of underlying nervous anxiety.
alternateExampleSentence: The candidate's flawless debate performance will evince her readiness for the presidential office.

definition: To make a bad situation worse
exampleSentence: Scratching the bug bite will only exacerbate the severe itching and cause an infection.
etymology: Latin *exacerbare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *acerbus* ‘harsh, bitter.’

memoryHook.text: Extra bad - making a bad situation extra bad by interfering.

quizSentence: Scratching that terrible mosquito bite will only exacerbate the itching and swelling.
alternateExampleSentence: The new tax policy will exacerbate the growing economic divide between the social classes.

exampleSentence: The citizens chose to exalt the heroic firefighter who saved three children from danger.
etymology: Latin *exaltare* ‘to raise high,’ from *ex-* ‘up’ + *altus* ‘high.’

memoryHook.text: Exhale - exhaling in pure awe as you praise and elevate a hero to a high rank.

quizSentence: The cult members exalt their charismatic leader as if he were a living god.
alternateExampleSentence: The epic poem was written to exalt the brave deeds of the ancient warrior.

exampleSentence: The toddler's constant screaming began to exasperate the exhausted passengers on the long flight.
etymology: Latin *exasperare* ‘to roughen,’ from *ex-* + *asper* ‘rough.’

memoryHook.text: Ex-aspirin - you have a splitting headache and realize you are out of aspirin, which thoroughly annoys and frustrates you.

quizSentence: The toddler's constant screaming began to seriously exasperate the exhausted babysitter.
alternateExampleSentence: Failing the same driving test three times would exasperate any eager teenager.

definition: To make a hole by digging
exampleSentence: The archaeologists plan to excavate the ancient ruins hidden beneath the desert sand.
etymology: Latin *excavare*, from *ex-* ‘out’ + *cavus* ‘hollow.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "cav" (cave) = to hollow out a cave or systematically dig historical artifacts out of the ground.

quizSentence: Archeologists plan to carefully excavate the ruins of the ancient Roman villa.
alternateExampleSentence: The construction crew had to excavate the entire lot before pouring the concrete foundation.

exampleSentence: The new DNA evidence will exculpate the innocent man after years in prison.
etymology: Latin *exculpare*, from *ex-* ‘from’ + *culpa* ‘blame.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "culp" (blame) = to officially take the blame off someone's record.

quizSentence: Newly discovered security footage exculpated the defendant from all charges related to the robbery.
alternateExampleSentence: The security footage served to exculpate the accused employee of any financial wrongdoing.

exampleSentence: The science class took a fun weekend excursion to the local natural history museum.
etymology: Latin *excursio* ‘a running out,’ from *excurrere* ‘to run forth.’
quizSentence: The school arranged a fun educational excursion to the natural history museum.
alternateExampleSentence: A brief excursion into the archive yielded a letter that transformed the entire investigation.

exampleSentence: The restaurant was closed down after health inspectors discovered execrable conditions in the kitchen.
etymology: Latin *execrabilis*, from *execrari* ‘to curse,’ *ex-* + *sacer* ‘sacred’ (invoke a curse).

memoryHook.text: Excrement - a movie so utterly foul and detestable it belongs in the sewer with the excrement.

quizSentence: The harsh critic called the new blockbuster film an execrable piece of trash.
alternateExampleSentence: The critic described the boring action movie as an execrable waste of cinematic time.

etymology: Medieval Latin exemplificare, from Latin exemplum ('example') + facere ('make')
definition: To be a typical example of
exampleSentence: Her brilliant academic achievements exemplify the high standards of the prestigious university.

memoryHook.text: Example + -ify = to make into an example.

quizSentence: Her career exemplifies the value of persistent, evidence-based advocacy.
alternateExampleSentence: The brutal dictator's terrible actions exemplify the worst traits of human nature.

exampleSentence: The coach used an inspiring speech to exhort his tired players to ultimate victory.
etymology: Latin *exhortari* ‘to encourage strongly,’ from *ex-* + *hortari* ‘to urge.’

memoryHook.text: Exhaust - cheering and urging your team on so intensely from the sidelines that you exhaust yourself.

quizSentence: The coach loudly exhorted his tired players to give maximum effort in the fourth quarter.
alternateExampleSentence: The governor will exhort the citizens to remain calm during the incoming severe hurricane.

exampleSentence: The exigent medical crisis required the doctor to perform emergency surgery immediately.
etymology: Latin *exigens*, present participle of *exigere* ‘to demand,’ literally ‘drive out.’

memoryHook.text: Exit gent - the building is currently on fire, so it is highly urgent and demanding that the gentleman exits immediately.

quizSentence: The rapidly spreading wildfire created an exigent situation requiring an immediate evacuation.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager handled the exigent situation with surprising calm and decisive professional action.

definition: To absolve from a criminal charge
exampleSentence: The judge will exonerate the defendant after the real thief confesses to the crime.
etymology: Latin *exonerare* ‘to unload,’ from *ex-* + *onus* ‘burden’—remove the burden of guilt.

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "onus" (burden) = taking the heavy burden of guilt permanently off someone's shoulders.

quizSentence: The DNA evidence was enough to exonerate the wrongfully convicted man.
alternateExampleSentence: The formal investigation will exonerate the police officer of any alleged serious misconduct.

exampleSentence: The luxury hotel charged an exorbitant fee for a simple bottle of drinking water.
etymology: Late Latin *exorbitans* ‘going off track,’ from *ex-* + *orbita* ‘wheel track.’

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "orbit" = a price tag so insanely high it has flown completely out of orbit.

quizSentence: The luxury hotel charged an exorbitant fee for a simple bottle of sparkling water.
alternateExampleSentence: Many young students struggle to pay the exorbitant tuition costs of private medical schools.

exampleSentence: The manager found it expedient to simply fire the lazy worker without any warning.
etymology: Latin *expedire* ‘to free the feet,’ from *ex-* + *pes* ‘foot’—set free, dispatch, then advantageous.

memoryHook.text: "ex-" (out) + "ped" (foot) = originally meaning to free a foot from a trap; doing what is most convenient and practical to solve a problem…

quizSentence: He chose the most expedient route to reach the hospital before visiting hours ended.
alternateExampleSentence: Settling the lawsuit out of court was an expedient way to save legal fees.

definition: To atone for guilt or sin
exampleSentence: He volunteered at the homeless shelter to expiate his past criminal behavior.
etymology: Latin *expiare*, from *ex-* ‘thoroughly’ + *piare* ‘to appease’ (*pius* ‘pious’).

memoryHook.text: Expiration - the pirate must do good deeds to make amends for his crimes before his expiration date.

quizSentence: He donated half his wealth to charity to expiate his past corporate misconduct.
alternateExampleSentence: He volunteered at the homeless shelter to expiate his past criminal transgressions.

etymology: Latin explicitus, past participle of explicare ('unfold')
exampleSentence: The teacher gave explicit instructions on how to format the final research paper.

memoryHook.text: Explicit = fully unfolded and spelled out, not hidden.

quizSentence: The author was explicit about which sources she relied on and which she rejected.
alternateExampleSentence: The violent movie received an explicit rating and was banned for younger audiences.

exampleSentence: The judge agreed to expunge the minor offense from the young man's permanent record.
etymology: Latin *expungere* ‘to prick out’ (cancel marks), from *ex-* + *pungere* ‘to prick.’

memoryHook.text: Sponge - using a massive sponge to completely wipe away and erase a criminal record.

quizSentence: The judge agreed to expunge the minor teenage offense from his permanent criminal record.
alternateExampleSentence: The dictator tried to expunge his political rival from all historical textbooks.

exampleSentence: The school board decided to expurgate the controversial novel before assigning it to students.
etymology: Latin *expurgare* ‘to cleanse,’ from *ex-* + *purgare* ‘purify.’

memoryHook.text: Purge - the school board deciding to purge a classic book of all its offensive content before assigning it.

quizSentence: The conservative school board decided to expurgate the classic novel before assigning it.
alternateExampleSentence: The publisher must expurgate the offensive language to make the magazine family friendly.

exampleSentence: There are only three extant copies of the rare manuscript remaining in the world.
etymology: Latin *extans*, present participle of *exstare* ‘to stand out, exist,’ from *ex-* + *stare* ‘stand.’

memoryHook.text: X-tent - the ancient explorer's tent still miraculously stands; it currently exists and survived the storm.

quizSentence: This beautiful illuminated manuscript is one of the oldest extant copies of the Bible.
alternateExampleSentence: The biologist studies the few extant species of the ancient prehistoric fish.

exampleSentence: The eager critics were quick to extol the brilliant performance of the young actor.
etymology: Latin *extollere* ‘to lift up,’ from *ex-* + *tollere* ‘raise.’

memoryHook.text: Toll - paying the toll bridge operator with highly enthusiastic praise instead of actual money.

quizSentence: The nutritionist continues to extol the health benefits of eating dark leafy greens.
alternateExampleSentence: Critics frequently extol the brilliant author for her vivid imagery and masterful storytelling.

exampleSentence: You must remove all extraneous information from your resume to keep it concise.
etymology: Latin *extraneus* ‘external,’ from *extra* ‘outside.’

memoryHook.text: "extra" (outside) = coming from the outside and therefore entirely irrelevant to the core subject.

quizSentence: Please remove all extraneous details from your report to keep it under two pages.
alternateExampleSentence: The detective ignored the extraneous details and focused entirely on the primary suspect.

etymology: Latin extra ('outside') + polare ('to polish'), via extrapolatio
definition: To estimate based on known trends
exampleSentence: The scientist can extrapolate the future climate trends based on current rising temperatures.

memoryHook.text: "Extra-" (beyond) + "polate" = push a pattern beyond the data you have.

quizSentence: From three years of scores, the committee extrapolated likely long-term improvement.
alternateExampleSentence: It is risky to extrapolate broad societal changes from a single isolated survey.

definition: To free from a difficult constraint
exampleSentence: The brave firefighters worked for hours to extricate the trapped driver from the vehicle.
etymology: Latin *extricare*, from *ex-* + *tricae* ‘troubles, hindrances.’

memoryHook.text: Trick - finally figuring out the puzzle's trick to free yourself from the trap.

quizSentence: Firefighters used the jaws of life to extricate the trapped driver from the crushed car.
alternateExampleSentence: He struggled to extricate himself from the complicated legal dispute with his business partner.

definition: To show or feel triumphant elation
exampleSentence: The victorious team began to exult loudly after winning the final championship game.
etymology: Latin *exultare* ‘to leap up,’ from *ex-* + *saltare* ‘to leap.’

memoryHook.text: Vault-pole - vaulting high into the air out of sheer triumph and joy after winning gold.

quizSentence: The crowd began to exult wildly when the home team scored the winning goal.
alternateExampleSentence: She will certainly exult when she opens the letter and sees her college acceptance.

exampleSentence: The desperate student attempted to fabricate a believable excuse for missing the final exam.
etymology: Latin *fabricare* ‘to construct,’ from *faber* ‘smith, maker.’
quizSentence: The dishonest employee tried to fabricate an elaborate excuse for missing the big meeting.
alternateExampleSentence: The corrupt journalist chose to fabricate quotes to make the news story more exciting.

etymology: Latin *facilis* ‘easy,’ from *facere* ‘to do’—done with ease, hence (disparagingly) too easy.

senses.exampleSentence: This game is so facile that even a four-yearold can master it.

senses.definition: Superficial, achieved with minimal thought or care, insincere
senses.exampleSentence: Any facile fix seemed too shallow to save a business so deep in debt.

memoryHook.text: "fac" (do/make) = an argument that is so easy to make that it is superficial and entirely lacks depth.

exampleSentence: Her facile piano performance lacked the emotional depth required for the classical masterpiece.
quizSentence: That complex geopolitical conflict requires more than a facile, one-sentence solution.
alternateExampleSentence: His facile argument collapsed the moment the experts presented real empirical data.

etymology: Latin facilitare, from facilis ('easy')
exampleSentence: The new software is designed to facilitate better communication between remote office workers.

memoryHook.text: Facile means easy — facilitate makes things easy to do.

quizSentence: The revised workflow facilitated faster review without sacrificing rigor.
alternateExampleSentence: The mediator helped facilitate a peaceful compromise between the two angry business partners.

exampleSentence: The jury rejected the fallacious logic presented by the desperate defense attorney.
etymology: Latin *fallaciosus*, from *fallere* ‘to deceive.’

memoryHook.text: "fall" (deceive) = based on a false, highly deceitful premise.

quizSentence: The advertisement relies on a fallacious claim that the pill will melt belly fat instantly.
alternateExampleSentence: The article was based on a fallacious assumption that ruined its scientific credibility.

exampleSentence: The fastidious editor demanded flawless grammar and perfect punctuation in every submitted manuscript.
etymology: Latin *fastidiosus* ‘disdainful,’ from *fastidium* ‘loathing, squeamishness.’

memoryHook.text: Fast tidier - obsessing over every tiny detail; nothing sloppy gets past.

quizSentence: The fastidious chef demands perfection and cleanliness in his busy kitchen.
alternateExampleSentence: A fastidious housekeeper will never tolerate even a single speck of visible dust.

definition: To fully understand after much thought
exampleSentence: He failed to fathom the complex theoretical physics concepts presented during the lecture.
etymology: Old English *fæthm* ‘span of the outstretched arms,’ used of measuring depth.

memoryHook.text: Originally a nautical unit of measurement for water depth (about 6 feet). To fathom a problem is to successfully reach the very bottom of…

quizSentence: Few outside observers could fathom the logistical complexity behind staging the international summit.
alternateExampleSentence: The young child could not fathom why his parents were moving to another city.

exampleSentence: The foolish prince made a fatuous remark that deeply offended the visiting ambassadors.
etymology: Latin *fatuus* ‘foolish,’ akin to *faeri* ‘to speak’ as babble.

memoryHook.text: Tattoo us - two drunk, incredibly silly friends shouting "Tattoo us!" at 3 AM; a foolish, pointless idea.

quizSentence: The arrogant politician made a fatuous comment that alienated his voters.
alternateExampleSentence: His fatuous behavior at the formal dinner embarrassed his entire wealthy family.

etymology: Old French faisable, from fais-, stem of faire ('to do')
exampleSentence: The engineer concluded that building the bridge over the canyon was not economically feasible.

memoryHook.text: Feasible = able to be done (think "do-able").

quizSentence: The plan is ambitious, yet still feasible if funding arrives on schedule.
alternateExampleSentence: Portable solar panels have made off-grid medical clinics feasible in remote rural regions.

exampleSentence: The fecund soil of the river valley produces abundant crops year after year.
etymology: Latin *fecundus* ‘fruitful,’ from *fe-/*fetus* ‘offspring.’

memoryHook.text: Feces - putting cow feces (manure) on the soil makes it highly fertile and incredibly productive.

quizSentence: The fecund river valley produces abundant harvests of wheat every year.
alternateExampleSentence: Her fecund imagination allowed her to write three brilliant fantasy novels in one year.

etymology: Latin *felix* ‘happy, fertile’ + *-ous*; suited to the occasion.

senses.exampleSentence: While his comments were idiotic and rambling, mine were felicitous and helpful.
I spent a felicitous afternoon visiting old friends.

memoryHook.text: Fleece - finding a warm fleece jacket in your trunk right as a blizzard hits is a highly fortunate, well-timed event.

exampleSentence: The speaker used a felicitous analogy that explained the complex scientific concept.
quizSentence: The speaker made a felicitous remark that captured the joyful mood of the wedding.
alternateExampleSentence: The sunny weather was a felicitous condition for the beautiful outdoor wedding ceremony.

exampleSentence: The feral cats in the alley survived by hunting mice and eating garbage.
etymology: Latin *fera* ‘wild beast,’ from *ferus* ‘wild.’

memoryHook.text: Fur all - a stray animal covered in fur all over, acting completely wild and untamed.

quizSentence: A pack of feral cats lives in the abandoned warehouse down by the old docks.
alternateExampleSentence: The abandoned dog reverted to a feral state after living in the dangerous woods.

exampleSentence: The fervent fans camped outside the stadium for three days to buy concert tickets.
etymology: Latin *fervens* ‘boiling,’ present participle of *fervere* ‘to boil, glow.’

memoryHook.text: Fever - having a burning, passionate fever for a political cause.

quizSentence: Fervent supporters stood in freezing rain just to glimpse the visiting president.
alternateExampleSentence: The civil rights leader delivered a fervent speech that deeply inspired the crowd.

exampleSentence: The fetid smell of rotting garbage filled the narrow alley behind the busy restaurant.
etymology: Latin *fetidus* ‘stinking,’ from *fetere* ‘to stink.’

memoryHook.text: Feet - a swamp smelling as horrific as a rotting pair of unwashed feet.

quizSentence: The stagnant swamp water emitted a fetid odor that made the brave explorers gag.
alternateExampleSentence: The explorers refused to drink the fetid water found in the stagnant jungle swamp.

exampleSentence: The prison guards placed a heavy iron fetter on the dangerous inmate's ankle.
etymology: Old English *feter* ‘chain,’ from Proto-Germanic *fetru*.

memoryHook.text: Feet - putting heavy iron chains on a prisoner's feet to severely restrict them.

quizSentence: The guards used heavy iron chains to fetter the prisoner to the stone wall.
alternateExampleSentence: Fear of failure can easily fetter a brilliant artist and prevent creative risks.

exampleSentence: The fickle weather changed from bright sunshine to a violent thunderstorm in twenty minutes.
etymology: Middle English *fikel* ‘deceitful,’ from Old English *ficel* ‘pertaining to fraud.’

memoryHook.text: Pickle - you can't decide if you want a sweet or a sour pickle because your tastes change constantly and unpredictably.

quizSentence: The fickle weather changed from bright sunshine to a freezing blizzard in under ten minutes.
alternateExampleSentence: The fickle consumers quickly abandoned the old smartphone for the shiny new model.

definition: Faithfulness to a person or cause
exampleSentence: The dog demonstrated fidelity to his owner by waiting by the hospital door.
etymology: Latin *fidelitas*, from *fides* ‘faith, trust.’

memoryHook.text: "fid" (trust/faith) = strict faithfulness and unwavering loyalty.

quizSentence: The loyal dog demonstrated fidelity by waiting by his master's grave for years.
alternateExampleSentence: The engineer praised the incredible audio fidelity of the expensive new speaker system.

exampleSentence: The poet frequently used figurative language to express deep sorrow and lingering regret.
etymology: Middle English from Late Latin *figurativus*, from Latin *figura* ‘shape, figure.’
quizSentence: The poet used figurative language to compare the roaring ocean to an angry, hungry beast.
alternateExampleSentence: He used the term in a figurative sense, not a literal one.

exampleSentence: The parents were flabbergasted when their lazy son brought home a perfect report card.
etymology: Colloquial 18th-c. British coinage; origin uncertain (perhaps playful blend).
quizSentence: The auditors were flabbergasted to discover three years of unrecorded transactions in the ledger.
alternateExampleSentence: The flabbergasted audience gasped as the magician suddenly vanished from the locked cage.

exampleSentence: The deflated balloon hung in a flaccid shape from the back of the chair.
etymology: Latin *flaccidus*, from *flaccus* ‘flabby, hanging loose.’

memoryHook.text: Flat - a tire that is completely flat, limp, and entirely lacking firmness.

quizSentence: The flaccid balloon slowly drifted to the floor after the loud birthday party finally ended.
alternateExampleSentence: His flaccid handshake gave the interviewing manager a poor first impression of him.

exampleSentence: The player committed a flagrant foul and was immediately ejected from the basketball game.
etymology: Latin *flagrans* ‘blazing,’ present participle of *flagrare* ‘to burn.’

memoryHook.text: Flag - a foul on the basketball court so obviously bad and intentional the referee throws a giant penalty flag.

quizSentence: Tripping the opposing player on purpose was a flagrant violation of the sportsmanship rules.
alternateExampleSentence: The corrupt CEO showed a flagrant disregard for the safety of his factory workers.

definition: Having a red or flushed complexion
exampleSentence: The architect despised the florid decorations covering the walls of the Victorian mansion.
etymology: Latin *floridus* ‘flowery,’ from *flos* ‘flower.’

memoryHook.text: "flor" (flower) = writing that is heavily decorated and excessively flowery.

quizSentence: The Victorian mansion was decorated with florid, overly complicated gold wallpaper.
alternateExampleSentence: The embarrassed teenager spoke with a florid face when the teacher called on him.

exampleSentence: The reckless driver continued to flout the speed limit despite receiving multiple expensive tickets.
etymology: Perhaps from Middle Dutch *fluiten* ‘to whistle at’ in mockery; influenced by *flout* ‘jest.’

memoryHook.text: Fly out - to openly mock and break the rules, then fly out of the country to avoid arrest.

quizSentence: The rebellious teenager loved to flout the strict school dress code by wearing bright colors.
alternateExampleSentence: Some large corporations blatantly flout environmental regulations to save money on proper waste disposal.

exampleSentence: The clever detective managed to foil the villain's devious plot to steal the diamonds.
etymology: From obsolete *foil* ‘defeat’ (as in hunting), perhaps from *full* ‘to tread’; distinct from metal *foil.*

senses.exampleSentence: The clever detective managed to foil the villain's devious plot to steal the diamonds.

senses.definition: A person or thing that contrasts with another, highlighting its qualities
senses.exampleSentence: The cynical detective served as a perfect foil to his optimistic partner.

memoryHook.text: Aluminum foil - wrapping the villain's evil plans in heavy aluminum foil to completely block and ruin them.

quizSentence: Batman always manages to foil the Joker's complex and dangerous criminal plots.
alternateExampleSentence: The cynical detective served as a perfect foil to his optimistic partner.

exampleSentence: The desperate survivors had to forage for edible berries in the dense forest.
etymology: Old French *fourrage*, from *fuerre* ‘straw, fodder,’ Frankish *fodr* ‘food.’
quizSentence: Lost hikers had to forage for edible berries and nuts in the dense forest.
alternateExampleSentence: The desperate raccoons will forage through the neighborhood trash cans every single night.

exampleSentence: The bank showed great forbearance by giving the struggling family more time to pay.
etymology: From *forbear* + *-ance*; Old English *forberan* ‘to endure.’

memoryHook.text: Bear - having the immense patience and self-control not to shoot the bear when it crosses your path.

quizSentence: The patient teacher showed incredible forbearance when dealing with the disruptive student.
alternateExampleSentence: The teacher handled the noisy children with remarkable forbearance and endless patience.

definition: To prevent by taking advance action
exampleSentence: The central bank raised interest rates to forestall a dangerous spike in inflation.
etymology: Middle English *forestallen* ‘to intercept goods before market,’ from *fore-* + *stall* ‘marketplace.’

memoryHook.text: Before stall - placing a heavy stall in the road before the enemy arrives to entirely prevent their advance.

quizSentence: The company sold off its valuable real estate to forestall an inevitable, crushing bankruptcy.
alternateExampleSentence: He took vitamins daily to forestall the severe symptoms of the winter flu.

exampleSentence: The forlorn puppy sat in the rain waiting for his owner to return home.
etymology: Old English *forloren* ‘lost, past participle of *forleosan* ‘lose utterly.’

memoryHook.text: Torn - your heart is totally torn because you are pitifully sad, lonely, and abandoned.

quizSentence: The abandoned puppy looked forlorn sitting alone in the cold, pouring rain.
alternateExampleSentence: She stared out the window with a forlorn expression after her closest friend departed.

exampleSentence: The strict monk decided to forsake all his worldly wealth and live in poverty.
etymology: Old English *forsacan* ‘to renounce,’ from *for-* ‘completely’ + *sacan* ‘to dispute, deny.’
quizSentence: The devoted monk chose to forsake his worldly possessions for a life of prayer.
alternateExampleSentence: A true loyal friend will never forsake you during a difficult financial crisis.

exampleSentence: The brave soldiers displayed incredible fortitude during the long and brutal winter battle.
etymology: Latin *fortitudo*, from *fortis* ‘strong, brave.’

memoryHook.text: "fort" (strong) = immense mental strength and courage while enduring deep pain.

quizSentence: The soldier showed fortitude while enduring hardship during active combat.
alternateExampleSentence: She showed remarkable emotional fortitude while recovering from the tragic loss of her father.

exampleSentence: Meeting the influential publisher at the crowded cafe was a fortuitous encounter.
etymology: Latin *fortuitus* ‘happening by chance,’ from *forte* ‘by chance,’ from * fors* ‘luck.’

memoryHook.text: "fortun" (chance) = something great happening by a lucky, happy accident rather than design.

quizSentence: The fortuitous discovery of the gold vein transformed the struggling miner.
alternateExampleSentence: The sudden rainstorm was a fortuitous break for the exhausted marathon runner.

exampleSentence: The town hall meeting provided an excellent forum for discussing new environmental policies.
etymology: Latin *forum* ‘open square, public place,’ related to *foris* ‘outside.’
quizSentence: The town hall meeting provided an excellent forum for citizens to voice their valid complaints.
alternateExampleSentence: The university hosted a public forum to discuss the controversial new campus housing policies.

definition: To heavily encourage the development of
exampleSentence: The program helps to foster a strong sense of community among the new residents.
etymology: Old English *fostrian* ‘to feed, nourish,’ from *fostor* ‘food, nourishment.’
quizSentence: The kind couple decided to officially foster the abandoned child in their loving home.
alternateExampleSentence: The kind family decided to foster three abandoned puppies until they found permanent homes.

exampleSentence: The fractious toddlers refused to share their toys and began crying loudly.
etymology: From *fraction* in sense of discord; Latin *frangere* ‘to break’ + *-ous.’

memoryHook.text: "fract" (break) = a group that is easily broken into arguments; highly irritable and unruly.

quizSentence: The fractious toddler threw a tantrum in the middle of the grocery store.
alternateExampleSentence: The exhausted teacher struggled to quiet the fractious children during the long assembly.

definition: Causing or affected by great anxiety
exampleSentence: The dangerous rescue mission was fraught with terrible risks and unknown variables.
etymology: Middle Dutch *vracht* ‘load’; English sense ‘laden (with danger)’ from maritime cargo.

memoryHook.text: Caught - a tense situation where you are constantly worried you will be caught because the room is filled with danger.

quizSentence: The dangerous rescue mission was fraught with incredible peril and unexpected complications.
alternateExampleSentence: Their relationship was always fraught with intense emotional drama and bitter arguments.

exampleSentence: The frenetic pace of the busy hospital emergency room exhausted the young nurses.
etymology: Old French *frenetique*, from Greek *phrenitis* ‘inflammation of the brain,’ from *phrēn* ‘mind.’

memoryHook.text: Frenzy - moving with wild, incredibly fast, frantic energy.

quizSentence: The busy restaurant kitchen operated at a frenetic pace during the crowded Friday dinner rush.
alternateExampleSentence: The frenetic pace of the trading floor exhausted even the most experienced brokers.

exampleSentence: The judge dismissed the frivolous lawsuit because it lacked any real legal merit.
etymology: Latin *frivolus* ‘silly, empty,’ perhaps from *friare* ‘to crumble.’

memoryHook.text: Free volley - playing a completely meaningless, silly free volley in tennis instead of focusing on the actual match.

quizSentence: The wealthy heiress spent her fortune on entirely frivolous luxury items and expensive vacations.
alternateExampleSentence: She spent her entire paycheck on frivolous items instead of paying her monthly rent.

exampleSentence: The frugal student saved money by cooking all his meals at home every week.
etymology: Latin *frugalis*, from *frux* ‘fruit, value’—worthy of the fruit of one’s labor.

memoryHook.text: Fruit girl - the girl who only eats free fruit from the trees to save money because she is highly economical.
