quizSentence: The frugal student saved money by eating simple, inexpensive meals.
alternateExampleSentence: He drives an older car because he prefers a frugal financial lifestyle.

exampleSentence: The spy cast a furtive glance over his shoulder to ensure nobody followed him.
etymology: French *furtif*, from Latin *furtivus*, from *fur* ‘thief.’

memoryHook.text: Fur thief - the fur thief moves quietly and secretly through the mansion to avoid being caught.

quizSentence: The suspicious spy cast a furtive glance around the room before dropping the secret package.
alternateExampleSentence: The cheating student made a furtive attempt to look at his neighbor's test paper.

etymology: From Luigi Galvani, whose experiments with electricity inspired the metaphor
definition: To shock or excite into action
exampleSentence: The inspiring speech helped to galvanize the tired workers into finishing the difficult project.

memoryHook.text: Galvanize recalls an electric jolt that shocks people into motion.

quizSentence: A single compelling speech galvanized the coalition to act before the vote.
alternateExampleSentence: The shocking news of the scandal will galvanize the voters before the upcoming election.

exampleSentence: The wealthy eccentric painted his historical mansion in garish shades of neon pink.
etymology: Perhaps from obsolete *gair* ‘to stare’; influenced by *yellow* folk etymology; uncertain.

memoryHook.text: Garnish - piling way too much cheap, brightly colored garnish on a plate, making the whole meal look tacky.

quizSentence: The neon green sweater was far too garish for a solemn funeral.
alternateExampleSentence: She wore a garish purple dress that attracted unwanted attention at the formal dinner.

exampleSentence: The garrulous witness repeatedly steered responses away from the question actually posed.
etymology: Latin *garrulus* ‘chattering,’ from *garrire* ‘to chatter.’

memoryHook.text: Gary rules - Gary never shuts up about how much he rules; an excessively talkative guy.

quizSentence: He avoided the garrulous barber who talked nonstop during haircuts.
alternateExampleSentence: The normally quiet child became surprisingly garrulous after eating too much sugary candy.

exampleSentence: The genial host welcomed every single guest with a warm smile and drink.
etymology: Latin *genialis* ‘festive, pertaining to birth (*genius* spirit),’ from *genius* ‘attendant spirit.’

memoryHook.text: "gen" (nature/born) = having a naturally friendly, warm, and cheerful disposition.

quizSentence: The genial host greeted every arrival with a warm smile.
alternateExampleSentence: The genial department chair eased what could have been a contentious annual review.

exampleSentence: His ridiculous gluttony at the buffet left him feeling sick for two days.
etymology: Old French *glotonie*, from Latin *glutto* ‘glutton.’
quizSentence: Consuming an entire wedding cake alone is pure gluttony.
alternateExampleSentence: The greedy king was famous for his gluttony and endless holiday feasts.

exampleSentence: The bullies tried to goad the quiet student into starting a fistfight.
etymology: Old English *gād* ‘pointed stick,’ from Germanic *gaitō* ‘arrow, dart.’

memoryHook.text: Goat - poking a stubborn goat repeatedly with a sharp stick to provoke it to move.

quizSentence: His older sister loved to goad him into throwing temper tantrums.
alternateExampleSentence: She used sarcastic remarks to goad her brother into cleaning the dirty kitchen.

exampleSentence: The wealthy gourmand traveled across Europe just to eat at expensive restaurants.
etymology: French *gourmand* ‘glutton,’ from Old French *gourmer* ‘to swallow greedily.’

memoryHook.text: Gourmet man - a man who loves eating rich, gourmet food in excessive amounts.

quizSentence: A true gourmand, he traveled to Paris just to try a specific cheese.
alternateExampleSentence: As a true gourmand, he refused to eat anything from a fast food restaurant.

exampleSentence: The politician's empty grandiloquence failed to impress the frustrated working class voters.
etymology: Latin *grandiloquus* ‘grand-speaking,’ from *grandis* ‘great’ + *loqui* ‘speak.’

memoryHook.text: "grand" (great) + "loqu" (speak) = speaking in a massive, overly pompous way.

quizSentence: The senator's grandiloquence hid the fact that his policies lacked substance.
alternateExampleSentence: He masked his lack of actual knowledge behind impressive vocabulary and grandiloquence.

exampleSentence: The billionaire unveiled a grandiose plan to build a floating city in the ocean.
etymology: French *grandiose*, from Italian *grandioso*, from Latin *grandis* ‘great.’
quizSentence: She abandoned her grandiose plans to build a castle in the suburbs.
alternateExampleSentence: Her grandiose claims about changing the entire tech industry were met with deep skepticism.

exampleSentence: The film critic complained about the gratuitous violence in the new action movie.
etymology: Old French *gratuit*, from Latin *gratuitus* ‘free of charge,’ from *gratia* ‘favor, thanks.’

memoryHook.text: Gratuity - adding an unnecessary, uncalled-for extra tip when the service was already bad.

quizSentence: The horror film relied on gratuitous violence rather than a scary plot.
alternateExampleSentence: The rich celebrity displayed a gratuitous amount of wealth during the television interview.

exampleSentence: The gregarious student quickly made dozens of new friends during freshman orientation.
etymology: Latin *gregarius* ‘belonging to a flock,’ from *grex* ‘herd, flock.’

memoryHook.text: "greg" (flock) = loving to be part of the flock; highly sociable and outgoing.

quizSentence: A gregarious personality can compensate for technical gaps when building early client relationships.
alternateExampleSentence: Lions are gregarious animals that prefer to live and hunt in large prides.

exampleSentence: The reckless driver caused a grievous injury to the pedestrian crossing the street.
etymology: Anglo-French *grevous*, from *grever* ‘to burden,’ Latin *gravare* ‘make heavy.’

memoryHook.text: Grief - an injury or mistake so severe that it causes immense grief.

quizSentence: The factory worker suffered a grievous injury from the malfunctioning press.
alternateExampleSentence: The company committed a grievous error by accidentally deleting the entire customer database.

exampleSentence: The cunning spy used her guile to steal the classified documents without detection.
etymology: Middle English from Old French *guile*, of Germanic origin akin to *wile.*

memoryHook.text: Disguise - a criminal using a clever disguise out of pure guile and trickery.

quizSentence: It takes a lot of guile to successfully bluff your way through a poker tournament.
alternateExampleSentence: He relied on pure guile rather than honest hard work to secure the promotion.

exampleSentence: The romantic comedy relied on a hackneyed plot that everyone had seen before.
etymology: From *hackney* ‘ordinary riding horse for hire,’ extended to anything overused.

memoryHook.text: From Hackney, an area in London where horses were bred to be rented out. The horses became so overworked and common that the word evolved…

quizSentence: "Love conquers all" is a hackneyed phrase that belongs in cheap greeting cards.
alternateExampleSentence: The politician filled his speech with hackneyed phrases and boring campaign slogans.

exampleSentence: The soldiers stood in silence to honor the hallowed grounds of the battlefield.
etymology: Old English *halgian* ‘to make holy,’ from *hālig* ‘holy.’

memoryHook.text: Halloween (All Hallows' Eve) - referring to the most holy, deeply respected evening.

quizSentence: The diplomatic corps treated the conference hall as hallowed ground for negotiation and protocol.
alternateExampleSentence: The ancient university is considered a hallowed institution of higher academic learning.

exampleSentence: The hapless traveler lost his luggage and missed his connecting flight to London.
etymology: From *hap* ‘luck’ (Old Norse *happ*) + *-less* ‘without.’

memoryHook.text: "hap" (luck/chance) + "less" = entirely without luck; doomed.

quizSentence: The hapless golfer managed to hit his ball directly into a passing garbage truck.
alternateExampleSentence: A kind stranger stopped to help the hapless driver change a flat tire.

etymology: Middle French *harangue*, from medieval Latin *haringa* ‘troop in line’ (a military address).

senses.exampleSentence: Everyone had heard the teacher’s harangue about gum chewing in class before.
The teacher harangued the class about brushing teeth after chewing gum.

memoryHook.text: Hear anger - a long, aggressive speech where all you hear is the speaker's intense anger.

exampleSentence: The angry coach delivered a bitter harangue after the team lost the championship game.
quizSentence: The coach delivered a blistering harangue after the team lost by forty points.
alternateExampleSentence: The dictator broadcast a furious harangue threatening his political enemies with strict prison sentences.

exampleSentence: The hardy mountain goats easily navigated the treacherous and icy rocky cliffs.
etymology: Middle English *hardi*, from Old French *hardi* ‘bold,’ from Frankish *hardjan* ‘make hard.’

memoryHook.text: Hard - a plant or person tough enough to survive extremely hard, freezing conditions.

quizSentence: These hardy succulents can survive for months without a single drop of water.
alternateExampleSentence: These hardy tomato plants can survive freezing temperatures and severe summer droughts.

exampleSentence: The weary soldiers survived a harrowing journey through the dense, enemy-infested jungle.
etymology: From *harrow* ‘to plow up’ (figuratively torment); Old English *hergian* ‘to harry.’

memoryHook.text: Arrowing - being shot at by a rain of flying arrows is a terrifying, highly distressing experience.

quizSentence: Surviving the avalanche was a harrowing experience he would never forget.
alternateExampleSentence: The documentary provides a harrowing look at the harsh realities of modern warfare.

exampleSentence: A haughty demeanor in interviews tends to alienate precisely the evaluators who hold authority.
etymology: Extension of *haught* ‘high,’ from Old French *haut* ‘high,’ Latin *altus.*

memoryHook.text: High - acting incredibly high and mighty, looking down on everyone else.

quizSentence: The haughty executive dismissed the intern's suggestion without glancing at the supporting analysis.
alternateExampleSentence: She cast a haughty glance at the messy room before walking away in disgust.

exampleSentence: The wealthy hedonist spent his entire fortune on wild parties and expensive cars.
etymology: Greek *hēdonē* ‘pleasure’ + *-ist’; coined for seekers of pleasure as a principle.

memoryHook.text: He done it - "He done it" just for the sheer thrill, because a hedonist lives strictly for physical pleasure.

quizSentence: As a dedicated hedonist, she spent all her money on spa days and fine wine.
alternateExampleSentence: As a dedicated hedonist, he believed that seeking physical pleasure was life's only purpose.

exampleSentence: The powerful empire maintained political hegemony over the smaller neighboring coastal nations.
etymology: Greek *hēgemonia* ‘leadership,’ from *hēgemōn* ‘leader,’ from *hēgeisthai* ‘to lead.’

memoryHook.text: Huge money - the empire with huge money has absolute dominance and control over the weaker nations.

quizSentence: The Roman Empire maintained strict hegemony over the Mediterranean for centuries.
alternateExampleSentence: The tech giant established total market hegemony by acquiring all its smaller competitors.

exampleSentence: The jury quickly convicted the criminal for his heinous acts against the community.
etymology: Old French *haineus*, from *haine* ‘hatred,’ from Frankish *hatis* ‘hatred.’

memoryHook.text: Hate us - committing crimes so terribly wicked they make the entire world hate us.

quizSentence: The jury quickly convicted the murderer for his particularly heinous crimes.
alternateExampleSentence: The dictator was internationally condemned for his heinous crimes against the civilian population.

exampleSentence: The diverse city boasts a heterogeneous population from all over the world.
etymology: Greek *heterogenēs*, from *heteros* ‘other’ + *genos* ‘kind.’

memoryHook.text: "hetero" (different) + "gen" (kind) = made up of completely different, varied parts.

quizSentence: The diverse neighborhood consists of a truly heterogeneous mix of cultures.
alternateExampleSentence: The teacher designed a lesson plan to engage a heterogeneous classroom of students.

exampleSentence: The popular television show went on a brief hiatus during the winter holidays.
etymology: Latin *hiatus* ‘opening, gap,’ from *hiare* ‘to gape.’

memoryHook.text: Hide us - the famous band begging "hide us" so they can take a long, badly needed break from the public.

quizSentence: The beloved television show returned to the air after a five-year hiatus.
alternateExampleSentence: The rock band announced a sudden hiatus after touring constantly for ten years.

exampleSentence: The military relies on a strict hierarchy to ensure orders are followed immediately.
etymology: Greek *hierarchia* ‘rule of a high priest,’ from *hieros* ‘sacred’ + *archē* ‘rule.’
quizSentence: She worked her way up the corporate hierarchy from intern to executive.
alternateExampleSentence: The corporate hierarchy places the chief executive officer at the top level.

exampleSentence: It is pure hypocrisy to lecture others about diet while eating junk food.
etymology: Greek *hypokrisis* ‘acting on a stage,’ from *hypo-* ‘under’ + *krinein* ‘to separate, judge.’
quizSentence: It is pure hypocrisy to complain about pollution while throwing trash out the window.
alternateExampleSentence: The voters were disgusted by the blatant hypocrisy of the corrupt local politician.

exampleSentence: The professor presented a hypothetical scenario to test the students' critical thinking skills.
etymology: Greek *hupothetikos*, from *hupotithenai* ‘to place under, suppose.’
quizSentence: The physics exam asked us to solve a hypothetical scenario involving time travel.
alternateExampleSentence: A business strategy built on hypothetical projections rarely survives contact with real market conditions.

exampleSentence: The brilliant iconoclast rejected traditional artistic rules and invented a new painting style.
etymology: Greek *eikōn* ‘image’ + *klan* ‘to break’—one who smashes idols or conventions.

memoryHook.text: "icon" (image) + "clast" (breaker) = a rebel who attacks and breaks heavily established traditions and beliefs.

quizSentence: The rebellious painter was an iconoclast who mocked traditional artistic standards.
alternateExampleSentence: As a fearless iconoclast, she constantly challenged the rigid norms of her society.

exampleSentence: His idiosyncratic habit of wearing mismatched socks always amused his close friends.
etymology: Greek *idios* ‘one’s own’ + *synkrasis* ‘mixture’—a personal blend of traits.

memoryHook.text: "idio" (peculiar/self) = a highly unique, strange trait specific to one individual.

quizSentence: His idiosyncratic habit of eating pizza with chopsticks amused his friends.
alternateExampleSentence: The director has an idiosyncratic filmmaking style that critics either love or hate.

exampleSentence: The ancient cult demanded idolatrous devotion to a strange statue made of gold.
etymology: Greek *eidōlolatreia* ‘image worship,’ from *eidōlon* ‘image’ + *latreia* ‘service.’

memoryHook.text: "idol" = worshiping an idol or a person to an excessive, blind degree.

quizSentence: The fans' idolatrous worship of the pop star bordered on an obsession.
alternateExampleSentence: His idolatrous admiration for the celebrity blinded him to her obvious personal flaws.

exampleSentence: The corrupt general suffered an ignominious defeat and was stripped of his rank.
etymology: Latin *ignominiosus*, from *in-* ‘not’ + *nomen* ‘name’—loss of good name.

memoryHook.text: "ig-" (not) + "nomin" (name) = having your good name totally destroyed; suffering a disgraceful public defeat.

quizSentence: The disgraced general suffered an ignominious retreat after losing the battle.
alternateExampleSentence: The scandal brought an ignominious end to her previously flawless political career.

exampleSentence: The smuggler was arrested for transporting illicit goods across the heavily guarded border.
etymology: Latin *illicitus* ‘not allowed,’ from *in-* + *licitus* ‘permitted.’

memoryHook.text: Ill - doing highly illegal things that make society ill.

quizSentence: Police uncovered an illicit gambling ring operating behind the butcher shop.
alternateExampleSentence: The police shut down the illicit gambling operation hidden behind the local bakery.

exampleSentence: You must immerse yourself in the language to truly become fluent in Spanish.
etymology: Latin *immergere*, from *in-* + *mergere* ‘to dip, plunge.’
quizSentence: She likes to immerse herself in a hot bath after a long day at work.
alternateExampleSentence: The author wanted readers to fully immerse themselves in his magical fantasy world.

exampleSentence: The laws of physics are generally considered to be absolute and immutable facts.
etymology: Latin *immutabilis*, from *in-* + *mutare* ‘to change.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "mut" (change) = a law of physics that is entirely unable to be mutated or changed.

quizSentence: The speed of light in a vacuum is an immutable law of physics.
alternateExampleSentence: Her strict moral principles remained immutable despite the heavy pressure from her peers.

exampleSentence: The royal guard maintained an impassive expression despite the screaming tourists nearby.
etymology: From *in-* ‘not’ + *passive* (Latin *pati* ‘to suffer, feel’).

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pass" (feeling) = an expression showing absolutely zero emotion or feeling.

quizSentence: The royal guard remained impassive despite the teasing tourists.
alternateExampleSentence: The judge remained impassive while listening to the highly emotional witness testimony.

exampleSentence: The butler maintained the wealthy estate with an impeccable attention to minor details.
etymology: Latin *impeccabilis*, from *in-* + *peccare* ‘to sin, stumble.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pecc" (sin) = a performance or outfit that is entirely without sin or flaw.

quizSentence: The butler's impeccable manners made him a favorite among the wealthy guests.
alternateExampleSentence: She wore an impeccable white suit to the important corporate board meeting.

exampleSentence: The impecunious artist struggled to pay his monthly rent for the tiny studio.
etymology: From Latin *pecunia* ‘money’ + *in-* ‘without’—without money.

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pecun" (money) = having absolutely no money.

quizSentence: The impecunious artist often traded his paintings for hot meals.
alternateExampleSentence: After losing his job, he was forced to adopt an impecunious lifestyle.

etymology: Latin impedire, from in- ('in') + pes ('foot') — to shackle the feet
exampleSentence: Poor lighting and narrow corridors impede the efficient evacuation of the aging facility.

memoryHook.text: "Im-" (in) + "ped" (foot) = feet caught, movement blocked.

quizSentence: Bureaucratic delays impeded implementation of the approved reforms.
alternateExampleSentence: Strict government regulations continue to impede the rapid growth of the tech startup.

etymology: Late Latin *imperativus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperare* ‘to command.’

senses.exampleSentence: It is imperative that you have these folders organized by midday.
Her imperative to have the folders organized by midday was perceived as ridiculous by the others.

exampleSentence: It is imperative that you finish this crucial assignment before the deadline tomorrow.
quizSentence: It is imperative that you submit your application before the midnight deadline.
alternateExampleSentence: Clean drinking water is an imperative for the survival of the village.

exampleSentence: The imperious manager demanded that everyone work late without any extra financial compensation.
etymology: Latin *imperiosus* ‘commanding,’ from *imperium* ‘command, empire.’

memoryHook.text: Emperor - acting like a bossy, highly arrogant emperor barking orders.

quizSentence: The imperious manager snapped her fingers and ordered the staff to work faster.
alternateExampleSentence: She waved her hand with an imperious gesture, dismissing the junior staff members.

exampleSentence: The student was punished for asking an impertinent question during the serious lecture.
etymology: Latin *impertinens* ‘not belonging,’ from *in-* + *pertinere* ‘to pertain’—out of place.

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pertinent" (proper/relevant) = acting outside the bounds of proper respect; incredibly rude to an authority figure.

quizSentence: The student was sent to the principal for making impertinent remarks.
alternateExampleSentence: His impertinent attitude quickly offended the prestigious guests at the formal dinner party.

exampleSentence: The thick waterproof jacket is entirely impervious to the harsh winter rain.
etymology: Latin *impervius*, from *in-* + *pervius* ‘passable,’ from *per-* ‘through’ + *via* ‘way.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "per" (through) + "via" (way) = providing no way through; a raincoat impervious to water.

quizSentence: The lead researcher appeared impervious to criticism, rarely acknowledging flaws in her methodology.
alternateExampleSentence: The arrogant politician seemed utterly impervious to the widespread criticism from the media.

exampleSentence: The impetuous teenager bought a used sports car without checking the engine first.
etymology: Late Latin *impetuosus*, from Latin *impetus* ‘attack, rush.’

memoryHook.text: Impulse - acting strictly on wild impulse without thinking of the consequences.

quizSentence: Marrying someone you just met in Vegas is a highly impetuous decision.
alternateExampleSentence: Her impetuous decision to quit her secure job shocked all of her friends.

etymology: Latin *impingere* ‘to drive in, strike against,’ from *in-* + *pangere* ‘to fasten, fix.’

senses.definition: To impact, affect, make an impression
senses.exampleSentence: The hail impinged the roof, leaving large dents.
New rules may impinge on fishing rights long protected by local custom.

memoryHook.text: Pinch - encroaching on someone's personal space until you literally pinch them.

exampleSentence: The noisy construction outside began to impinge upon the quiet atmosphere of the library.
quizSentence: Excessive administrative duties impinge on the time professors dedicate to original research.
alternateExampleSentence: He feared the new security laws would severely impinge on his personal freedoms.

exampleSentence: The implacable enemy army refused to accept any terms of surrender or peace.
etymology: Latin *implacabilis*, from *in-* + *placare* ‘to appease.’

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "plac" (calm/please) = an enemy whose raging anger is impossible to calm down.

quizSentence: The prosecution faced an implacable opposing counsel who refused every proposed plea arrangement.
alternateExampleSentence: Her implacable grief over the tragedy lasted for many difficult, lonely years.

etymology: Late Latin *implēmentum* ‘filling up,’ from *implēre* ‘to fulfill’ (*in-* + *plēre* ‘fill’).

senses.exampleSentence: She used a metal implement to pry the stubborn lid off the jar.

senses.definition: To put into effect, to institute
senses.exampleSentence: The mayor implemented a policy using cameras to catch graffiti vandals.

exampleSentence: The school board decided to implement a strict dress code for all students.
quizSentence: The city council voted to implement a new recycling program next month.
alternateExampleSentence: The software team must implement the new security features by the end of Friday.

exampleSentence: The captured spy confessed to crimes that would implicate several prominent government officials.
etymology: Latin *implicare* ‘to fold in,’ from *in-* + *plicare* ‘to fold.’
quizSentence: The forged signature on the check will implicate the accountant in the fraud.
alternateExampleSentence: The captured thief tried to implicate his innocent brother to save his own life.

exampleSentence: There was an implicit agreement that he would eventually inherit the family business.
etymology: Latin *implicitus* ‘folded in,’ past participle of *implicare*.
quizSentence: Her tight-lipped smile carried an implicit warning to stop asking questions.
alternateExampleSentence: Her silence was seen as an implicit approval of the controversial plan.

exampleSentence: The ancient stone fortress was considered impregnable to any invading enemy army.
etymology: Old French *impregnable*, from *in-* + *pregnable* ‘able to be taken’ (from *prendre* ‘take’).

memoryHook.text: "im-" (not) + "pregnable" (vulnerable to capture) = a heavy stone fortress that is impossible to break into.

quizSentence: The thick stone walls made the ancient castle virtually impregnable.
alternateExampleSentence: The bank vault is guarded by an impregnable steel door and biometric scanners.

exampleSentence: The impudent child stuck his tongue out at the strict school principal.
etymology: Latin *impudens* ‘shameless,’ from *in-* + *pudens* ‘modest,’ from *pudere* ‘to be ashamed.’

memoryHook.text: Rude student - a highly bold, shamelessly disrespectful student backtalking the principal.

quizSentence: The impudent teenager rolled his eyes when asked to clean his room.
alternateExampleSentence: The judge scolded the lawyer for his impudent remarks during the serious trial.

exampleSentence: The biased manager tried to impute the project's failure to his newest employee.
etymology: Latin *imputare* ‘to reckon, charge,’ from *in-* + *putare* ‘to think, reckon.’

memoryHook.text: Put - to put the blame for the mistake directly onto someone else's record.

quizSentence: You cannot impute the company's financial failure to one single employee.
alternateExampleSentence: It is unfair to impute malicious motives to a simple, honest mistake.

exampleSentence: The bored teenager scrolled mindlessly through inane social media videos for several hours.
etymology: Latin *inanis* ‘empty, void.’

memoryHook.text: Insane - a comment so painfully empty and silly it borders on insane.

quizSentence: The sitcom was filled with inane dialogue that insulted the viewers' intelligence.
alternateExampleSentence: He kept making inane comments that disrupted the serious flow of the meeting.

exampleSentence: The terrified witness was so inarticulate that the police could not understand him.
etymology: Latin *inarticulatus* ‘not jointed,’ from *in-* + *articulare* ‘to speak distinctly.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "articulate" = entirely unable to speak clearly or express thoughts.

quizSentence: Shock made him entirely inarticulate when he won the lottery.
alternateExampleSentence: His inarticulate mumbling made it impossible to follow his complex scientific presentation.

etymology: Latin *incarnare* ‘to make flesh,’ from *in-* + *caro* ‘flesh.’

senses.exampleSentence: In the church pageant, I play the role of greed incarnate.
The alien evaded detection by incarnating himself in a human form.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (in) + "carn" (flesh) = a spirit or idea given physical, bodily form in the flesh.

exampleSentence: The cruel dictator was viewed by his oppressed citizens as evil incarnate.
quizSentence: With her fierce temper and cruel jokes, she seemed like a demon incarnate.
alternateExampleSentence: She played the role of a villain who was cruelty and malice incarnate.

etymology: Latin *incendiarius*, from *incendium* ‘conflagration,’ from *incendere* ‘to set on fire.’

senses.exampleSentence: The incendiary remark during the match nearly caused a riot in the stands.
Gas and lighter fluid are incendiary materials that should be kept out of hot storage areas.

memoryHook.text: "incend" (fire) = a bomb or a speech specifically designed to cause massive fires or conflict.

exampleSentence: The rebel leader delivered an incendiary speech that sparked a violent city riot.
quizSentence: The rebel leader distributed incendiary pamphlets to spark a revolution.
alternateExampleSentence: The newspaper published an incendiary article that angered many local community leaders.

exampleSentence: The incessant barking of the neighbor's dog kept us awake all night.
etymology: Late Latin *incessans*, from *in-* + *cessare* ‘to cease.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "cess" (stop) = a dripping faucet that is never stopping.

quizSentence: The incessant hum of the server room made prolonged work there mentally taxing.
alternateExampleSentence: She grew tired of his incessant complaining about the cold winter weather.

exampleSentence: His business plan was still inchoate and lacked any clear financial structure.
etymology: Latin *inchoatus* ‘begun,’ from *inchoare* ‘to start work’ (origin disputed).

memoryHook.text: Inches - a business plan that is only a few inches along; just barely starting and completely unformed.

quizSentence: His thesis was still in an inchoate stage, consisting mostly of scattered notes.
alternateExampleSentence: The artist struggled to turn her inchoate ideas into a finished masterpiece.

exampleSentence: The critic wrote an incisive review that accurately highlighted the film's structural flaws.
etymology: Latin *incisus* ‘cut into,’ from *incidere*, from *in-* + *caedere* ‘to cut.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (into) + "caedere" (cut) = mental analysis that cuts straight to the heart of the matter; razor-sharp.

quizSentence: Her incisive critique cut right to the core of the author's flawed argument.
alternateExampleSentence: Her incisive analytical skills helped the team quickly solve the complex database problem.

exampleSentence: He had a strong inclination to stay home instead of attending the party.
etymology: Latin *inclinatio*, from *inclinare* ‘to lean toward.’
quizSentence: A natural inclination toward caution serves an auditor well in a high-stakes financial review.
alternateExampleSentence: The quiet student showed a natural inclination toward studying advanced mathematical theories.

exampleSentence: The security footage provided incontrovertible proof that the suspect robbed the bank.
etymology: Latin *incontrovertibilis*, from *in-* + *controvertere* ‘to dispute.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "controvert" (dispute) = DNA evidence that is entirely impossible to argue against.

quizSentence: The DNA match provided incontrovertible proof of the suspect's guilt.
alternateExampleSentence: The scientist presented incontrovertible evidence to support his groundbreaking new biological theory.

exampleSentence: The incorrigible child refused to listen to his parents or follow basic rules.
etymology: Latin *incorrigibilis*, from *in-* + *corrigere* ‘to correct.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "corrigere" (correct) = a criminal so deeply bad they are completely beyond correction.

quizSentence: The incorrigible prankster glued the teacher's coffee mug to the desk.
alternateExampleSentence: He is an incorrigible optimist who always sees the bright side of tragedies.

exampleSentence: The employee received a small salary increment at the end of the year.
etymology: Latin *incrementum* ‘growth,’ from *increscere* ‘to grow upon.’
quizSentence: Your salary will increase by a small increment every six months.
alternateExampleSentence: The software developer improved the code in small increments over several weeks.

etymology: Latin *incumbere* ‘to lie upon,’ hence pressing as a duty.

senses.exampleSentence: The incumbent senator is already serving his fifth term.
It is incumbent upon this organization to offer aid to all who seek it.

memoryHook.text: Occupies the seat now - the officeholder already in power (the one whose role is filled).

exampleSentence: The incumbent mayor easily won reelection after a successful four years in office.
quizSentence: The incumbent mayor is heavily favored to win a second term in office.
alternateExampleSentence: It is incumbent upon the manager to ensure the safety of all employees.

exampleSentence: The indefatigable marathon runner continued training despite the pouring rain and heavy winds.
etymology: Latin *indefatigabilis*, from *in-* + *defatigare* ‘to wear out.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "defatigate" (tire) = a marathon runner whose energy is impossible to tire out.

quizSentence: The indefatigable marathoner crossed the finish line without even breaking a sweat.
alternateExampleSentence: Her indefatigable work ethic earned her the absolute respect of her corporate colleagues.

exampleSentence: The museum exhibit highlights the beautiful artwork of the indigenous tribes of Australia.
etymology: Late Latin *indigena* ‘native,’ from *indi-* ‘within’ + *gen-* ‘be born.’
quizSentence: Kangaroos and koalas are indigenous solely to the continent of Australia.
alternateExampleSentence: This rare species of orchid is indigenous to the dense Amazonian rain forest.

exampleSentence: The local charity provides hot meals and warm blankets to the indigent population.
etymology: Old French *indigent*, from Latin *indigere* ‘to need,’ literally ‘to lack in.’

memoryHook.text: Dig in dirt - someone so desperately poor they have to dig in the dirt to find food.

quizSentence: The local soup kitchen provides hot meals for the city's indigent population.
alternateExampleSentence: The free clinic offers essential medical care to indigent families in the city.

exampleSentence: The unfair tax increase sparked widespread indignation among the struggling working class.
etymology: Latin *indignatio*, from *indignari* ‘to regard as unworthy,’ from *in-* + *dignus* ‘worthy.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "dign" (worthy) = fierce anger sparked by being treated in a completely unworthy or unfair way.

quizSentence: She wrote a letter of pure indignation after receiving the insulting bill.
alternateExampleSentence: She expressed righteous indignation when the manager accused her of stealing the money.

exampleSentence: The indolent teenager slept until noon and refused to do any household chores.
etymology: Late Latin *indolens* ‘free from pain,’ from *in-* + *dolere* ‘to suffer’—avoiding effort.

memoryHook.text: Indoor tent - laying lazily in an indoor tent all day doing absolutely nothing.

quizSentence: The indolent cat spent fourteen hours asleep on the warmest spot on the rug.
alternateExampleSentence: His indolent behavior at the office quickly led to his inevitable termination.

exampleSentence: The heroic soldiers displayed an indomitable spirit during the harsh winter battle.
etymology: Late Latin *indomitabilis*, from *in-* + *domitare* ‘to tame,’ frequentative of *domare*.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "domit" (tame) = an underdog spirit that is impossible to crush or tame.

quizSentence: Helen Keller's indomitable spirit allowed her to overcome tremendous obstacles.
alternateExampleSentence: Despite countless rejections, the young author maintained an indomitable desire to publish.

exampleSentence: The doctor prescribed a mild medication to induce sleep for the anxious patient.
etymology: Latin *inducere* ‘to lead in,’ from *in-* + *ducere* ‘to lead.’
quizSentence: The doctor prescribed medication to induce labor for the overdue mother.
alternateExampleSentence: Nothing could induce the stubborn child to eat his plate of green vegetables.

exampleSentence: The breathtaking beauty of the Grand Canyon filled the tourists with ineffable awe.
etymology: Latin *ineffabilis*, from *in-* + *effari* ‘to speak out.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "effable" (speakable) = looking at the Grand Canyon and feeling an awe so immense it cannot be expressed in words.

quizSentence: The view of the Grand Canyon at sunrise possesses an ineffable beauty.
alternateExampleSentence: The stunning sunset over the ocean possessed an ineffable beauty that defied description.

exampleSentence: The inept mechanic accidentally caused more damage to the engine of the car.
etymology: Latin *ineptus* ‘unsuited,’ from *in-* + *aptus* ‘fitted.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "apt" (skilled) = entirely clumsy and lacking any skill for the job.

quizSentence: The inept plumber accidentally flooded the entire basement while fixing the sink.
alternateExampleSentence: His socially inept comments made everyone at the formal dinner party deeply uncomfortable.

exampleSentence: The inexorable march of time eventually turns ancient mountains into flat desert plains.
etymology: Latin *inexorabilis*, from *in-* + *exorare* ‘to obtain by entreaty’—not moved by pleading.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "exorare" (persuade) = the marching army was impossible to persuade or stop.

quizSentence: The inexorable march of time slowly turns children into adults.
alternateExampleSentence: The inexorable advance of the glacier slowly crushed everything standing in its path.

exampleSentence: The two rival nations share an inextricable bond forged through centuries of trade.
etymology: Latin *inextricabilis*, from *in-* + *extricare* ‘to disentangle.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "extricate" (untangle) = two headphones hopelessly and impossibly tangled together.

quizSentence: The city's economic fortunes are inextricable from the health of its port district.
alternateExampleSentence: The history of the coastal town is an inextricable part of maritime folklore.

exampleSentence: The cruel dictator will live in infamy for his terrible crimes against humanity.
etymology: Latin *infamia*, from *in-* + *fama* ‘report, reputation.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "fame" = possessing world fame, but strictly for highly evil reasons.

quizSentence: The traitor's name will live in infamy for betraying his own country.
alternateExampleSentence: The date of the surprise attack is forever etched in national infamy.

exampleSentence: The struggling business requires an immediate infusion of cash to avoid bankruptcy.
etymology: Latin *infusio*, from *infundere* ‘to pour in,’ from *in-* + *fundere* ‘pour.’
quizSentence: The dying patient required an immediate infusion of red blood cells.
alternateExampleSentence: The chef added a delicate infusion of fresh herbs to the vegetable soup.

exampleSentence: The engineer designed an ingenious device that turns salt water into drinking water.
etymology: Latin *ingeniosus* ‘talented,’ from *ingenium* ‘innate quality, wit.’
quizSentence: The engineer designed an ingenious device to safely catch falling glass.
alternateExampleSentence: Her ingenious marketing strategy doubled the company's revenue in just three short months.

exampleSentence: The ingenuous child believed every single word the clever magician told her.
etymology: Latin *ingenuus* ‘native, freeborn,’ hence frank and open.

memoryHook.text: Genuine - someone with a highly innocent, trusting, and completely genuine nature.

quizSentence: Her ingenuous belief in the goodness of everyone made her an easy target for scams.
alternateExampleSentence: His ingenuous nature made him an easy target for experienced scammers and thieves.

etymology: Latin inhaerere, from in- ('in') + haerere ('stick')
exampleSentence: There is an inherent risk of injury when participating in extreme mountain sports.

memoryHook.text: Inherent = stuck inside as a core trait.

quizSentence: Risk is inherent in experimentation, but it can be measured and managed.
alternateExampleSentence: A strong sense of loyalty is an inherent trait of many working dogs.

exampleSentence: The harsh new regulations will severely inhibit the growth of small local businesses.
etymology: Latin *inhibere* ‘to hold in,’ from *in-* + *habere* ‘to hold.’
quizSentence: Cold temperatures can seriously inhibit the growth of tropical plants.
alternateExampleSentence: Fear of public speaking can inhibit a student from participating in class discussions.

exampleSentence: The harsh desert climate is inimical to the survival of most plant life.
etymology: Late Latin *inimicalis*, from Latin *inimicus* ‘enemy,’ from *in-* + *amicus* ‘friend.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "amicus" (friend) = deeply unfriendly, hostile, and actively harmful.

quizSentence: Heavy frost is entirely inimical to the survival of a fragile citrus crop.
alternateExampleSentence: His aggressive management style proved inimical to a healthy corporate office environment.

exampleSentence: The ancient prophet loudly condemned the rampant iniquity of the corrupt city.
etymology: Old French *iniquité*, from Latin *iniquitas* ‘unfairness,’ from *iniquus* ‘unequal, unjust.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "equ" (fair/equal) = a situation of gross injustice or deeply wicked behavior.

quizSentence: The crusading journalist sought to expose the dark iniquity of the corrupt prison system.
alternateExampleSentence: The documentary exposed the brutal iniquity of the illegal global drug trade.

exampleSentence: The judge issued a strict injunction to halt the demolition of the building.
etymology: Late Latin *iniunctio*, from Latin *iniungere* ‘to enjoin,’ from *in-* + *iungere* ‘join.’
quizSentence: A judge issued an injunction to stop the demolition of the historic theater.
alternateExampleSentence: The striking workers ignored the court injunction and continued to block the factory.

exampleSentence: The talented artist possesses an innate ability to capture human emotion on canvas.
etymology: Latin *innatus* ‘inborn,’ past participle of *innasci* ‘to be born in.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (in) + "nat" (born) = a perfect pitch musical talent you were naturally born with.

quizSentence: Birds possess an innate sense of direction that guides their winter migrations.
alternateExampleSentence: Some birds have an innate sense of direction for their long winter migrations.

exampleSentence: The small spider looked scary but was actually innocuous to humans and pets.
etymology: Latin *innocuus* ‘harmless,’ from *in-* + *nocuus* ‘hurtful,’ from *nocēre* ‘to harm.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "noc" (harm) = entirely safe and harmless.

quizSentence: The seemingly innocuous mushroom turned out to be highly poisonous.
alternateExampleSentence: He made a seemingly innocuous comment that accidentally offended his sensitive older sister.

exampleSentence: The failing company must innovate its product line to survive in the market.
etymology: Latin *innovare* ‘to renew,’ from *in-* + *novare* ‘to make new,’ from *novus* ‘new.’
quizSentence: Tech companies must constantly innovate or risk being left behind by competitors.
alternateExampleSentence: Pressure from global competition forced the manufacturer to innovate across every stage of production.

exampleSentence: The gossip columnist used sly innuendo to suggest the actor was having an affair.
etymology: Latin *innuendo* ‘by nodding to,’ ablative gerund of *innuere* ‘to nod to, hint.’
quizSentence: The gossip column was full of scandalous innuendo about the actor's divorce.
alternateExampleSentence: The politician's speech was full of dark innuendo about his opponent's shady finances.

exampleSentence: The doctor must inoculate the children against the dangerous virus before school starts.
etymology: Latin *inoculare* ‘to graft an eye (bud),’ from *in-* + *oculus* ‘eye’ (bud of a plant).
quizSentence: You must inoculate your new puppy against rabies before bringing him to the park.
alternateExampleSentence: The harsh early failures helped to inoculate the young entrepreneur against future disappointment.

exampleSentence: The harsh inquisitor demanded a full confession from the terrified political prisoner.
etymology: Latin *inquisitor*, from *inquirere* ‘to seek into,’ from *in-* + *quaerere* ‘ask.’
quizSentence: The ruthless inquisitor demanded the names of the hidden rebels.
alternateExampleSentence: The attorney acted like a relentless inquisitor during the tense courtroom cross-examination.

exampleSentence: The growing teenager had an insatiable appetite and ate three dinners every night.
etymology: Latin *insatiabilis*, from *in-* + *satiare* ‘to fill.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "satiable" (satisfied) = a hunger for power that is absolutely impossible to satisfy.

quizSentence: The hungry teenager has an insatiable appetite for greasy fast food.
alternateExampleSentence: Her insatiable curiosity led her to read every history book in the library.

exampleSentence: The insidious disease slowly damages the nervous system before any symptoms appear.
etymology: Latin *insidiosus* ‘cunning,’ from *insidiae* ‘ambush,’ from *insidere* ‘to sit in.’

memoryHook.text: Inside us - a dangerous, sneaky disease spreading quietly inside us without being noticed until it's too late.

quizSentence: Carbon monoxide is an insidious threat because it is totally odorless and invisible.
alternateExampleSentence: He launched an insidious plot to secretly undermine the authority of the manager.

exampleSentence: The lawyer tried to insinuate that the witness was lying to the jury.
etymology: Latin *insinuare* ‘to wind in,’ from *in-* + *sinus* ‘curve, fold.’
quizSentence: The auditor insinuated through selective questioning that the financial discrepancies were not accidental.
alternateExampleSentence: A raised eyebrow at the evidence was enough to insinuate doubt without an accusation.

exampleSentence: The insipid movie lacked any real plot development or interesting character arcs.
etymology: French *insipide*, from Late Latin *insipidus*, from *in-* + *sapidus* ‘tasty,’ from *sapere* ‘to taste.’

memoryHook.text: Sip it - the tea is so totally flavorless and boring you can barely force yourself to sip it.

quizSentence: The hospital food was so insipid that visitors brought spices from home.
alternateExampleSentence: The food critic dismissed the chef's new soup as painfully insipid.

exampleSentence: The insolent teenager rolled his eyes when the strict teacher gave him detention.
etymology: Latin *insolens* ‘unusual, arrogant,’ from *in-* + *solere* ‘to be accustomed’—out of bounds.

memoryHook.text: Insulting - a teenager showing an incredibly rude and insulting lack of respect to a judge.

quizSentence: The insolent clerk chewed gum and ignored the customers waiting in line.
alternateExampleSentence: Her insolent behavior during the formal interview cost her the prestigious corporate job.

exampleSentence: The angry rebel leader managed to instigate a violent riot in the city.
etymology: Latin *instigare* ‘to urge on,’ from *in-* + *stigare* ‘to prick, goad.’
quizSentence: The bully tried to instigate a fight by shoving the smaller kid in the hallway.
alternateExampleSentence: He tried to instigate a fistfight by insulting the captain of the team.

exampleSentence: The insular island community rarely welcomed foreign visitors or adopted modern technology.
etymology: Latin *insularis* ‘of an island,’ from *insula* ‘island.’

memoryHook.text: "insula" (island) = having a highly isolated and narrow-minded worldview, living purely on a cultural island.

quizSentence: The isolated farming community remained highly insular and distrustful of strangers.
alternateExampleSentence: Her insular worldview made it difficult for her to understand different cultural perspectives.

exampleSentence: The armed insurgent forces captured the military base during the dead of night.
etymology: Latin *insurgere* ‘to rise up,’ from *in-* + *surgere* ‘to rise.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (against) + "surgere" (rise) = rebels rising up violently against the established government.

quizSentence: A lone insurgent managed to sabotage the military convoy under the cover of darkness.
alternateExampleSentence: The government sent troops to crush the violent insurgent rebellion in the mountains.

exampleSentence: A strong foundation is an integral part of constructing a stable brick house.
etymology: Late Latin *integralis*, from Latin *integer* ‘whole, untouched.’
quizSentence: A sturdy foundation is an integral component of any well-built house.
alternateExampleSentence: Good communication is integral to the success of any healthy romantic relationship.

exampleSentence: The rude guest continued to interject his own opinions during the formal presentation.
etymology: Latin *intericere* ‘to throw between,’ from *inter-* + *iacere* ‘to throw.’

memoryHook.text: "inter" (between) + "ject" (throw) = sharply throwing your own comment between other speakers' words.

quizSentence: Please do not interject your opinions while the guest speaker is presenting.
alternateExampleSentence: She chose to interject a funny joke to lighten the tense meeting atmosphere.

exampleSentence: The skilled interlocutor managed to extract valuable information during the casual conversation.
etymology: Modern Latin *interlocutor*, from Latin *interloqui* ‘to speak between.’

memoryHook.text: "inter" (between) + "loc" (speak) = the specific person you are speaking back and forth with in a conversation.

quizSentence: The translator served as a vital interlocutor between the two foreign diplomats.
alternateExampleSentence: The diplomat acted as a neutral interlocutor between the two hostile national leaders.

exampleSentence: The boring lecture felt interminable to the exhausted students in the warm classroom.
etymology: Late Latin *interminabilis*, from *in-* + *terminare* ‘to limit’—without limit.

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "termin" (end) = a boring university lecture that feels completely endless.

quizSentence: The three-hour graduation ceremony felt interminable in the hot sun.
alternateExampleSentence: We waited in an interminable line just to buy tickets for the concert.

exampleSentence: The dark clouds were a clear intimation that a severe storm was approaching.
etymology: Latin *intimatio* ‘an announcement, a hint,’ from *intimare* ‘to make known,’ from *intimus* ‘innermost, most familiar.’

memoryHook.text: Intimate - quietly sharing a very subtle, intimate hint rather than a direct statement.

quizSentence: The CEO's pause was the first intimation that the earnings report would disappoint investors.
alternateExampleSentence: He gave no intimation that he planned to resign from the company tomorrow.

exampleSentence: The long-standing border dispute remains an intractable problem for the United Nations diplomats.
etymology: Latin *intractabilis*, from *in-* + *tractare* ‘to handle, manage.’

memoryHook.text: "in-" (not) + "tract" (pull/manage) = a highly stubborn problem or person that is utterly unmanageable.

quizSentence: The border dispute proved intractable, resisting resolution across three successive administrations.
alternateExampleSentence: His intractable nature made it difficult for him to compromise with others.
