etymology: Raids stripping conquered settlements braided violence with systematic hauling away of movable wealth.
quizSentence: Viking raiders would frequently pillage coastal villages for gold and supplies.
alternateExampleSentence: History books describe how pirates would pillage the cargo of passing ships.

exampleSentence: Winning the championship was the pinnacle of her short but brilliant career.
etymology: Latin *pīna*, a spike or pin, crowned literal peaks and metaphorical crests alike.
quizSentence: Reaching the summit of Mount Everest represents the pinnacle of a climber's career.
alternateExampleSentence: The explorers reached the pinnacle of the mountain after three difficult days.

exampleSentence: The author is known for writing pithy sentences that pack great meaning.
etymology: Old English *pitha* named marrow gnawed from bone—speech pared marrow-clean of needless fat.

memoryHook.text: Pity - it's a pity his speech was so incredibly short, but it was highly dense and effective.

quizSentence: The wise philosopher was known for his pithy quotes that summarized complex truths simply.
alternateExampleSentence: He offered a pithy response that summarized the entire complex situation well.

exampleSentence: The company paid the interns a pittance for their hard daily work.
etymology: French *pitance* began humble charity crumbs; wages can still shrink to painfully thin driblets.

memoryHook.text: Pity cents - a wage so insultingly tiny they just throw you a few extra cents out of sheer pity.

quizSentence: He quit his grueling job because they only paid him a pathetic pittance.
alternateExampleSentence: He struggled to survive on the pittance he earned at the factory.

exampleSentence: The manager tried to placate the angry customer with a full refund.
etymology: Latin *placāre* smooths beasts, crowds, or gods toward soothed acceptance.

memoryHook.text: Play date - setting up a highly fun play date to completely calm down a raging, angry toddler.

quizSentence: He bought flowers to placate his partner after forgetting their anniversary dinner.
alternateExampleSentence: She bought a small gift to placate her brother after the argument.

exampleSentence: The surface of the lake remained placid throughout the calm summer morning.
etymology: *Placidus* pictured mirror-steady water echoed by moods equally unstirred.

memoryHook.text: Plastic - an ocean surface as completely smooth, calm, and unchanging as solid plastic.

quizSentence: The surface of the placid lake was as smooth and reflective as a mirror.
alternateExampleSentence: He has a placid temperament that helps him stay calm during stress.

exampleSentence: The politician filled his speech with platitude after platitude about national unity.
etymology: *Plātus* suggests flat stamping—ideas interchangeable as bland metal blanks lacking contour.

memoryHook.text: Flat attitude - offering a flat, highly overused cliché instead of real, helpful advice.

quizSentence: "Everything happens for a reason" is a tired platitude that rarely comforts a grieving person.
alternateExampleSentence: She hates every tired platitude about how money cannot buy true happiness.

exampleSentence: The brilliant surgeon received many plaudits for her successful medical research project.
etymology: Latin *plaudere* rallied crowds honoring feats with public hand-thunder down the street.

memoryHook.text: "plaudere" (clap) = receiving heavy, wildly enthusiastic applause and praise from the crowd.

quizSentence: The stunning theatrical performance earned well-deserved plaudits from the tough critics.
alternateExampleSentence: He deserves many plaudits for his brave actions during the house fire.

exampleSentence: The witness provided a plausible explanation for his presence at the scene.
etymology: Latin applause cues hint what earns brief assent—even when sharper audit waits backstage.
quizSentence: Her excuse that traffic caused her lateness was entirely plausible during rush hour.
alternateExampleSentence: That is a plausible theory, but it still requires much more evidence.

exampleSentence: The farmer enjoyed a plenitude of fresh vegetables during the harvest season.
etymology: *Plētūdō* measures brimful fullness where scarcity cannot find footing.

memoryHook.text: "plen" (full) = a completely full, massive abundance of food.

quizSentence: The autumn harvest brought a plenitude of pumpkins and squash to the farmer's market.
alternateExampleSentence: There is a plenitude of reasons to choose this beautiful vacation spot.

exampleSentence: The website offers a plethora of resources for students learning new languages.
etymology: Medical Greek *plethṓrē* warned blood—or rhetoric—flooded perilously beyond healthy balance.

memoryHook.text: Plaza full - a plaza completely full of a massive overabundance of items.

quizSentence: The huge buffet offered a plethora of dessert options, from cheesecake to chocolate fondue.
alternateExampleSentence: She faced a plethora of choices while shopping for a new laptop.

exampleSentence: The pliable leather is easy to shape into custom handmade leather shoes.
etymology: French *plier* bends yielding material—or temperament—with elastic give.

memoryHook.text: Ply - like plywood, it bends easily and is highly flexible.

quizSentence: The hot glass is pliable and can be stretched into beautiful, delicate shapes.
alternateExampleSentence: He has a pliable personality that makes him an easy team player.

exampleSentence: The story had a poignant ending that brought the audience to tears.
etymology: Latin *pungere*, prick, lengthened sweet pain memory recognizes cleanly.

memoryHook.text: Point - a sad memory so sharp it feels like it points straight into your heart.

quizSentence: The old photograph of his late grandfather brought back a poignant rush of memories.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt a poignant sense of loss when she left her home.

definition: A strong verbal or written attack
exampleSentence: The professor wrote a sharp polemic against the modern education reform bills.
etymology: Greek *pólemos*, war, armed rhetoric as argumentative campaigning rather than joint inquiry.

memoryHook.text: Polemic = debate weaponized: a blistering attack essay or speech against a person or idea.

quizSentence: The angry journalist published a fiery polemic condemning the city's corrupt mayor.
alternateExampleSentence: He is known for publishing a polemic in the local newspaper today.

exampleSentence: The dark clouds were a portent of the coming winter storm.
etymology: Latin *portentum* ‘sign, omen,’ from *portendere* ‘to stretch forth as a warning,’ from *por-* (variant of *prae-*) + *tendere* ‘to stretch.’

memoryHook.text: Important - a highly important sign that something terrible is about to happen.

quizSentence: A black cat crossing your path is considered an unlucky portent in many cultures.
alternateExampleSentence: The sudden crash in stock prices is a portent of market instability.

etymology: Latin postulare ('to demand, assume')

senses.definition: To assume as true for the sake of argument or theory
senses.exampleSentence: The essay postulates that readers already accept the author's definition of fairness.

senses.definition: Something taken as self-evident or assumed without proof
senses.exampleSentence: The entire proof rests on a postulate that critics say is outdated.

memoryHook.text: Postulate = put forward a starting claim before you prove the rest.

quizSentence: Economists postulate a link between trust and investment, then test it empirically.
exampleSentence: Scientists postulate that the universe began with a sudden and grand explosion.
alternateExampleSentence: He started his logic proof by asking us to postulate that the lines are parallel.
definition: To suggest as a given fact

exampleSentence: The hikers carried only potable water to stay healthy on the trail.
etymology: Latin *potāre*, drink, judged liquids fit—or harmless—for human throats.

memoryHook.text: Pot - water safe enough to boil in a pot and drink.

quizSentence: The hikers carried a filtration straw to ensure the river water was safe and potable.
alternateExampleSentence: The village depends on the local well for its supply of potable water.

exampleSentence: The wealthy potentate ruled the small kingdom with a firm, absolute hand.
etymology: *Potēns*, mighty, backed rulers commanding outsized fiat over subjects.

memoryHook.text: "potent" (powerful) = an incredibly powerful ruler or dictator.

quizSentence: The arrogant potentate demanded that his subjects bow whenever he entered the room.
alternateExampleSentence: Every foreign potentate was invited to the royal wedding at the palace.

exampleSentence: He offered a pragmatic solution that fixed the broken engine quickly.
etymology: Greek *prāgmatikós* tethered ideals to workable deeds outsiders can inspect.
quizSentence: Policy makers chose a pragmatic solution that prioritized immediate economic recovery over ideology.
alternateExampleSentence: The business owner is a pragmatic leader who prioritizes profit and growth.

exampleSentence: The hikers stood at the edge of the precipice looking down below.
etymology: *Praeceps*, headlong, aligned dizzy literal lips with perilous metaphorical thresholds.

memoryHook.text: Press of ice - standing dangerously on the very edge of a massive cliff of ice.

quizSentence: The mountain goat stood fearlessly on the very edge of the rocky precipice.
alternateExampleSentence: The country was standing on the precipice of a major financial crash.

etymology: Latin praecipitare, from praeceps ('headlong')

senses.definition: To cause something to happen suddenly
senses.exampleSentence: Trade disputes precipitated a sharp drop in consumer confidence.

senses.definition: To cause a substance to separate from a solution (chemistry)
senses.exampleSentence: Cooling the mixture caused salts to precipitate from the liquid.

memoryHook.text: Precipitate = headlong — events fall fast when you precipitate them.

quizSentence: A single miscalculation can precipitate a chain of failures in complex systems.
exampleSentence: The sudden increase in oil prices will precipitate a rise in inflation.
alternateExampleSentence: Her decision to quit will precipitate a change in company leadership today.

exampleSentence: The loud noise from the street will preclude any chance of sleep.
etymology: *Praecludere* shut doors preemptively on routes dissent might wander.

memoryHook.text: "pre-" (before) + "cludere" (shut) = shut the door before it happens, making it impossible.

quizSentence: A prior felony conviction will preclude you from purchasing a firearm in this state.
alternateExampleSentence: The strict rules preclude the use of mobile phones during the exam.

exampleSentence: The precocious child read advanced novels before starting the first grade today.
etymology: *Praecox*, ripening early, praises—or worries—skills ahead of birthdays.

memoryHook.text: Latin praecox ("cooked before"): a child whose mind has "cooked" ahead of schedule.

quizSentence: The precocious five-year-old could already play complex Mozart sonatas on the piano.
alternateExampleSentence: She is a precocious musician who plays the piano like a professional.

exampleSentence: He has a strong predilection for reading mystery novels during the winter.
etymology: French *prédilection*, from Latin *praediligere* ‘to prefer,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *diligere* ‘to esteem, choose.’

memoryHook.text: Pre-direction - you lean in a pre-direction because you have a strong preference for it.

quizSentence: He has a strong predilection for spicy food and puts hot sauce on everything.
alternateExampleSentence: The scientist has a predilection for studying the behavior of forest insects.

exampleSentence: The preponderance of the evidence suggests the defendant is innocent of the crime.
etymology: *Praeponderāre* sank scales under one decisive mass rivals could not outweigh.

memoryHook.text: "pre-" (before) + "ponder" (weigh) = an amount so massive it outweighs everything else.

quizSentence: A preponderance of circumstantial evidence led the jury to a guilty verdict.
alternateExampleSentence: A large preponderance of the voters support the new tax reform plan.

exampleSentence: He made a prepossessing appearance during his first day at the company.
etymology: From *pre-* + *possess*; originally ‘occupy the mind beforehand,’ hence ‘impress favorably at first meeting.’

memoryHook.text: Possess - someone so attractive you want to possess their attention.

quizSentence: The real estate agent showed us a prepossessing Victorian home with a wrap-around porch.
alternateExampleSentence: The house has a prepossessing design that attracts many potential buyers today.

exampleSentence: The unusual silence in the woods did presage the coming violent storm.
etymology: French *presage*, from Latin *praesagium* ‘foreboding,’ from *praesagire* ‘to forebode,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *sagire* ‘to perceive keenly’ (cf. *sagax* ‘keen’).

memoryHook.text: "pre-" (before) + "sage" (wise) = wise intuition that reads what is coming before it arrives.

quizSentence: The sudden drop in barometric pressure is often a presage to a severe thunderstorm.
alternateExampleSentence: The positive market data might presage a period of strong economic growth.

exampleSentence: The author was prescient in predicting the rise of modern digital technology.
etymology: Latin *praescientia* or adjective *praesciens*, from *praescire* ‘to know beforehand,’ from *prae-* ‘before’ + *scire* ‘to know.’

memoryHook.text: "pre-" (before) + "sci" (know) = knowing what will happen before it does.

quizSentence: Her prescient warning about the housing market crash saved her clients millions of dollars.
alternateExampleSentence: She made a prescient decision to sell her shares before the crash.

exampleSentence: The doctor decided to prescribe a strong antibiotic for the throat infection.
etymology: *Praescribere* inked authoritative rules—from judges or physicians—for others to follow.
quizSentence: The doctor will prescribe a strong course of antibiotics to clear up your infection.
alternateExampleSentence: The school rules prescribe the exact uniform students must wear every day.

exampleSentence: It was presumptuous of him to assume he would get the promotion.
etymology: *Praesūmere*, take beforehand, leapt claims resting on swagger before proofs earned footing.
quizSentence: It is presumptuous to assume that all voters share the same political priorities.
alternateExampleSentence: She made a presumptuous comment about his personal life during the lunch.

exampleSentence: He kept up the pretense of being rich to impress his peers.
etymology: *Praetendere* stretched cover—fabric or manners—between raw truth and public face.
quizSentence: He kept up the pretense of being wealthy by renting expensive sports cars.
alternateExampleSentence: She used the pretense of work to leave the dinner party early.

exampleSentence: The scientists discovered a primeval forest hidden deep in the frozen north.
etymology: Latin *primaevus* ‘of the first age,’ from *primus* ‘first’ + *aevum* ‘age’—belonging to earliest times.

memoryHook.text: "prim" (first) + "ev" (age) = relating to the very first, ancient ages of the earth.

quizSentence: The hikers ventured deep into the dark, primeval forest where few humans ever go.
alternateExampleSentence: The story takes place in a primeval time before modern civilization began.

exampleSentence: The family suffered through extreme privation during the long economic depression today.
etymology: *Privāre* peeled essentials away until gnawing scarcity surfaced in daily living.

memoryHook.text: Deprive - a state of extreme poverty where you are deprived of basic needs.

quizSentence: The refugees suffered extreme privation during the harsh, freezing winter months.
alternateExampleSentence: He grew up in privation but later built a wealthy and stable life.

exampleSentence: The judge is known for her high probity and her fair rulings.
etymology: *Probus*, upright, resisted backstage deals rumor could corrode.

memoryHook.text: Probe - a politician whose morals are so flawless they withstand the deepest legal probe.

quizSentence: The judge is a woman of absolute probity who has never taken a bribe.
alternateExampleSentence: He acted with moral probity even when it was difficult to do.

exampleSentence: She has a natural proclivity for solving difficult puzzles and math problems.
etymology: Latin *proclivitas* ‘a sloping forward, tendency,’ from *proclivis* ‘sloping forward,’ from *pro-* ‘forward’ + *clivus* ‘slope.’

memoryHook.text: Incline-built - in lean toward a habit; a tendency so steady it feels like gravity.

quizSentence: The mischievous puppy has a definite proclivity for chewing on expensive leather shoes.
alternateExampleSentence: He shows a proclivity for sports rather than reading books at home.

exampleSentence: The chef works hard to procure the best ingredients for his restaurant.
etymology: *Pro-* with *curāre* marched care until needed goods landed in hand.
quizSentence: The research team must procure high-quality materials to ensure valid and reliable results.
alternateExampleSentence: The army struggled to procure enough supplies for the long winter war.

exampleSentence: The comedian was fined for using profane language during his live show.
etymology: *Profānum* barred uninitiated footing around consecrated precincts—and speech that cheapens sacred things.

memoryHook.text: "pro-" (outside) + "fanum" (temple) = disrespect spoken outside the sacred space.

quizSentence: He was kicked out of the church for shouting profane insults during the sermon.
alternateExampleSentence: The sacred relics were damaged by the profane actions of the thieves.

exampleSentence: The profligate heir wasted his family fortune on parties and fast cars.
etymology: *Pro-* plus flinging verbs hurled fortunes or reputations away until ruins showed publicly.

memoryHook.text: Profits gate - throwing profits out the gate because you are reckless with money.

quizSentence: The profligate heir blew through his entire inheritance in less than two years.
alternateExampleSentence: She regretted her profligate spending habits after losing her stable career today.

exampleSentence: The accident left a profuse amount of water on the concrete floor.
etymology: *Profundere* poured blossoms, thanks, or words outward without stingy counting.

memoryHook.text: "pro-" (forth) + "fundere" (pour) = pouring forth in massive abundant quantities.

quizSentence: The marathon runner offered profuse thanks to the volunteer handing out water.
alternateExampleSentence: He offered profuse thanks for the help he received during the move.

exampleSentence: The king decided to promulgate a new law regarding trade and taxes.
etymology: *Promulgāre* marched edicts into common hearing so ignorance could scarcely plead innocence.

memoryHook.text: Prom gate - announcing new rules loudly right at the prom gate.

quizSentence: The dictator used state-run television to actively promulgate his political propaganda.
alternateExampleSentence: The university will promulgate the new schedule for all students by Monday.

exampleSentence: The gardeners propagate new plants from the cuttings of the old shrubs.
etymology: Latin *propagāre*, set slips to root, marched ideas—or lineages—outward across new ground.
quizSentence: Gardeners often propagate spider plants by clipping and planting the small offshoots.
alternateExampleSentence: The media helps to propagate the ideas of the new political party.

exampleSentence: He has a propensity for forgetting his keys when leaving the house.
etymology: *Propendēre*, hang toward something, etched reliable habits tipped one direction.

memoryHook.text: Pro pen - a writer with a natural tendency to always grab a pro pen.

quizSentence: Certain bird species have a propensity for migrating long distances during the winter.
alternateExampleSentence: The data shows a propensity for customers to buy on the weekends.

exampleSentence: The sunny morning was a propitious start to the important wedding day.
etymology: Omens counted *propitius* leaned kindly—fortune seeming to cooperate with earnest effort.

memoryHook.text: Profits - conditions look favorable; strong signs you could see big profits.

quizSentence: Finding a four-leaf clover right before the exam felt like a very propitious sign.
alternateExampleSentence: It was a propitious moment to ask for a raise in salary.

exampleSentence: The event was conducted with the utmost propriety and respect for all.
etymology: French *propriété* tightened conduct communities reward as fitting and polite.

memoryHook.text: Proper - acting with strict, perfectly proper, socially acceptable behavior.

quizSentence: The strict boarding school placed a heavy emphasis on manners and social propriety.
alternateExampleSentence: He questioned the propriety of the manager dating a junior staff member.

exampleSentence: The report was filled with prosaic details that bored the committee members.
etymology: *Prōsa* named plain marching speech instead of verse’s ornament.

memoryHook.text: Prose - writing that sounds like ordinary prose instead of poetry; dull.

quizSentence: His writing style is rather prosaic and lacks any sort of creative imagination.
alternateExampleSentence: She wanted to escape her prosaic life by moving to the city.

exampleSentence: The school decided to proscribe the use of chewing gum in class.
etymology: *Prōscrībere* nailed names up publicly—to exile foes or outlaw trade legibly.

memoryHook.text: "pro-" (before) + "scribere" (write) = write a name on a list to legally forbid someone.

quizSentence: The city council voted to strictly proscribe the use of fireworks within city limits.
alternateExampleSentence: The law serves to proscribe the sale of illegal goods in stores.

exampleSentence: The actor is a protean talent who plays many different roles well.
etymology: Proteus-shaped myth lent nimble versatility—many masks, one crafty knack.

memoryHook.text: From Proteus, Greek sea god who changed shape to escape - versatile, constantly shifting.

quizSentence: The protean actor transformed into six different characters in one play.
alternateExampleSentence: The situation is protean and changes rapidly based on the newest data.

exampleSentence: The soldier demonstrated great prowess on the battlefield during the fierce fight.
etymology: Old French *prouesse* crowned deeds whose skill or daring auditors retold.
quizSentence: The samurai was renowned throughout the province for his unmatched martial prowess.
alternateExampleSentence: She is famous for her prowess as one of the city's best chess players.

exampleSentence: He showed great prudence by saving most of his income for retirement.
etymology: *Prūdēns*, knowing ahead, spared rash burns by tallying fallout before leaping.

memoryHook.text: Prude - so careful and cautious with choices that people joke you are a prude.

quizSentence: Displaying financial prudence, she saved twenty percent of every paycheck she earned.
alternateExampleSentence: Fiscal prudence dictates that the council should avoid excessive spending during economic uncertainty.

exampleSentence: The magazine was criticized for its prurient interest in the celebrity's life.
etymology: *Prūrīre* itched morally—eyes craving illicit detail coyly disguised as curiosity.
quizSentence: The scandalous magazine was banned from the school library for its prurient content.
alternateExampleSentence: He felt a prurient curiosity about the scandalous events at the party.

exampleSentence: The students were punished for their puerile behavior during the quiet assembly.
etymology: *Puer*, boy, tinted jokes—or logic—playground-bound despite calendars.

memoryHook.text: Pure ill - a child acting so immaturely it makes you feel purely ill.

quizSentence: Throwing spitballs at the substitute teacher is puerile behavior for a high schooler.
alternateExampleSentence: He made a puerile joke that nobody in the room found funny.

exampleSentence: The pugnacious boxer started a fight at the weigh-in before the match.
etymology: *Pugnāre*, fight, tightened reflexes awake before diplomacy cooled insults.

memoryHook.text: "pugn" (fight) = eager to argue or fight; combative energy.

quizSentence: The pugnacious little terrier would bark and snap at dogs ten times its size.
alternateExampleSentence: She has a pugnacious attitude that often leads to arguments with others.

exampleSentence: The artist was captivated by the pulchritude of the landscape at sunset.
etymology: *Pulcher*, fair or handsome, praises visible charms the public gaze notices.

memoryHook.text: Pull attitude - beauty so strong it pulls the room's attitude toward you.

quizSentence: The classical poet wrote countless sonnets praising the pulchritude of his beloved muse.
alternateExampleSentence: Her natural pulchritude made her a successful model for the luxury brand.

exampleSentence: He is punctilious about arriving exactly on time for every important meeting.
etymology: *Punctum*, point, tracked ceremony down to dotted *i*s without mercy.

memoryHook.text: Punctuation - obsessed with tiny details; corrects your text-message punctuation.

quizSentence: The punctilious hall monitor cited students if they were even thirty seconds late.
alternateExampleSentence: The librarian was punctilious regarding the organization of the rare book archive.

exampleSentence: The pungent aroma of the spicy curry filled the entire small kitchen.
etymology: *Pungere*, prick, spiced odors or sarcasm into edges refusing blur.

memoryHook.text: Punch in - a smell so sharp it punches you in the nose.

quizSentence: The pungent aroma of roasting garlic drifted from the Italian restaurant's kitchen.
alternateExampleSentence: He disliked the pungent smell of the chemicals used in the factory.

exampleSentence: The judge issued a punitive fine to discourage further reckless driving behavior.
etymology: *Pūnīre* loaded judgments—courtroom or parental—with deliberate sting.

memoryHook.text: "pun" (punish) = intended to punish, not merely to correct.

quizSentence: The judge imposed harsh punitive damages to ensure the corporation never repeated the offense.
alternateExampleSentence: The company took punitive measures against the employees who broke the rules.

exampleSentence: The hikers got stuck in a muddy quagmire after the heavy rains.
etymology: Quaking mire swallowed boots—plans likewise bog down with sucking inertia.

memoryHook.text: Quack mire - a duck stuck in a muddy, complicated swamp.

quizSentence: The military intervention quickly turned into a messy political quagmire with no easy exit.
alternateExampleSentence: The legal case became a quagmire of endless paperwork and conflicting evidence.

exampleSentence: That quaint inn retains its rustic charm despite the modern renovations nearby.
etymology: English gathered crafty-knowing senses into amiably antiquated charms that feel studiedly darling.
quizSentence: Tourists wander through the quaint village streets admiring the eighteenth-century colonial architecture.
alternateExampleSentence: The town is famous for its quaint shops and beautiful flower gardens.

etymology: Medieval Latin qualificare, from Latin qualis ('of what kind') + facere ('make')

senses.definition: To limit or modify a claim
senses.exampleSentence: The author qualifies the headline by adding important exceptions in the next sentence.

senses.definition: To meet the requirements for something
senses.exampleSentence: Only three candidates qualified for the final round of interviews.

memoryHook.text: On the SAT, qualify often means add limits — like adding qualities that narrow a claim.

quizSentence: A good thesis qualifies broad claims so they match the evidence presented.
exampleSentence: You must qualify your statement by providing more evidence for the claim.
alternateExampleSentence: She failed to qualify for the final round of the Olympic race.

exampleSentence: He was in a quandary about which college offer to finally accept.
etymology: English (16th c.), origin uncertain; often linked to Latin *quando* ‘when,’ suggesting inability to decide *when* or how to choose among options.

memoryHook.text: Wandering - wandering lost because you are stuck in painful uncertainty.

quizSentence: Choosing between the high-paying job and the fulfilling job left her in a quandary.
alternateExampleSentence: The team faced a quandary when their main project leader suddenly quit.

exampleSentence: The police used calm words to quell the anger of the crowd.
etymology: Old English *cwellan* killed outright; gentler English still crushes tumults flat.

memoryHook.text: Quiet well - force quiets the riot well.

quizSentence: The riot police used tear gas to quickly quell the violent uprising in the square.
alternateExampleSentence: She took medicine to quell the persistent pain in her tired legs.

exampleSentence: The querulous customer complained about every minor detail of her hotel room.
etymology: *Querī*, complain, lengthened chronic grumble pitched at petty irritants.

memoryHook.text: Quarrel - whining and always ready to pick a petty quarrel.

quizSentence: The querulous customer complained about the lighting, the temperature, and the music.
alternateExampleSentence: His querulous tone made it very hard to have a peaceful conversation.

exampleSentence: He had a quixotic dream of building a city on the moon.
etymology: Cervantes’ knight lent noble fantasy lunging at problems windmills shrug off.

memoryHook.text: Don Quixote charged windmills as giants - idealistic but impractical.

quizSentence: His quixotic quest to build a time machine out of spare microwave parts ultimately failed.
alternateExampleSentence: The plan was quixotic and failed to consider the actual financial costs.

exampleSentence: He found comfort in the quotidian tasks of cleaning his small apartment.
etymology: *Quōtīdiānus* tallied mornings stacked until spectacle thinned to numb habit.

memoryHook.text: Quota - same boring daily quota; ordinary everyday life.

quizSentence: These quotidian activities define the daily existence of residents in the quiet village.
alternateExampleSentence: The museum exhibit explores the quotidian life of people in ancient Rome.

exampleSentence: The angry customer began to rail against the unfair store return policy.
etymology: Middle English *railen* vented bitterness in prolonged, accusing speech shouted like hammered planks.

memoryHook.text: Derail - ranting so hard you try to derail the meeting.

quizSentence: The angry pundit took to the airwaves to rail against the new tax legislation.
alternateExampleSentence: She chose to rail at the injustice of the local election results.

exampleSentence: The milk had gone rancid because it was left out all night.
etymology: *Rancēre*, rank stench, spoiled oils—or alliances—past safe savor.
quizSentence: The butter left out overnight tasted rancid, so she discarded it immediately.
alternateExampleSentence: He threw away the rancid meat he found in the old refrigerator.

exampleSentence: The divorce was filled with deep rancor between the two former partners.
etymology: Latin bitterness aged slights into poison lingering after triggers cooled.

memoryHook.text: Rank core - bitter hatred sitting in the core of your heart.

quizSentence: Despite the bitter divorce, they managed to co-parent without any obvious rancor.
alternateExampleSentence: He spoke without rancor about the person who caused his job loss.

exampleSentence: The teacher quickly built a strong rapport with all of her students.
etymology: French *rapporter*, carry back, caught mirrored ease flowing both directions.
quizSentence: The friendly therapist quickly established a strong rapport with the nervous child.
alternateExampleSentence: They developed a deep rapport while working on the science project together.

exampleSentence: It was a rash decision to quit his job without another one.
etymology: Middle English *rash* ‘hasty, quick,’ perhaps akin to Old Norse *röskr* ‘bold, active’; the sense ‘reckless’ develops from undue speed.
quizSentence: Investors should avoid making rash decisions during volatile periods in the stock market.
alternateExampleSentence: She made a rash promise that she was unable to keep later.

exampleSentence: The raucous party next door kept the entire neighborhood awake until dawn.
etymology: *Raucus*, hoarse, widened ears assaulted by unmodulated peals.

memoryHook.text: Ruckus - a loud chaotic crowd making a ruckus.

quizSentence: The raucous crowd caused the speaker to pause until order could be restored.
alternateExampleSentence: The players gave a raucous cheer when the final whistle was blown.

exampleSentence: The city planned to raze the old stadium to build new apartments.
etymology: Old French *raser*, scrape smooth, left dust heaps where masonry once bristled.

memoryHook.text: Razor - bulldozing a building down as if shaved by a razor.

quizSentence: Developers plan to raze the abandoned factory to build a new shopping mall.
alternateExampleSentence: They decided to raze the house because it was not structurally safe.

exampleSentence: The manager issued a sharp rebuke to the employee for being late.
etymology: Old French *rebuchier*, thrust back, hurled corrective heat straight at faults exposed.
quizSentence: The general issued a stern rebuke to the captain for ignoring his direct orders.
alternateExampleSentence: She accepted the public rebuke with grace and promised to do better.

exampleSentence: The recalcitrant horse refused to enter the trailer for the long journey.
etymology: *Calcitrāre*, kick back, resisted orders visibly with heels dug in.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "calcitrare" (kick) = kicking back; stubbornly disobedient.

quizSentence: The recalcitrant protestors refused to vacate the building despite receiving multiple formal warnings.
alternateExampleSentence: The recalcitrant student would not complete his homework despite the strict warnings.

exampleSentence: He asked the team to recapitulate the main points of the meeting.
etymology: *Caput*, head, marched argument vertebrae retraced cleanly top-down.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (again) + "capit" (head) = revisit the main heads or points in summary.

quizSentence: The professor used the final lecture to recapitulate the essential concepts of biology.
alternateExampleSentence: She tried to recapitulate the story for her friends who missed it.

exampleSentence: It is important to reciprocate the kindness shown by your good friends.

alternateExampleSentence: The country will reciprocate the trade deal with its own economic concessions.

exampleSentence: The famous author lived a reclusive life in a cabin in woods.
etymology: *Claudere*, shut, lengthened solitude behind doors politely closed.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "clus" (shut) = shut away from the world; withdrawn.

quizSentence: The reclusive billionaire rarely left the heavily guarded walls of his mountain estate.
alternateExampleSentence: He became reclusive after the tragic accident that killed his closest friend.

etymology: *Conciliāre* reheated goodwill once ruptures cooled toward workable armistices.

senses.exampleSentence: The feuding neighbors finally reconciled when one brought the other a delicious tuna noodle casserole.

senses.definition: To make consistent with existing ideas
senses.exampleSentence: She tried to reconcile her skepticism with the UFO she had filmed.

exampleSentence: They finally managed to reconcile their differences after a very long talk.
quizSentence: It took years for the estranged brothers to finally reconcile their differences.
alternateExampleSentence: It is hard to reconcile his kind words with his mean actions.

exampleSentence: The judge is a person of high rectitude who follows the law.
etymology: *Rectus*, straight, praised spines refusing crooked bends in duty.

memoryHook.text: "rect" (straight/right) = upright, morally straight behavior.

quizSentence: The judge was a model of moral rectitude who refused every offer of a bribe.
alternateExampleSentence: He acted with moral rectitude even when it was difficult to do.

etymology: French roots of *redouter*, dread anew, crowned reputations foes hesitated to poke.

senses.exampleSentence: The fortress looked redoubtable set against a stormy sky.
The audience greeted the redoubtable speaker with a standing ovation.

memoryHook.text: Doubt - an opponent so intimidating you doubt you can win.

exampleSentence: She was a redoubtable opponent on the tennis court during the finals.
quizSentence: The heavyweight champion was a redoubtable opponent who had never lost a fight.
alternateExampleSentence: The army faced a redoubtable enemy that had advanced and modern weapons.

definition: To make a light ray bend
exampleSentence: The glass prism will refract the light into a colorful rainbow pattern.
etymology: *Frangere*, break—here light kinks obeying thresholds between media.
quizSentence: A prism will refract white light into a beautiful spectrum of rainbow colors.
alternateExampleSentence: Water can refract light to make a straight stick look slightly bent.

exampleSentence: They decided to refurbish the old hotel to attract more young tourists.
etymology: *Furbir*, polish, marched tired interiors toward freshened gleam methodically.
quizSentence: She bought an old dresser at a garage sale and planned to refurbish it.
alternateExampleSentence: She works to refurbish antique furniture that she finds at local sales.

definition: To logically prove a statement wrong
exampleSentence: The scientist provided evidence to refute the claim made by the critic.
etymology: *Refūtāre*, beat back, collapsed claims evidence refused sustaining.
quizSentence: The scientist published a comprehensive paper to definitively refute the flawed theory.
alternateExampleSentence: He could not refute the facts presented by the detective in court.

etymology: *Gurges*, whirlpool, pictured stomachs cancelling southbound visas mechanically.

senses.exampleSentence: Feeling sick, Chuck regurgitated his dinner.
Margaret rushed through the test, regurgitating all of the facts she’d memorized an hour earlier.

definition: To casually throw up swallowed food
exampleSentence: The bird will regurgitate food to feed its chicks in the nest.
quizSentence: The mother bird will chew the worms and regurgitate them for her hungry chicks.
alternateExampleSentence: He can regurgitate facts for the test but does not understand them.

etymology: Latin reiterare, from re- ('again') + iterare ('to repeat')
exampleSentence: She had to reiterate her point because the audience was very noisy.

memoryHook.text: "Re-" (again) + "iterate" (repeat) = say it again on purpose.

quizSentence: The coach reiterated the same strategy because the team kept drifting off plan.
alternateExampleSentence: The manager decided to reiterate the safety rules to all new workers.

etymology: *Relegāre*, send away quietly, parked people—or topics—on subsidiary tables.

senses.definition: To assign to the proper place
senses.exampleSentence: At the astrology conference, Simon was relegated to the Scorpio room.

senses.definition: To assign to an inferior place
senses.exampleSentence: After spilling wine on a guest, the waiter was relegated to the worst shift.

memoryHook.text: Relocate - moved down to a lower, less important rank or role.

exampleSentence: The team was relegated to a lower division after a poor season.
quizSentence: After his terrible mistake, the manager decided to relegate him to a desk job.
alternateExampleSentence: He was relegated to a minor position after the corporate merger failed.

exampleSentence: She does not relish the idea of moving to a new city.
etymology: Old French *releschier*, lick again, tied keen pleasure on the tongue—or in a contest—doubled willingly.

memoryHook.text: Hot dog relish - savoring a moment the way you savor relish on a hot dog.

quizSentence: Competitive runners relish the challenge of competing against world-class athletes during finals.
alternateExampleSentence: He eats his favorite meal with great relish after a long day.

exampleSentence: The students attended a remedial course to improve their basic math skills.
etymology: *Remedium* rehealed cracks ordinary pacing skimmed past carelessly.
quizSentence: The struggling student was placed in a remedial math class to learn the basics.
alternateExampleSentence: She received remedial training to help her use the new software program.

exampleSentence: The security guard was remiss in his duty to lock the gates.
etymology: *Remittere* flagged duties dropped slackly instead of carried through.

memoryHook.text: Miss - careless with duty so you miss the deadline entirely.

quizSentence: Prosecutors would be remiss to ignore such compelling forensic evidence in this case.
alternateExampleSentence: The committee was remiss in failing to notify stakeholders about the upcoming meeting.

etymology: *Novāre*, renew, marched studs, conduit, cladding toward code-worthy soundness.

senses.exampleSentence: The renovated antique candelabra looked as good as new.

senses.definition: To enlarge and make prettier, especially a house
senses.exampleSentence: After getting renovated, the house was twice as big and much more attractive.

exampleSentence: They spent all summer trying to renovate the kitchen in their house.
quizSentence: Civic leaders plan to renovate the abandoned theater to restore its former grandeur.
alternateExampleSentence: The city will renovate the library to provide more space for readers.

definition: The honorable condition of being famous
exampleSentence: The author achieved great renown after his first novel became a bestseller.
etymology: *Renomer*, name again, amplified reputations echoing willingly in public ear.
quizSentence: The opera singer achieved international renown for her breathtaking soprano voice.
alternateExampleSentence: The brilliant surgeon achieved global renown for inventing a life-saving cardiovascular operation.

exampleSentence: His renunciation of the crown shocked the entire nation during the ceremony.
etymology: *Renūntiāre*, announce backward, relinquished titles or pleasures aloud without clawbacks.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "nuntiare" (announce) = announce you formally renounce a claim.

quizSentence: The ascetic monk's vows included a total renunciation of all personal wealth.
alternateExampleSentence: The monk signed a renunciation of all his wealth and material goods.

exampleSentence: The repentant criminal asked for forgiveness from the family he had hurt.
etymology: *Paenitēre* dragged conscience toward apologies felt—not merely staged.
quizSentence: The repentant sinner prayed deeply for forgiveness at the altar.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt repentant for the way she treated her sister last week.

exampleSentence: The book is replete with stories about the hero's many great adventures.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (again) + "plere" (fill) = filled back up to maximum capacity.

quizSentence: The Thanksgiving table was replete with turkey, stuffing, and three kinds of pie.
alternateExampleSentence: The forest is replete with different types of wild animals and birds.

definition: To gently rest or lie down
exampleSentence: She found repose in the quiet garden after a busy day today.
etymology: *Repausāre*, pause again, banked stillness limbs finally spared.

memoryHook.text: Pose - lying still in a calm resting pose.

quizSentence: The exhausted traveler found a moment of quiet repose in the shady park.
alternateExampleSentence: The weary travelers stopped to repose near the cool and flowing river.

exampleSentence: His reprehensible conduct toward his employees led to his immediate firing today.
etymology: *Reprehendere*, seize back fault, tagged conduct auditors could rebuke plainly.

memoryHook.text: Apprehend - behavior so bad the police should apprehend you.

quizSentence: Stealing from a charity donation box is an absolutely reprehensible act.
alternateExampleSentence: It is reprehensible to treat innocent animals with such cruelty and pain.

exampleSentence: The prisoner received a brief reprieve from the judge before the execution.
etymology: Anglo-French respite habits stalled execution’s blade just long enough to draw another breath.
quizSentence: The sudden rainstorm offered a welcome reprieve from the blistering summer heat.
alternateExampleSentence: The clear weather was a welcome reprieve from the weeks of rain.

exampleSentence: She looked at him with reproach for missing the important family event.
etymology: Old French *reprocher* thrust fault intimately close—the burn of visible blame lingering.

memoryHook.text: Roach - scolding the owner for a roach in the kitchen.

quizSentence: Her spotless professional record is beyond any reproach.
alternateExampleSentence: He suffered the reproach of his peers for his failure to act.

exampleSentence: The village reprobate spent his days drinking and causing trouble for neighbors.
etymology: Latin *reprobare* (‘test and reject’) labeled persons judged unworthy of honor.

memoryHook.text: Probation - a scoundrel who failed probation and slid back to trouble.

quizSentence: The old reprobate spent his pension gambling at the dog track and drinking cheap whiskey.
alternateExampleSentence: The judge sentenced the habitual reprobate to five years in state prison.

exampleSentence: The teacher had to reprove the student for speaking during the test.
etymology: Latin *reprobare* sharpened into speech that faults conduct without indulgence.

memoryHook.text: Prove - scolding someone until they prove they will not repeat it.

quizSentence: A good mentor will gently reprove a student's mistakes without crushing their confidence.
alternateExampleSentence: She did not want to reprove her friend despite the obvious mistake.

exampleSentence: The company had to repudiate the false claims made by the executive.
etymology: Latin *repudiare* refused a spouse or debt; English widened the divorce to ideas.

memoryHook.text: Refuse it - reject a claim and refuse it as false.

quizSentence: The company quickly issued a statement to repudiate the racist comments made by its CEO.
alternateExampleSentence: He chose to repudiate his former beliefs and join a new party.

etymology: Latin *repellere* (‘push back’) describes shields and armies forcing an advance away.

senses.exampleSentence: Antisocial Annie tried to repulse people by neglecting to brush her teeth.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "puls" (drive) = drive an attack or feeling back.

exampleSentence: The army managed to repulse the enemy attack at the castle wall.
quizSentence: The defenders poured boiling oil over the castle walls to repulse the invaders.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt a strong sense of repulse when she saw the mess.

exampleSentence: The reputable firm provides excellent financial advice to all of its clients.

quizSentence: Only reputable sources should be cited when writing an academic paper on history.
alternateExampleSentence: You should hire a reputable company to fix the roof of house.

exampleSentence: The office will submit a requisition for new computers for the staff.
etymology: Latin *requisitio* claims stores or people by rule when need is certified.
quizSentence: The office manager submitted a formal requisition for a new high-speed printer.
alternateExampleSentence: He signed the official requisition to secure the supplies for the camp.

exampleSentence: The company decided to rescind the job offer after the background check.
etymology: Latin *rescindere* (‘cut off’) cancels orders as if severing a rope.

memoryHook.text: Send back - cancel an order and send it entirely back.

quizSentence: The university will rescind your acceptance letter if you fail your final high school exams.
alternateExampleSentence: The governor chose to rescind the order that closed all public parks.

exampleSentence: The resilient plant continued to grow despite the lack of direct light.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "salire" (jump) = bounce back after a hard hit.

quizSentence: Rubber is a highly resilient material that snaps back into shape after being stretched.
alternateExampleSentence: She is a resilient person who recovers quickly from major life setbacks.

exampleSentence: He remained resolute in his decision to finish the degree this year.
etymology: Latin *resolvere* binds one sense ‘loosen’ and another ‘set firm,’ here the latter.

memoryHook.text: Resolution - a firm resolution you refuse to break.

quizSentence: She remained resolute in her decision to climb the mountain without oxygen.
alternateExampleSentence: The soldiers were resolute as they prepared for the upcoming difficult battle.

etymology: Noun *resolve* is will gathered tight enough to hold a costly course.

senses.exampleSentence: Sarah and Emma resolved their differences and shook hands.
Lady Macbeth resolved to whip her husband into shape.

exampleSentence: They managed to resolve their legal dispute through a formal mediation process.
quizSentence: The couple went to therapy hoping to successfully resolve their marital conflicts.
alternateExampleSentence: He showed strong resolve while training for the long distance marathon race.

exampleSentence: The vacation provided a brief respite from the stress of his work.
etymology: Anglo-French *respit* granted delays; English keeps the merciful pause.

memoryHook.text: Rest bit - a badly needed rest bit during an exhausting hike.

quizSentence: Finding a cool, air-conditioned cafe provided a brief respite from the sweltering humidity.
alternateExampleSentence: The shady tree offered a cool respite from the hot summer sun.

exampleSentence: The palace was resplendent with gold decorations and many bright crystal lights.
etymology: Latin *resplendere* (‘shine forth’) lights cliffs, cloth, or virtue with outer glow.

memoryHook.text: Splendid - shining so bright it looks splendid.

quizSentence: The peacock looked resplendent displaying its bright blue and green tail feathers.
alternateExampleSentence: She looked resplendent in her elegant dress at the annual charity ball.

exampleSentence: The court ordered the thief to pay restitution to all his victims.
etymology: Latin *restituere* sets stolen goods—or justice—back upon their proper shelf.

memoryHook.text: Restore - paying or working to restore what was stolen or broken.

quizSentence: The vandal was ordered by the court to pay full restitution for the broken window.
alternateExampleSentence: He offered an apology and restitution to fix the damage he caused.

exampleSentence: The restive crowd waited impatiently for the doors of the stadium opening.
etymology: Latin *restare* (‘stand still’) ironically names crowds impatient under restraint.

memoryHook.text: Restless - a horse tense and restless, unable to stand still.

quizSentence: The restive horse pawed the ground nervously as the thunderstorm approached.
alternateExampleSentence: The horse became restive when it heard the loud noise outside stall.

exampleSentence: The company had to retract the false claims made in the advertisement.
etymology: Latin *retractare* handles a claim again until it is pulled inside.

memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "tract" (pull) = formally pull back a false statement.

quizSentence: The newspaper was forced to retract the defamatory article and print a formal apology.
alternateExampleSentence: She tried to retract her harsh words after seeing he was upset.

exampleSentence: The team decided to revel in their victory after the final game.
etymology: Old French *reveler* tied noisy festivity to disorder that feels like harmless riot.

memoryHook.text: Rebel - the rebel parties all night after a wild win.

quizSentence: The victorious football team went out to revel in their championship win.
alternateExampleSentence: They gathered to revel in the success of the new product launch.

exampleSentence: The students revere the professor for his many contributions to the science.
etymology: Latin *revereri* (‘fear in awe’) lowers voices before grave excellence.

memoryHook.text: Reverend - deep respectful awe, like toward a reverend.

quizSentence: In many cultures, children are taught to deeply revere and respect their elders.
alternateExampleSentence: Many people revere the memory of the leader who changed the nation.

exampleSentence: The state decided to revoke his license after the serious traffic accident.
