memoryHook.text: "re-" (back) + "voc" (call) = call a license back and cancel it.

quizSentence: The judge will immediately revoke your driver's license if you get another speeding ticket.
alternateExampleSentence: The club will revoke your membership if you break the house rules.

exampleSentence: The poet began to rhapsodize about the beauty of the spring season.
etymology: Greek *rhapsōidía* stitched song; English verbs lift speech toward lyric height.

memoryHook.text: Rap song - praising or ranting with the heat of a passionate rap.

quizSentence: He would frequently rhapsodize about the incredible beauty of the Tuscan countryside.
alternateExampleSentence: She likes to rhapsodize about the amazing food she ate in Italy.

exampleSentence: The movie was banned due to its ribald jokes and offensive content.
etymology: Old French *ribaud* named wandering soldiers whose jokes stayed loud and coarse.

memoryHook.text: Rip bawd - coarse, lewd humor ripped from the tavern; indecent and deliberately shocking.

quizSentence: The drunken sailors entertained themselves by singing ribald songs at the tavern.
alternateExampleSentence: He told a ribald story that made the entire room feel uncomfortable.

exampleSentence: The report claims the industry is rife with fraud and illegal activity.
etymology: Old English *rīfe* meant copious growth; rumor and error may run equally *rife*.

memoryHook.text: Rifle - an area rife with rifles; common and dangerous.

quizSentence: The corrupt police department was rife with bribery and scandal.
alternateExampleSentence: The dark forest is rife with dangerous animals and thick, tangled vines.

exampleSentence: He sat quietly to ruminate on the choices he made last year.
etymology: Latin *ruminari* (‘chew cud’) borrowed the cow’s patience for slow thought.

memoryHook.text: Latin ruminare (chew the cud) - chew the same thought over and over.

quizSentence: She needed several days to ruminate on the job offer before responding.
alternateExampleSentence: You should ruminate on the proposal before you give a final answer.

exampleSentence: The spy used a clever ruse to enter the heavily guarded building.
etymology: Old French *ruser* (‘dodge’) folded feints into plans that sidestep straight fight.

memoryHook.text: Confuse - a trick meant to confuse the enemy.

quizSentence: The Trojan Horse was a brilliant military ruse used to sneak soldiers into the city.
alternateExampleSentence: Spies employed a clever ruse to infiltrate the enemy base without attracting attention.

exampleSentence: The movie featured a saccharine plot that was far too sentimental today.
etymology: Latin *saccharum* (‘sugar’) sugared tone until listeners feel their teeth ache.

memoryHook.text: Saccharin - sweetness as fake as the sweetener; cloying and artificial.

quizSentence: The movie's saccharine soundtrack ruined the serious tone of the dramatic scene.
alternateExampleSentence: She offered a saccharine smile that did not seem at all genuine.

exampleSentence: The historical site is considered sacrosanct by the local native population today.
etymology: Latin *sacer* (‘sacred’) plus *sanctus* marks zones mockery must not enter.

memoryHook.text: "sacro" (sacred) + "sanct" (holy) = doubly sacred; not to be violated.

quizSentence: To the devout monk, the ancient temple texts were considered entirely sacrosanct.
alternateExampleSentence: He treated his private time as sacrosanct and never accepted work calls.

exampleSentence: The leader is admired for the sagacity he shows in difficult times.
etymology: Latin *sagax* (‘keen of scent’) praised trackers—then judges—who notice slightest traces.

memoryHook.text: Sage - the deep judgment of a wise sage.

quizSentence: The village elder was sought out by many for his remarkable wisdom and sagacity.
alternateExampleSentence: Her sagacity helped the team avoid a major mistake on the project.

exampleSentence: The salient point of his argument was that the plan was expensive.
etymology: Latin *salīre* (‘leap’) makes the fact that jumps before quieter details.

memoryHook.text: Sail - the main sail is the most salient feature on the boat.

quizSentence: The consultant summarized the most salient points of the lengthy financial report.
alternateExampleSentence: She focused on the salient facts while writing her final research paper.

exampleSentence: He offered a friendly salutation to his neighbor while walking the dog.
etymology: Latin *salūtātiō* from *salūs* (‘health, safety’) begins speech with goodwill’s bow.
quizSentence: She ended her formal letter with a polite salutation and her handwritten signature.
alternateExampleSentence: The formal letter began with the traditional salutation to the committee chair.

exampleSentence: The herbal salve helped to heal the burn on her small finger.
etymology: Old English *sealf* and Latin *salvum* met in soothing salves for skin and shame.

memoryHook.text: Solve - ointment that soothes a burn and solves the worst of the sting.

quizSentence: Aloe vera gel is an excellent cooling salve for a painful sunburn.
alternateExampleSentence: He used kind words as a salve for her hurt and feelings.

exampleSentence: The sanctimonious preacher lectured everyone while failing to follow his own rules.
etymology: Latin *sanctimonia* feigned holiness loudly worn for show.

memoryHook.text: "sanct" (holy) = acting holier-than-thou to claim moral high ground.

quizSentence: The sanctimonious politician lectured others on morality while secretly taking bribes.
alternateExampleSentence: She hated the sanctimonious attitude of the people at the private club.

exampleSentence: He remains sanguine about the company's chances of success this coming year.
etymology: Latin *sanguis* (‘blood’) flushed healthy confidence into European medical metaphor.

memoryHook.text: Latin sanguis (blood): old humor theory tied blood tone to confident optimism.

quizSentence: Despite the poor economic forecast, the CEO remained sanguine about the company's future.
alternateExampleSentence: She is sanguine despite the many challenges she faces in her life.

exampleSentence: A small snack was enough to satiate his hunger before the dinner.
etymology: Latin *satiare* packs the belly—or audience—past any polite stopping point.

memoryHook.text: Satisfy ate - you ate enough to satisfy brutal hunger.

quizSentence: An elaborate banquet failed to satiate the desires of the demanding historical monarch.
alternateExampleSentence: He could never satiate his curiosity about how the universe was formed.

exampleSentence: The critic wrote a scathing review of the newly opened action movie.
etymology: Old Norse *skaða* (‘hurt’) became English *scathe*, then adjective *scathing*.

memoryHook.text: Scalding - a review that scalds; harshly critical.

quizSentence: The theater critic wrote a scathing review that essentially closed the play on opening night.
alternateExampleSentence: He received a scathing lecture from his boss for the late report.

exampleSentence: The host gave a scintillating performance that kept the audience very engaged.
etymology: Latin *scintilla* (‘spark’) leaps from steel, stars, and rapid minds alike.

memoryHook.text: Sparkling - wit and energy sparkling like champagne bubbles.

quizSentence: The brilliant astrophysicist delivered a scintillating lecture on the formation of black holes.
alternateExampleSentence: She wore a scintillating diamond necklace that sparkled brightly in the light.

exampleSentence: The accountant is scrupulous about tracking every single penny of the budget.
etymology: Latin *scrupulus* (‘small sharp stone’) stood for pebbles troubling conscience’s shoe.

memoryHook.text: Scrap - checking every scrap because honesty demands perfect detail.

quizSentence: The scrupulous accountant caught an error of three cents in the ledger.
alternateExampleSentence: He made a scrupulous effort to follow every rule of the game.

etymology: Latin scrutinium, from scrutari ('to search through rubbish carefully')
exampleSentence: The detective began to scrutinize the evidence found at the crime scene.

memoryHook.text: Scrutinize = search through every detail as if hunting for hidden trash.

quizSentence: Voters scrutinize campaign promises when economic data tell a different story.
alternateExampleSentence: You should scrutinize the contract before you sign it with the firm.

exampleSentence: The politician was sued for spreading scurrilous lies about his opponent today.
etymology: Latin *scurrilis* (‘buffoonish’) from *scurra* (‘city idler’) sank speech into mud.

memoryHook.text: Scurry - ugly rumors that make the target want to scurry away.

quizSentence: The tabloid printed a scurrilous rumor about the actress having a secret love child.
alternateExampleSentence: She refused to read the scurrilous gossip posted on the internet site.

exampleSentence: The sedentary lifestyle led to several health problems for the busy accountant.
etymology: Latin *sedēre* (‘sit’) marks lives that seldom rise to stretch or roam.

memoryHook.text: "sed" (sit) = life spent sitting; little movement.

quizSentence: Office workers who lead a sedentary lifestyle are encouraged to exercise more often.
alternateExampleSentence: He spends his sedentary workday sitting in front of a computer screen.

exampleSentence: The soldiers used semaphore to communicate across the battlefield without any noise.
etymology: Greek *sēma* (‘sign’) and *phorein* (‘carry’) named cloth alphabets waved at sea.
quizSentence: The sailor used bright orange flags to send a semaphore message to the ship.
alternateExampleSentence: He signaled the ship using a simple semaphore system from the beach.

exampleSentence: This seminal work on public health changed how nations track viral disease outbreaks.
etymology: Latin *sēmen* (‘seed’) scatters papers that sprout whole schools after.

memoryHook.text: "semin" (seed) = a seed idea that spawns a whole field of later work.

quizSentence: The discovery of penicillin was a seminal moment in the history of modern medicine.
alternateExampleSentence: His work is considered seminal in the field of modern classical music.

exampleSentence: The room was decorated with sensual fabrics and soft, warm candle light.
etymology: Latin *sēnsus* narrows to touch, taste, and appetite’s warmer claims.
quizSentence: The massage therapist used warm, scented oils to create a deeply sensual experience.
alternateExampleSentence: She enjoys the sensual pleasure of listening to live jazz music nightly.

exampleSentence: The sculptor creates sensuous forms that feel smooth to the human touch.
etymology: Latin *sēnsus* widens to aesthetic touch—velvet light, slow music, ripe color.
quizSentence: The sensuous curve of the sports car's body was designed to reduce aerodynamic drag.
alternateExampleSentence: The wine has a sensuous quality that lingers on the tongue long.

exampleSentence: Finding the rare book was an act of pure serendipity for him.
etymology: The tale of three princes of *Serendip* made accident look like wit.

memoryHook.text: Walpole coined it from "The Three Princes of Serendip" - lucky finds by accident.

quizSentence: Finding her lost ring in the garden years later was pure serendipity.
alternateExampleSentence: Their meeting was a matter of serendipity while traveling through the city.

exampleSentence: The lake was serene during the early hours of the morning.
etymology: Latin *serēnus* cleared storm from sky and temper alike.
quizSentence: The serene mountain lake was perfectly still, reflecting the pine trees like a mirror.
alternateExampleSentence: She finds a serene sense of peace while gardening in the yard.

exampleSentence: The servile intern would do anything to please the demanding office manager.
etymology: Latin *servilis* bent the knee low before whoever held the whip.

memoryHook.text: "serv" (serve/slave) = groveling, excessively submissive.

quizSentence: He hated how his servile assistant constantly bowed and agreed with his every word.
alternateExampleSentence: He resented the servile attitude of the people who worked for him.

exampleSentence: The sinuous path winds through the dense forest toward the mountain top.
etymology: Latin *sinuare* (‘bend in folds’) follows oxbow rivers and sinuous prose.

memoryHook.text: Sinus - a road winding like sinus passages; serpentine curves.

quizSentence: The sinuous mountain road twisted dangerously along the edge of the steep cliff.
alternateExampleSentence: She moved with a sinuous grace while performing on the large stage.

exampleSentence: The judge commended the defendant for maintaining his sobriety for two years.
etymology: Latin *sōbrius* began ‘not drunk’ and rose toward steady judgment.
quizSentence: The recovering alcoholic proudly celebrated his fifth year of continuous sobriety.
alternateExampleSentence: He values the clarity and sobriety of his mind during important work.

exampleSentence: The nurse was solicitous about the comfort of the patient in bed.
etymology: Latin *sollicitus* (‘thoroughly stirred’) worries over another’s welfare with care.

memoryHook.text: Sole - hovering over whether your shoe sole is hurting your foot.

quizSentence: The solicitous flight attendant constantly checked to make sure the nervous passenger was okay.
alternateExampleSentence: He is always solicitous regarding the needs of his aging family members.

exampleSentence: His solipsistic view made it hard for him to empathize with others.
etymology: Latin *solus* (‘alone’) with *ipse* (‘self’) traps theory in one private theater.

memoryHook.text: "solus" (alone) + "ipse" (self) = the world is only "me" in your mind.

quizSentence: Her solipsistic worldview made it impossible for her to empathize with anyone else's struggles.
alternateExampleSentence: The artist created a solipsistic work that focused only on his life.

exampleSentence: The salt is soluble in water and will disappear if you stir.
etymology: Latin *solvere* (‘loosen’) marks salts that surrender to water’s embrace.
quizSentence: Sugar is soluble and will dissolve quickly in a hot cup of tea.
alternateExampleSentence: The mystery is soluble if you examine all the available facts today.

etymology: Chemistry’s *solvent* is the liquid that loosens solute into solution.

senses.definition: A substance that can dissolve other substances
senses.exampleSentence: Water is sometimes called the universal solvent because almost all other substances can dissolve into it.
Upon receiving an unexpected check from her aunt, Annabelle found herself suddenly solvent.

memoryHook.text: Solve rent - cash flow strong enough to solve rent and pay debts.

exampleSentence: The business is finally solvent after a very long and difficult year.
quizSentence: The business remained financially solvent despite the global economic recession.
alternateExampleSentence: You need a strong solvent to remove the paint from the floor.

exampleSentence: The hot afternoon sun made the entire class feel deeply somnolent today.
etymology: Latin *somnus* (‘sleep’) thickened eyelids toward half-dream.

memoryHook.text: "somn" (sleep) = drowsy, ready to nod off.

quizSentence: The warm fire and soft music left the travelers feeling somnolent before bedtime.
alternateExampleSentence: He struggled to stay awake during the long and somnolent afternoon lecture.

exampleSentence: The movie was full of sophomoric humor that only the children enjoyed.
etymology: Greek *sophos* plus *mōros* (‘wise fool’) tags the sophomore’s swagger.

memoryHook.text: Sophomore - acting like a cocky, immature sophomore.

quizSentence: The frat boys thought their sophomoric prank with the shaving cream was hilarious.
alternateExampleSentence: He realized his early attempts at poetry were just sophomoric and shallow.

exampleSentence: The country is a sovereign state that makes its own national laws.
etymology: Old French *sovereign* crowned Latin *superānus* (‘highest’) with full command.
quizSentence: The sovereign nation refused to let any foreign military powers cross its borders.
alternateExampleSentence: Each person is a sovereign individual responsible for their own life choices.

exampleSentence: The tech startup relies on speculative technology that has not been proven.
etymology: Latin *speculārī* (‘look out’) let traders guess at cargoes still below the horizon.
quizSentence: His theory about ancient aliens building the pyramids is entirely speculative and unproven.
alternateExampleSentence: Investing your life savings in unproven cryptocurrency remains a deeply speculative financial strategy.

exampleSentence: The witness provided a spurious account of what happened at the scene.
etymology: Latin *spurius* marked illegitimate birth; English marks illegitimate claims.

memoryHook.text: Furious - the judge furious at evidence that is clearly fake.

quizSentence: The internet is full of spurious quotes falsely attributed to famous historical figures.
alternateExampleSentence: The article was based on spurious data that fooled the general public.

exampleSentence: The small town began to stagnate as the main factory closed down.
etymology: Latin *stagnum* (‘standing water’) pictures pools that cease to flow.
quizSentence: Without any new ideas or investments, the small town's economy began to stagnate.
alternateExampleSentence: Water will stagnate if it does not flow through the garden pond.

exampleSentence: The company has a staid reputation that appeals to very conservative investors.
etymology: Latin *stāre* in *staid* marks characters that stand steady without theatrics.

memoryHook.text: Stayed - so staid they stayed home; serious, steady, unflashy.

quizSentence: The staid bank manager wore the exact same gray suit and tie every single day.
alternateExampleSentence: She wore a staid suit to the formal meeting with the executives.

exampleSentence: He is too stingy to pay for a better meal for family.
etymology: Old English *stingan* (‘sting’) nicked coins as if each hurt.
quizSentence: The stingy boss refused to pay for coffee in the breakroom for his employees.
alternateExampleSentence: The company is stingy with its benefits and pays the lowest wages.

exampleSentence: She remained stoic while hearing the difficult news about her sick father.
etymology: Greek *Stōikos* named porch philosophers who practiced pain’s long patience.

memoryHook.text: From the Stoa Poikile porch where Zeno taught - endure pain without theatrics.

quizSentence: The stoic athlete showed no emotion despite the painful injury during the match.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a stoic person who rarely shows any visible emotional reaction.

exampleSentence: The stolid guard stood at his post without moving for three hours.
etymology: Latin *stolidus* (‘dull’) labels minds that seldom quicken to surprise.

memoryHook.text: Solid - emotionally solid like a brick wall; unmovable.

quizSentence: The stolid bouncer stood at the club entrance with a blank, unreadable expression.
alternateExampleSentence: He gave a stolid response that failed to excite the bored audience.

exampleSentence: The hikers completed a strenuous climb to the top of the mountain.
etymology: Latin *strenuus* (‘brisk, hardy’) praises strain willingly borne.
quizSentence: Chopping firewood all afternoon is strenuous physical labor.
alternateExampleSentence: He requires a strenuous workout to prepare for the upcoming marathon race.

exampleSentence: The strident voice of the critic could be heard across the room.
etymology: Latin *stridēre* (‘creak, shriek’) names noise that scrapes the ear.

memoryHook.text: Trident - noise as harsh as a trident slammed into stone.

quizSentence: A strident fire alarm pierced the quiet atmosphere of the library reading room.
alternateExampleSentence: She made a strident demand for the company to change its policies.

exampleSentence: The amazing magic trick served to stupefy everyone in the theater audience.
etymology: Latin *stupēre* (‘be stunned’) names the club blow to thought.

memoryHook.text: Stupid - shocked stupid; brain freezes for a moment.

quizSentence: The magician's ability to levitate a car managed to stupefy the audience.
alternateExampleSentence: The complexity of the math problem was enough to stupefy the students.

exampleSentence: The ruthless emperor sought to mercilessly subjugate all rebellious tribes within the territory.
etymology: Latin *iugum* (‘yoke’) reappears in *subiugāre*, forcing necks under rule.

memoryHook.text: "sub" (under) + "jugum" (yoke) = bring a people under your yoke.

quizSentence: The invading empire sought to subjugate the local tribes and force them into slavery.
alternateExampleSentence: They fought to subjugate their fears and finish the dangerous mission tonight.

exampleSentence: The view from the top of the peak was sublime today.
etymology: Latin *sublimis* (‘raised aloft’) lifts peak, poetry, or terror toward sky.
quizSentence: Tasting a perfectly cooked truffle risotto is a sublime culinary experience.
alternateExampleSentence: She wrote a sublime piece of music that touched every single listener.

exampleSentence: The dog is submissive and always listens to his owner's commands daily.
etymology: Latin *submittere* (‘place under’) bends will beneath another’s hand.
quizSentence: The submissive dog rolled onto its back to show surrender to the larger alpha wolf.
alternateExampleSentence: He hates the submissive behavior of people who never speak their minds.

etymology: Latin subsequens, from sub- ('after') + sequi ('to follow')
exampleSentence: The storm arrived, and subsequently the power went out for six hours.

memoryHook.text: "Sub-" (after) + "sequ-" (follow) = what follows later in sequence.

quizSentence: The law passed in May and subsequently changed hiring practices across the state.
alternateExampleSentence: She studied law and subsequently became a judge in the state court.

etymology: Latin substantiare, from substantia ('substance, essence')
exampleSentence: The detective used evidence to substantiate the claim that he was home.

memoryHook.text: Substance = solid proof — substantiate gives a claim substance.

quizSentence: Charts alone cannot substantiate causation without a plausible mechanism.
alternateExampleSentence: The company could not substantiate the promises made in the new advertisement.

exampleSentence: The professor provided a succinct explanation of the theory in ten minutes.
etymology: Latin *succingere* (‘gird from below’) tucked arguments tight like a belt.

memoryHook.text: Sucked in - speech with extra words sucked in; tight and brief.

quizSentence: The instructions on the fire extinguisher are appropriately brief and succinct.
alternateExampleSentence: Please keep your final report succinct and focus on the main results.

exampleSentence: The essay contained many superfluous words that made it hard to read.
etymology: Latin *superfluus* (‘overflowing’) spills more than use can drink.

memoryHook.text: "super" (over) + "flu" (flow) = extra detail flowing over the brim.

quizSentence: The author edited out three pages of superfluous description to quicken the pacing.
alternateExampleSentence: He cut out the superfluous details to make the story much clearer.

exampleSentence: A surfeit of food at the party led to much wasted dinner.
etymology: Old French *surfet* filled the stomach until disgust arrived.

memoryHook.text: Surf it - so many waves you cannot surf them all; excess.

quizSentence: A surfeit of apples caused the orchard to drop its prices dramatically.
alternateExampleSentence: The market has a surfeit of goods that nobody wants to buy.

exampleSentence: Observers surmise that recent political tension stems from underlying economic instability within cities.
etymology: Old French *surmise* overtook Latin *supermittere* for notions lightly launched.

memoryHook.text: Summarize - guess the whole plot from scraps, like forced summarizing.

quizSentence: Historians surmise that the lost civilization collapsed due to a severe prolonged drought.
alternateExampleSentence: She began to surmise that her friends were planning a surprise party.

exampleSentence: The spy made a surreptitious attempt to photograph the secret government documents.
etymology: Latin *subripere* (‘snatch secretly’) hid hands inside sleeves.

memoryHook.text: Syrup - pouring extra syrup when nobody is watching.

quizSentence: She took a surreptitious peek at her phone under the desk during the exam.
alternateExampleSentence: He took a surreptitious glance at his watch during the long meeting.

exampleSentence: She acted as a surrogate mother for the couple who wanted children.
etymology: Latin *surrogātus* (‘asked in place of’) names proxies who wear another’s chair.
quizSentence: Since they could not conceive, the couple decided to hire a surrogate mother.
alternateExampleSentence: The robot acts as a surrogate for a human in dangerous tasks.

exampleSentence: The manager is surrounded by a sycophant group that agrees with everything.
etymology: Greek *sūkon* (‘fig’) plus *phainein* whispered informers; English keeps oily praise.

memoryHook.text: Psyche + fawn - flatters power to win favor; a professional bootlicker.

quizSentence: The king's court was filled with groveling sycophants desperate for royal favor.
alternateExampleSentence: He hates being a sycophant and prefers to speak his own mind.

etymology: Greek syntithenai, from syn- ('together') + tithenai ('to place')
exampleSentence: The scientist works to synthesize new chemicals for the medical research team.

memoryHook.text: "Syn-" (together) + "thesis" (placing) = place ideas together into one whole.

quizSentence: Strong conclusions synthesize conflicting studies instead of citing only one.
alternateExampleSentence: She managed to synthesize many different ideas into one very clear theory.

exampleSentence: There was a tacit agreement that the project would be finished soon.
etymology: Latin *tacitus* (‘silent’) binds parties without ink.

memoryHook.text: Take it - a silent deal where everyone takes it and says nothing.

quizSentence: A tacit agreement between the two factions effectively halted the long-standing border conflict.
alternateExampleSentence: The partners maintained a tacit understanding that financial details would remain confidential.

exampleSentence: The taciturn man spoke very little during the long family dinner party.
etymology: Latin *tacitus* with *-urnus* marks habitually quiet temperaments.

memoryHook.text: "tacit" (silent) = habitually quiet; not chatty.

quizSentence: The taciturn witness provided only brief answers during the intense cross-examination period.
alternateExampleSentence: She is taciturn by nature and prefers to listen rather than talk.

exampleSentence: The speaker made a tangential comment that distracted from the main point.
etymology: Latin *tangere* (‘touch’) touches a subject only at odd edges.

memoryHook.text: Tangent - barely grazing the topic; flies off on a tangent.

quizSentence: The professor went off on a tangential rant about his cats instead of teaching calculus.
alternateExampleSentence: His research has a tangential connection to the field of modern physics.

exampleSentence: Ignoring the safety rules is tantamount to inviting a disaster at work.

memoryHook.text: Total amount - equivalent in seriousness or value to another total.

quizSentence: Leaving a dog locked in a hot car is tantamount to animal cruelty.

exampleSentence: Sorting historical archives is a tedious task requiring patience and meticulous focus.
etymology: Latin *taedium* bored the spirit until clocks crawled.
quizSentence: Peeling fifty pounds of potatoes by hand is a tedious chore.

exampleSentence: He had the temerity to ask for a raise after failing project.

quizSentence: She had the temerity to demand a raise after losing the firm's biggest client.
alternateExampleSentence: She showed the temerity to challenge the boss during the public meeting.

exampleSentence: The leader preached temperance and urged the crowd to avoid drinking alcohol.

alternateExampleSentence: He practices temperance in all things to maintain his excellent physical health.

exampleSentence: His argument was tenable, but he failed to convince the skeptical jury.

memoryHook.text: "tenere" (hold) = an argument you can hold up under scrutiny.

exampleSentence: The company's financial future is tenuous after the sudden market crash today.

memoryHook.text: Ten percent grip - holding the rope by the thinnest tenth; shaky.

quizSentence: The bridge relied on a tenuous system of rusting cables that looked near collapse.

exampleSentence: The telescope is used to observe terrestrial events from a high mountain.

alternateExampleSentence: Scientists are looking for signs of life on other non terrestrial planets.

exampleSentence: The timorous child hid behind his mother when the loud dog barked.
etymology: Latin *timor* (‘fear’) shrank spirits from risks others barely notice.

memoryHook.text: Tremble - timid and trembling like a frightened mouse.

quizSentence: The timorous kitten hid under the couch whenever the doorbell rang.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a timorous person who avoids any form of public speaking.

exampleSentence: The customer went on a long tirade about the poor restaurant service.
etymology: French *tirade* named volleys of speech shot in prolonged bursts.

memoryHook.text: Tired - a long angry speech that leaves everyone tired.

quizSentence: The angry chef launched into a foul-mouthed tirade when the waiter dropped the plates.
alternateExampleSentence: She ended the tirade and walked out of the room in anger.

exampleSentence: He is a toady who constantly compliments the boss to get ahead.

quizSentence: The CEO's little toady follows him around everywhere, carrying his briefcase and fetching coffee.
alternateExampleSentence: She refuses to be a toady just to secure a better position.

exampleSentence: He carried a heavy tome filled with records of the royal family.

memoryHook.text: Tomb - a huge tome as heavy as a tombstone.

quizSentence: The library holds a leather-bound tome detailing the town's history.
alternateExampleSentence: The researcher studied every single page of the ancient history tome today.

exampleSentence: The cold weather made the small lizard feel torpid and very slow.

memoryHook.text: Torpedo - floating sluggish like a torpedo before launch.

quizSentence: During hibernation, the torpid bear's heart rate drops to just a few beats per minute.
alternateExampleSentence: He felt torpid after the large meal and took a long nap.

exampleSentence: The torrid sun made it hard to work in the field today.
etymology: Latin *torrēre* (‘parch’) scorched sand, love, and rhetoric with heat.

memoryHook.text: Tear it - passion so hot it tears at restraint; scorching heat.

quizSentence: The couple embarked on a torrid summer romance that burned out by September.
alternateExampleSentence: They shared a torrid romance that eventually ended after only one year.

exampleSentence: The drive up the mountain was tortuous and required much careful focus.
etymology: Latin *torquēre* (‘twist’) bent roads, plots, and spines into knots.

memoryHook.text: Torture - path so twisting it feels like torture to navigate.

quizSentence: The tortuous maze of medieval streets makes it easy to get lost downtown.
alternateExampleSentence: He gave a tortuous explanation that confused everyone in the room today.

exampleSentence: The horse is tractable and easy to ride for the young child.
etymology: Latin *tractāre* (‘handle’) names beings that yield to the rein.

memoryHook.text: "tract" (lead/manage) = easy to lead; docile and manageable.

quizSentence: The new substitute teacher found the class to be surprisingly quiet and tractable.
alternateExampleSentence: She prefers working with tractable people who are open to new ideas.

exampleSentence: The garden is a tranquil place to escape the noise of city.
etymology: Latin *trānquillus* stilled water until reflections held steady.

alternateExampleSentence: He enjoys the tranquil atmosphere of the woods during the early morning.

exampleSentence: It is wrong to transgress the boundaries established by the local laws.
etymology: Latin *trānsgredī* (‘step across’) crossed lines etched by rule or gods.

memoryHook.text: "trans" (across) + "gress" (step) = step across a moral line you should not.

quizSentence: Those who transgress the sacred laws of the temple will be banished forever.
alternateExampleSentence: He was punished because he decided to transgress the rules of class.

exampleSentence: The beauty of the sunset is transient and fades in few minutes.
etymology: Latin *trānsīre* (‘go across’) marks guests who never unpack.

memoryHook.text: "trans" (across) + "ire" (go) = passing through; not staying long.

quizSentence: A hotel near the airport primarily caters to a transient population of travelers.
alternateExampleSentence: He has a transient job that requires him to travel every month.

exampleSentence: The alchemist tried to transmute lead into gold using a magic ritual.
etymology: Latin *trānsmūtāre* shifted nature the way heat shifts ore.

memoryHook.text: "trans" (across) + "mut" (change) = change something into another substance.

quizSentence: The villain used a magical spell to transmute the heroic prince into a tiny frog.
alternateExampleSentence: She hopes to transmute her pain into a beautiful and moving song.

exampleSentence: The trial was a travesty of justice that shocked the entire community.
etymology: French *travestir* (‘disguise’) dressed solemn things in clown clothes.

memoryHook.text: Tragedy - a mockery of justice so bad it is a tragedy.

quizSentence: Allowing the guilty murderer to walk free on a technicality is a travesty of justice.
alternateExampleSentence: The play was a travesty and made a mockery of the history.

exampleSentence: Her tremulous voice revealed how nervous she felt before the big speech.
etymology: Latin *tremere* (‘tremble’) shook voice, hand, or leaf alike.

memoryHook.text: Tremble - voice trembling from fear.

quizSentence: She read the eulogy in a tremulous voice, struggling to hold back her tears.
alternateExampleSentence: He made a tremulous attempt to stand after falling on the ice.

exampleSentence: The author provided a trenchant analysis of the current national economic crisis.
etymology: Old French *trenchier* (‘cut’) carved arguments to the bone.

memoryHook.text: Trench - criticism that cuts a deep trench.

quizSentence: The political commentator is known for his trenchant and biting analysis of current events.
alternateExampleSentence: Her trenchant wit made her a very popular guest on the show.

exampleSentence: He felt great trepidation before stepping onto the stage for the play.
etymology: Latin *trepidus* (‘trembling’) edged every step with imagined pits.

memoryHook.text: Trap - dread that you are walking into a trap.

quizSentence: Candidates entered the interview room with trepidation, fearing the rigorous questioning ahead.
alternateExampleSentence: The hikers walked with trepidation through the dark and quiet mountain pass.

exampleSentence: The screenplay was filled with trite dialogue and a very boring plot.
etymology: Latin *trītus* (‘worn by rubbing’) names phrases polished smooth by reuse.

memoryHook.text: Tripe - an idea as tired as cheap tripe.

quizSentence: The commencement speech offered trite advice about following dreams and reaching higher.
alternateExampleSentence: He hated the trite advice his friends gave about his personal life.

exampleSentence: The truculent teenager started a fight with his neighbor over the fence.
etymology: Latin *trux* (‘fierce, savage’) sharpened animals—and tempers.

memoryHook.text: Truck - aggressive like a truck ready to run you down.

quizSentence: The truculent drunk shoved the bartender and challenged everyone in the pub to a fight.
alternateExampleSentence: She displayed a truculent attitude during the meeting with the human resources.

exampleSentence: The manager had to truncate the meeting because he was running late.
etymology: Latin *truncus* (‘maimed, trunk’) lopped tails from tales and trees.

memoryHook.text: Trunk - chop at the trunk to shorten the tree.

quizSentence: The editor decided to truncate the final chapter to meet publication space requirements.
alternateExampleSentence: You should truncate the long video to make it fit the screen.

exampleSentence: The river became turgid after the heavy rains flooded the local valley.
etymology: Latin *turgidus* swelled veins, clouds, and prose beyond their seams.

memoryHook.text: Turgid = swollen - bloated language or tissue, overstuffed and heavy (not muddy/turbid).

quizSentence: Readers struggled to parse the turgid prose found in the lengthy academic dissertation.
alternateExampleSentence: The boring professor delivered a turgid lecture filled with unnecessary academic jargon.

exampleSentence: The judge condemned the criminal for his acts of moral turpitude today.
etymology: Latin *turpis* (‘shameful’) sank conduct beyond easy washing.

memoryHook.text: Moral filth - conduct courts label crimes of moral turpitude; deep baseness.

quizSentence: The corrupt judge was removed from the bench for acts of extreme moral turpitude.
alternateExampleSentence: The scandal exposed the deep turpitude existing within the corporate board room.

exampleSentence: Plastic waste has become ubiquitous, polluting even the most remote oceanic environments today.
etymology: Latin *ubīque* (‘anywhere’) multiplied a thing until no corner lacks it.

memoryHook.text: "ubique" (everywhere) = showing up everywhere at once.

quizSentence: Coffee shops have become practically ubiquitous on every street corner in the city.
alternateExampleSentence: The coffee shop chain is ubiquitous and found in every large city.

exampleSentence: He took umbrage at the rude comment regarding his professional work ethics.
etymology: Latin *umbra* (‘shade’) named shadows of irritation crossing the face.

memoryHook.text: "umbr" (shadow) = take offense as if a disrespectful shadow fell on you.

quizSentence: Citizens took umbrage at the sudden proposal to increase local property tax rates.
alternateExampleSentence: She felt umbrage when the neighbor insulted her garden and her plants.

exampleSentence: He has an uncanny ability to predict what people will say next.
etymology: Scots *uncanny* once meant ‘malicious’; modern use stresses the odd beyond reason.
quizSentence: The wax figure at the museum bore an uncanny resemblance to the real president.
alternateExampleSentence: The resemblance between the two brothers is truly uncanny to all observers.

exampleSentence: The unctuous lobbyist used manipulative charm to influence the senators during the vote.
etymology: Latin *unguere* (‘anoint’) greased flattery until it slid too smooth.

memoryHook.text: Oil-on-tone - flattery so smooth it feels greasy; slick, insincere charm.

quizSentence: The unctuous car salesman used excessive flattery to try and secure a quick commission.
alternateExampleSentence: She disliked the unctuous flattery of the employees seeking a quick promotion.

etymology: From under + mine — to dig beneath foundations
exampleSentence: The constant criticism will eventually undermine her confidence in her creative skills.

memoryHook.text: To undermine is to dig under a wall until it collapses.

quizSentence: Repeated delays undermined public trust in the agency's forecasts.
alternateExampleSentence: He tried to undermine the project by spreading rumors about the team.

exampleSentence: The fields of wheat undulate in the wind like a golden ocean.
etymology: Latin *undula* (‘little wave’) traced hills, snakes, and cloth in motion.

memoryHook.text: Undulate - waves moving under the surface in smooth rolls.

quizSentence: The belly dancer's hips began to smoothly undulate to the rhythm of the drums.
alternateExampleSentence: The path continues to undulate through the hills on the mountain range.

exampleSentence: The teacher had to upbraid the student for talking during the exam.
etymology: Old English *ūp* plus *breġdan* ‘plucked threads upward’ into sharp reproof.

memoryHook.text: Braid - scolding someone for ruining your careful braid.

quizSentence: The judge did not hesitate to upbraid the attorney for inappropriate courtroom conduct.
alternateExampleSentence: He did not want to upbraid his friend for the minor mistake.

exampleSentence: The ambitious general tried to usurp the power of the elected president.
etymology: Latin *ūrpare* meant ‘use’; *usurpāre* took rights another should hold.

memoryHook.text: Use up - seize power to use up what is left of the kingdom.

quizSentence: The ambitious duke plotted to assassinate his brother and usurp the royal throne.
alternateExampleSentence: The rival company attempted to usurp the market share of the leader.

exampleSentence: The building has a utilitarian design that focuses on function over beauty.
etymology: Latin *ūtilis* weighed choices by outcomes people can actually use.
quizSentence: The concrete bunker was a purely utilitarian structure designed for safety, not beauty.
alternateExampleSentence: She prefers a utilitarian approach to solving the complex problems at work.

exampleSentence: He dreams of a utopia where everyone lives in peace and harmony.
etymology: Greek *ou* (‘not’) plus *topos* (‘place’) named good islands on no real map.

memoryHook.text: More coined "Utopia" from ou + topos ("no place") - a perfect society that cannot exist.

quizSentence: The sci-fi novel depicted a flawless, peaceful utopia where war and poverty had been eradicated.
alternateExampleSentence: The philosopher described a utopia that could never exist in real life.

exampleSentence: She continues to vacillate between choosing the job in London or Paris.
etymology: Latin *vacillāre* (‘sway’) kept judgment rocking between two stools.

memoryHook.text: Vase - wavering forever between two vases you cannot choose.

quizSentence: The council members continue to vacillate regarding the construction of the new bridge.
alternateExampleSentence: Do not vacillate when you need to make a fast, important decision.

definition: Showing a total lack of intelligence
exampleSentence: The politician gave a vacuous speech that contained no real policy ideas.
etymology: Latin *vacuus* hollowed heads until echo answered inside.

memoryHook.text: "vac" (empty) = empty behind the eyes; mindless.

quizSentence: The reality television star gave a vacuous interview filled with giggles and empty stares.
alternateExampleSentence: He stared with a vacuous expression at the confusing math problem today.

exampleSentence: The data will validate the theory he proposed earlier this last month.
etymology: Medieval Latin *validāre* strengthened claims until they stand trial.
quizSentence: You must stamp your parking ticket at the desk to validate it for free exit.
alternateExampleSentence: She needs to validate her feelings before she can move on today.

exampleSentence: The party was vapid and lacked any interesting music or good conversation.
etymology: Latin *vapidus* (‘flat-tasting’) described wine—and wit—gone spiritless.

memoryHook.text: Vapor - remark so dull it evaporates like vapor.

quizSentence: The pop song featured a catchy beat but vapid and meaningless lyrics.
alternateExampleSentence: He wrote a vapid article that failed to interest the bored readers.

exampleSentence: The garden features variegated leaves that show many shades of bright green.
etymology: Latin *varius* (‘varied’) with *-āre* lent English leaves and fabrics many streaked tones.

memoryHook.text: "varius" (various) = patched with varied colors or tones.

quizSentence: The gardener planted a beautiful bed of variegated hostas with green and white striped leaves.
alternateExampleSentence: The painting is a variegated work of art with many different colors.

exampleSentence: The candidate vehemently denied the claims made by his political rival today.
etymology: Latin *vehēment-* (‘carrying along’) drove adverbs toward violent conviction.

memoryHook.text: Vehicle - yelling with full heat at the car that cut you off.

quizSentence: The suspect vehemently denied having any involvement in the bank robbery.
alternateExampleSentence: She vehemently argued against the plan to build the new highway nearby.

exampleSentence: He used a wood veneer to give the cheap desk a finish.
etymology: German *Furnier* and French *fournir* gave English a skin of fine wood.
quizSentence: Beneath his polite veneer, he was actually a ruthless and calculating businessman.
alternateExampleSentence: The polite smile was just a veneer for his deep hidden anger.

exampleSentence: The venerable professor has taught at the university for fifty long years.
etymology: Latin *venerābilis* applied to gray wisdom the crowd steps back to honor.

memoryHook.text: "venerari" (respect) = worthy of reverence for age, dignity, or achievement.

quizSentence: The venerable oak tree had shaded the town square for over two centuries.
alternateExampleSentence: The town is home to many venerable buildings from the colonial era.

exampleSentence: The citizens gather annually to venerate the memory of the national hero.
etymology: Latin *vēnerārī* approached shrines with lowered eyes and measured speech.

memoryHook.text: Revere-plus - honor deeply as if before something sacred (same family as reverent).

quizSentence: Many ancient cultures used to venerate the sun as a powerful and life-giving deity.
alternateExampleSentence: Many cultures venerate their ancestors through special rituals and yearly public festivals.

exampleSentence: The veracity of the report was questioned by many of the experts.
etymology: Latin *vērācitās* weighed words on scales that refuse polite weighting.

memoryHook.text: "ver" (truth) = truthfulness you can stake a claim on.

quizSentence: The journalist checked three different sources to confirm the veracity of the whistleblower's claims.
alternateExampleSentence: You should check the veracity of the facts before you post online.

definition: Expressed in far too many words
exampleSentence: The report was too verbose and should have been much shorter today.
etymology: Latin *verbōsus* packed sentences with more sacks than cargo needs.

memoryHook.text: "verb" (word) = padded with too many words; talky and windy.

quizSentence: The verbose professor took twenty minutes to answer a simple yes-or-no question.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a verbose speaker who often loses the interest of listeners.

exampleSentence: The hikers walked through the verdant forest during the early summer morning.
etymology: Old French *vert* from Latin *viridis* painted hills newly waked.

memoryHook.text: "virid" (green) = green with lush growth.

quizSentence: After the spring rains, the rolling hills transformed into a lush, verdant paradise.
alternateExampleSentence: The hills were verdant after the heavy rains fell on the region.

exampleSentence: There is not a single vestige of the ancient city left today.
etymology: Latin *vestīgium* (‘footstep’) marks where something once stood—barely.

memoryHook.text: Vest - only a ripped old vest remains as a trace of the wreck.

quizSentence: The crumbling stone chimney is the last remaining vestige of the old pioneer cabin.
alternateExampleSentence: The ruin is a vestige of a time before the modern era.

exampleSentence: The enigmatic behavior of the witness continued to vex investigators throughout the trial.
etymology: Latin *vexāre* (‘shake, jostle’) shook temper until knots formed.

memoryHook.text: Hex - annoy someone as if you cast a petty hex on them.

quizSentence: Complex bureaucratic regulations continue to vex entrepreneurs attempting to launch new local businesses.
alternateExampleSentence: It does not help to vex yourself over things you cannot change.

exampleSentence: He lived a vicarious life through the adventures of his favorite characters.
etymology: Latin *vicārius* (‘substitute’) felt another’s pain—or victory—from afar.

memoryHook.text: Borrowed thrill - living someone else's excitement secondhand.

quizSentence: The paralyzed former athlete experienced a vicarious thrill watching his son win the race.
alternateExampleSentence: She gained a vicarious thrill from hearing about the daring mountain climb.

exampleSentence: He learned to accept every vicissitude of life with a calm attitude.
etymology: Latin *vicis* (‘change, turn’) counted fate’s revolving chair.
quizSentence: Farmers must constantly adapt to the unpredictable vicissitude of the seasonal weather.
alternateExampleSentence: The project suffered a vicissitude that forced the team to restart today.

exampleSentence: The security team must remain vigilant to protect the site from theft.
etymology: Latin *vigil* (‘awake’) kept lamps trimmed while others slept.
quizSentence: The vigilant night watchman patrolled the factory perimeter with a bright flashlight.
alternateExampleSentence: She is a vigilant parent who watches over her children at play.

exampleSentence: The media campaign worked to vilify the candidate before the election started.
etymology: Latin *vilis* (‘cheap, base’) plus *-fy* dragged names through cheap mud.

memoryHook.text: "vilis" (vile) + "-fy" (make) = talk someone into looking vile.

quizSentence: The opposing campaign ran nasty television ads trying to vilify the incumbent senator.
alternateExampleSentence: It is wrong to vilify someone because they hold a different opinion.

exampleSentence: The new evidence will vindicate him of all the charges against him.
etymology: Latin *vindex* (‘avenger, defender’) cleared honor with evidence’s blade.

memoryHook.text: Vindicare-show the accusation was false; clear a ruined name.

quizSentence: The newly discovered fingerprint evidence will finally vindicate the wrongfully accused suspect.
alternateExampleSentence: She worked hard to vindicate her reputation after the false news reports.

exampleSentence: He is a vindictive person who holds grudges against his old friends.
etymology: Latin *vindicta* (‘vengeance’) nursed grudges past prudence.

memoryHook.text: Victim - wanting the other side to become your suffering victim.

quizSentence: The vindictive ex-wife keyed his car and poured sugar in the gas tank.
alternateExampleSentence: The vindictive ex-employee tried to damage the company files after being fired.

exampleSentence: He is a piano virtuoso who performs at the best global theaters.
etymology: Italian *virtuoso* praised skill so polished it looks like virtue.
quizSentence: The young violin virtuoso sold out concert halls around the world before she turned twenty.
alternateExampleSentence: She played the violin with the skill of a true musical virtuoso.

exampleSentence: The viscous oil flowed slowly down the side of the metal drum.
etymology: Latin *viscum* (*birdlime* sticky) kin to *viscus* lent gluey drag to liquids.

memoryHook.text: Viscosity - thick, sticky, slow to pour.

quizSentence: Cold honey is viscous and pours very slowly from the plastic bear bottle.
alternateExampleSentence: He used a viscous glue to hold the heavy pieces together today.

exampleSentence: The critic wrote a vitriolic review that attacked the personal life director.
etymology: Latin *vitrium* (‘glass’) tangled with strong acids that etch metal and tone.

memoryHook.text: Vitriol - words as burning as strong acid.

quizSentence: The internet comments section quickly devolved into a mess of vitriolic insults and threats.
alternateExampleSentence: She received vitriolic messages from angry people on her public social media.

exampleSentence: The angry boss began to vituperate his employees for the small mistake.
etymology: Latin *vituperāre* (‘blame’) hurled fault-finding like clods.

memoryHook.text: Viper - hissing insults with viper venom.

quizSentence: The furious customer began to vituperate the poor cashier over an expired coupon.
alternateExampleSentence: It is never helpful to vituperate others even when they are wrong.

exampleSentence: She is a vivacious person who brings energy to every social gathering.
etymology: Latin *vīvere* (‘live’) stepped dances with quick feet and quicker wit.

memoryHook.text: "viv" (life) = lively, bubbling with life.

quizSentence: The vivacious cheerleader brought incredible energy and spirit to the gloomy locker room.
alternateExampleSentence: The vivacious puppy played in the yard until he fell fast asleep.

exampleSentence: He finally found his true vocation when he started working in medicine.
etymology: Latin *vocātiō* (‘a calling’) named work that summons like a horn.

memoryHook.text: "voc" (call) = a career that feels like a calling.

quizSentence: Nursing is not merely a job to her; she treats it as a lifelong vocation.
alternateExampleSentence: Teaching is a noble vocation that requires much patience and deep passion.

exampleSentence: The vociferous crowd cheered as the team scored the final winning goal.
etymology: Latin *vōx* plus *ferre* (‘carry’) bore noise to the farthest benches.

memoryHook.text: "voc" (voice) + "ferre" (carry) = carrying a loud voice into the room.

quizSentence: The vociferous crowd outside the courthouse demanded a harsh sentence for the criminal.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a vociferous critic of the new government tax reform plan.

exampleSentence: Do not wallow in your sadness after a difficult day at work.
etymology: Old Germanic roots of *wallow* rolled beasts—and souls—in mud or pity.
quizSentence: Pigs wallow in cool mud to protect their sensitive skin from the hot sun.
alternateExampleSentence: The pigs like to wallow in the mud on the hot day.

exampleSentence: The influence of the old king began to wane after the war.
etymology: Old English *wanian* thinned the moon—and hope—toward dark.

memoryHook.text: Wayne - Wayne Enterprises lights dimming as Bruce fades after a brutal night.

quizSentence: The campfire's bright glow began to wane as the logs burned to embers.
alternateExampleSentence: The moon will wane until it becomes a tiny sliver of light.

exampleSentence: The criminal was arrested for the wanton destruction of public city property.
etymology: Middle English *wantowen* (‘undisciplined’) slipped into moral chaos.

memoryHook.text: Wonton - wanton waste of a whole bowl of wonton soup on purpose; reckless malice.

quizSentence: The vandals engaged in wanton destruction, smashing windows and overturning cars for no reason.
alternateExampleSentence: She showed a wanton disregard for the safety of her fellow passengers.

exampleSentence: He created a whimsical painting filled with bright colors and strange shapes.
etymology: From *whim*, English *whimsical* lets fancy tilt the logical chessboard.
quizSentence: The children's book features a whimsical world filled with talking teacups and flying pigs.
alternateExampleSentence: She has a whimsical sense of humor that makes everyone laugh often.

exampleSentence: The wily fox escaped the hunter by hiding in the dark woods.
etymology: Old Norse *vīgr* (‘battle-wise’) shaded into sly cleverness.

memoryHook.text: Wile E. - cunning traps and scams like the cartoon coyote.

quizSentence: The wily coyote managed to snatch a chicken right out from under the farmer's nose.
alternateExampleSentence: He is a wily negotiator who always finds a way to win.

definition: Attractive in a charmingly fresh way
exampleSentence: The winsome child charmed everyone at the party with her sweet smile.
etymology: Old English *wynsum* mingled joy (*wyn*) with inviting faces.

memoryHook.text: Win some - charm that wins some hearts instantly.

quizSentence: The child's winsome smile convinced her grandfather to buy her an ice cream.
alternateExampleSentence: He has a winsome personality that makes it easy to make friends.

exampleSentence: She gave a wistful look at the old home she once lived.
etymology: From an older sense tied to *wist* (‘intent gaze’), English *wistful* softened into gentle longing.

memoryHook.text: Wishful - nostalgia colored by sad wishful longing.

quizSentence: He cast a wistful glance at his old high school while leaving his hometown.
alternateExampleSentence: He felt wistful when he heard the music from his high school.

exampleSentence: The old farmer had a wizened face from years in the sun.
etymology: Older *wizened* tied to wise suggests skin drawn tight by many winters.

memoryHook.text: Wizard - face wizened like a storybook wizard: shriveled and lined.

quizSentence: The wizened old fortune teller stared deeply into her crystal ball with tired, cloudy eyes.
alternateExampleSentence: The wizened branches of the ancient oak swayed in the cold autumn wind.

exampleSentence: He felt the wrath of the manager for missing the important deadline.
etymology: Old English *wrǣththu* burned hot as twisted bands of flame.
quizSentence: The ancient Greeks believed that a violent thunderstorm was a sign of Zeus's terrible wrath.
alternateExampleSentence: The storm unleashed its wrath upon the small coastal town last night.

exampleSentence: The farmer used a wooden yoke to lead the oxen across field.
etymology: Old English *geoc* named wood that links two beasts—and metaphoric burdens.
quizSentence: The farmer securely fastened the heavy wooden yoke around the necks of the two oxen.
alternateExampleSentence: They fought to break the yoke of the king and find freedom.

exampleSentence: He worked with zealous passion to finish the project before the deadline.
etymology: Greek *zēlos* (‘ardor, rivalry’) fired reformers and fans alike.

memoryHook.text: Jealous - fire of a jealous lover turned toward a cause; fiercely devoted.

quizSentence: The zealous missionary traveled to dangerous remote jungles to spread his religious beliefs.
alternateExampleSentence: She showed zealous effort in her pursuit of the championship gold medal.

exampleSentence: The band reached the zenith of its popularity during the late nineties.
etymology: Medieval Latin *cenit* reshaped Arabic *samt* (‘path, direction’) into the point directly overhead.

memoryHook.text: Zenith tip - apex of the arc (sun at noon); your highest point, not the bottom below.

quizSentence: At the zenith of his career, the actor won three Academy Awards in one night.
alternateExampleSentence: The sun was at its zenith during the heat of the afternoon.

exampleSentence: A light zephyr blew through the garden during the warm summer evening.
etymology: Greek *Zephyros* named the west wind that warms shores toward spring.

memoryHook.text: Zero fear - breeze so gentle it gives you zero fear of a storm.

quizSentence: A cool, refreshing zephyr blew gently off the ocean, providing relief from the blazing sun.
alternateExampleSentence: A gentle zephyr cooled the sweltering fields, providing relief to the weary laborers.
